FROM mapping_entry
WHERE active_to IS NULL OR active_to > CURRENT_DATE;

title: Model Consumption Policy and Customer Mapping Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines Redwood's Model Consumption Policy and a repeatable customer mapping process that converts product-level fidelity and consumption choices into billing, SLOs, and rollout plans. It is intended for product managers, solutions engineers, pricing, and account teams executing migrations or packaging changes that affect per-route fidelity, token accounting, and unit economics.

Goals:
- Create a single, auditable mapping from customer consumption intent -> fidelity tier -> pricing and SLO bundle.
- Reduce customer surprises during migrations or packaging changes by providing prescriptive migration steps and verification checks.
- Enable automated route-level enforcement and telemetry for consumption-driven decisions.

Scope:
- Applies to hosted and dedicated customers; private deployments must follow the same contract template but may defer enforcement to customer-managed control plane integrations.
- Covers text generation, chat, and embeddings consumption models. Reranking/structured-output pipelines are covered where token accounting differs.
- Excludes internal-only experimental fidelity flags unless explicitly surfaced to customers.

Key Definitions:
- Fidelity tier: One of {baseline, performance, high-fidelity, deterministic}. Each tier maps to a set of model variants and quantization profiles.
- Consumption unit: The billing unit (tokens, embedding units, or API-call units) used to bill customers.
- Route: API path or customer-configured endpoint with independent SLOs and fidelity settings.

Policy Summary (high level):
1) Assign a default fidelity tier per customer tenant (customer_default_tier).
2) Allow per-route fidelity overrides with explicit customer intent and consent.
3) Map fidelity tiers to pricing multipliers and SLO targets (see table below).
4) During rollout and migration, use a canary window with traffic shadowing and quality checks before billing changes.

Fidelity -> Pricing & SLO mapping (canonical):

| Fidelity tier   | Typical model variants | Quantization profile | Latency SLO p95 | Pricing multiplier (relative to baseline) | Use cases |
|-----------------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------------|------------------------------------------|-----------|
| baseline        | small-optimized        | int8                 | 250ms           | 1.0                                      | low-cost interactive; high volume|
| performance     | medium-latency         | mixed-precision       | 120ms           | 1.4                                      | SLA-sensitive web apps|
| high-fidelity   | large-llm-varianti     | fp16 / mixed         | 300ms           | 2.6                                      | summarization, codegen, reasoning|
| deterministic   | temperature-locked     | fp16                 | 800ms           | 3.8                                      | reproducible pipelines, evals|

Implementation blueprint (technical):
- Data model: store per-tenant default tier and per-route override in TenantConfig APIs.
- API surface: new optional parameters on create-route/update-route: fidelity_tier and fidelity_consent_at.
- Feature flags: rollout.fidelity_policy_v2 (boolean) and rollout.fidelity_metering_v1.
- Enforcement: billing pipeline reads resolved tier at invoice time and applies multiplier to token units and to reserved-capacity allocation.

Sample TenantConfig manifest (YAML):

---
tenant_id: tenant-012345
default_fidelity_tier: performance
route_overrides:
  - route: /v1/chat/completions
    fidelity_tier: high-fidelity
    fidelity_consent_at: 2029-02-20T14:10:00Z
  - route: /v1/embeddings
    fidelity_tier: baseline
    fidelity_consent_at: 2028-12-01T08:00:00Z
---

API contract example (response excerpt):

{
  "route": "/v1/chat/completions",
  "resolved_fidelity_tier": "high-fidelity",
  "pricing_multiplier": "2.6",
  "slo_p95_ms": "300"
}

Migration & customer communication playbook (step-by-step):
1. Discovery and mapping (owner: solutions engineering)
   - Export current route usage by tenant (last 90 days): tokens per route, peak qps, latency distributions, and error rates.
   - Map routes to recommended fidelity tiers using the automated recommendations engine (see Related: predictive-batching-cost-optimizer-prd).
2. Customer impact assessment (owner: account manager + finance)
   - Run billing delta simulation for next 90 days using projected usage and candidate tiers. Provide a clear table with baseline vs projected monthly cost.
3. Communication and opt-in windows (owner: customer success)
   - Send a one-page impact summary and opt-in URL with the new tier selection. Default behavior: do not change billing until customer explicitly accepts or after a 60-day notice window for mandatory moves.
4. Technical canary (owner: eng-platform)
   - Create shadow routes in staging: shadow traffic mirroring at 5% and produce quality metrics (ROUGE/BLEU/embedding-similarity) for 14 days.
   - Run alert checks: fidelity_quality_drop > 3% -> stop rollout.
5. Metering and billing cutover (owner: billing)
   - On customer acceptance, enable meter multiplier flag in billing for the tenant and schedule invoice preview.
   - Post-cutover verify invoices for two billing cycles; provide refund/adjustment play if mis-billing occurs.

Verification checklist (pre-deploy):
- [ ] TenantConfig default tier resolves in config API for all test tenants.
- [ ] Per-route override respected in runtime and surfaced in logs.
- [ ] Billing multiplier applied to invoice preview in sandbox.
- [ ] Telemetry captured: tokens_by_tier, latency_by_tier, quality_metrics_by_tier.
- [ ] Customer-facing guidance content drafted and reviewed.

Metric targets & guardrails (post-rollout monitoring):
- Billing reconciliation variance <= 0.5% in first invoice post-rollout.
- Fidelity_quality_change (customer-per-probe metric) <= 2% negative delta.
- Latency breach rate (requests > SLO p95) change <= +2% compared to pre-rollout baseline.
- If any guardrail violated, initiate automatic temporary downgrade to previous tier and alert product-sre.

Operational runbook excerpt (for on-call):
1. Alert: "fidelity.billing.unexpected-delta" triggered
   - Owner: billing-oncall
   - Checks: confirm multiplier applied, pull invoice-preview, cross-check tokens_by_tier for affected tenant.
   - Remediation: revert multiplier, open ticket to customer, schedule postmortem if >$10k impact.
2. Alert: "fidelity.quality.regression" triggered
   - Owner: product-sre + applied-ml team
   - Checks: compare latest quality probes with canary baseline; inspect model_variant selection traces.
   - Remediation: rollback route override flag and disable canary traffic; notify customer success.

Pricing & packaging considerations:
- Introduce explicit fidelity add-on SKU for non-baseline choices to keep base product price attractive for PLG flows.
- For Reserved Capacity customers, provide an option to lock fidelity tiers for the term; apply contractual pricing instead of dynamic multipliers.
- Add a migration credit policy: customers moving from high-fidelity -> baseline within 30 days get pro-rated credits for overage.

Customer-facing artifacts to prepare:
- One-page impact summary (PDF) per tenant showing current vs proposed cost and fidelity mapping.
- FAQ: short answers explaining what fidelity tiers mean, how quality is measured, and how to opt-out.
- Console UI mock: per-route toggle with "preview invoice" and "run canary" actions.

Rollout timeline (recommended 8-week cadence):
- Week 0: finalize policy and billing support; create docs and legal copy for contractual add-on.
- Week 1–2: internal canary + smoke tests on ~10 pilot tenants.
- Week 3–4: expansion to 50 tenants with targeted usage patterns.
- Week 5: start mass communication to customers with 60-day opt-in window.
- Week 6–8: billing cutover and 30-day verification period.

Ownership and escalation:
- Product: policy definition, docs, and customer comms templates.
- Engineering: feature flags, APIs, enforcement hooks, telemetry.
- Billing/Finance: invoice preview, reconciliation, credits.
- Customer Success/Solutions: discovery, opt-in coordination, support.
- Escalation path: Account -> Solutions Eng -> Product -> Billing -> Legal.

Appendix A — Customer mapping example

Tenant: Acme Retail (tenant-acme)

Routes and recommended tiers:
- /v1/embeddings -> baseline (cost-sensitive analytics)
- /v1/chat/completions -> performance (customer values latency)
- /v1/summarize -> high-fidelity (large-doc summarization quality matters)

Projected cost delta (monthly):
- Baseline billing: $18,400
- Post-mapping projected: $25,720 (+39.7%)
- Recommended action: present two options: staged canary to verify quality improvements or opt-in to lock high-fidelity for 12 months with 10% commitment discount.

Appendix B — FAQ (short)
Q: Will my existing SLAs change?
A: No. Default customer SLAs remain unchanged unless you opt into a higher fidelity tier that brings a new SLO. All changes will be previewed before billing.

Q: Can I opt back to baseline?
A: Yes. Downgrades are supported but may trigger re-training of local caches and transient latency variation. Downgrades within 30 days will be considered for migration credits.

Contacts:
- Product owner: Maya Thompson (mthompson@redwood.ai)
- Billing lead: Ethan Park (ethan.park@redwood.ai)
- Solutions eng: Riya Kapoor (riya.kapoor@redwood.ai)

Related documents and references:
- predictive-batching-cost-optimizer-prd (recommendation engine)
- per-route-slo-creditization-and-billing-prd-2027-09-15
- fidelity-packages-and-customer-migration-playbook-2028-01-24
- console-usage-insights-2-0-release-notes-2027-08-18

Revision history:
- 2028-11-20: Drafted by Maya Thompson.
- 2028-12-12: Added billing multiplier guidance (Ethan Park).
- 2029-02-20: Incorporated canary quality gates and consent flow (Riya Kapoor).
- 2029-03-05: Published with migration checklist and sample manifests.

    fidelity_consent_at: 2029-02-20T14:10:00Z
  - route: /v1/embeddings
    fidelity_tier: baseline
    fidelity_consent_at: 2028-12-01T08:00:00Z
---

API contract example (response excerpt):

{
  "route": "/v1/chat/completions",
  "resolved_fidelity_tier": "high-fidelity",
  "pricing_multiplier": "2.6",
  "slo_p95_ms": "300"
}

Migration & customer communication playbook (step-by-step):
1. Discovery and mapping (owner: solutions engineering)
   - Export current route usage by tenant (last 90 days): tokens per route, peak qps, latency distributions, and error rates.
   - Map routes to recommended fidelity tiers using the automated recommendations engine (see Related: predictive-batching-cost-optimizer-prd).
2. Customer impact assessment (owner: account manager + finance)
   - Run billing delta simulation for next 90 days using projected usage and candidate tiers. Provide a clear table with baseline vs projected monthly cost.
3. Communication and opt-in windows (owner: customer success)
   - Send a one-page impact summary and opt-in URL with the new tier selection. Default behavior: do not change billing until customer explicitly accepts or after a 60-day notice window for mandatory moves.
4. Technical canary (owner: eng-platform)
   - Create shadow routes in staging: shadow traffic mirroring at 5% and produce quality metrics (ROUGE/BLEU/embedding-similarity) for 14 days.
   - Run alert checks: fidelity_quality_drop > 3% -> stop rollout.
5. Metering and billing cutover (owner: billing)
   - On customer acceptance, enable meter multiplier flag in billing for the tenant and schedule invoice preview.
   - Post-cutover verify invoices for two billing cycles; provide refund/adjustment play if mis-billing occurs.

Verification checklist (pre-deploy):
- [ ] TenantConfig default tier resolves in config API for all test tenants.
- [ ] Per-route override respected in runtime and surfaced in logs.
- [ ] Billing multiplier applied to invoice preview in sandbox.
- [ ] Telemetry captured: tokens_by_tier, latency_by_tier, quality_metrics_by_tier.
- [ ] Customer-facing guidance content drafted and reviewed.

Metric targets & guardrails (post-rollout monitoring):
- Billing reconciliation variance <= 0.5% in first invoice post-rollout.
- Fidelity_quality_change (customer-per-probe metric) <= 2% negative delta.
- Latency breach rate (requests > SLO p95) change <= +2% compared to pre-rollout baseline.
- If any guardrail violated, initiate automatic temporary downgrade to previous tier and alert product-sre.

Operational runbook excerpt (for on-call):
1. Alert: "fidelity.billing.unexpected-delta" triggered
   - Owner: billing-oncall
   - Checks: confirm multiplier applied, pull invoice-preview, cross-check tokens_by_tier for affected tenant.
   - Remediation: revert multiplier, open ticket to customer, schedule postmortem if >$10k impact.
2. Alert: "fidelity.quality.regression" triggered
   - Owner: product-sre + applied-ml team
   - Checks: compare latest quality probes with canary baseline; inspect model_variant selection traces.
   - Remediation: rollback route override flag and disable canary traffic; notify customer success.

Pricing & packaging considerations:
- Introduce explicit fidelity add-on SKU for non-baseline choices to keep base product price attractive for PLG flows.
- For Reserved Capacity customers, provide an option to lock fidelity tiers for the term; apply contractual pricing instead of dynamic multipliers.
- Add a migration credit policy: customers moving from high-fidelity -> baseline within 30 days get pro-rated credits for overage.

Customer-facing artifacts to prepare:
- One-page impact summary (PDF) per tenant showing current vs proposed cost and fidelity mapping.
- FAQ: short answers explaining what fidelity tiers mean, how quality is measured, and how to opt-out.
- Console UI mock: per-route toggle with "preview invoice" and "run canary" actions.

Rollout timeline (recommended 8-week cadence):
- Week 0: finalize policy and billing support; create docs and legal copy for contractual add-on.
- Week 1–2: internal canary + smoke tests on ~10 pilot tenants.
- Week 3–4: expansion to 50 tenants with targeted usage patterns.
- Week 5: start mass communication to customers with 60-day opt-in window.
- Week 6–8: billing cutover and 30-day verification period.

Ownership and escalation:
- Product: policy definition, docs, and customer comms templates.
- Engineering: feature flags, APIs, enforcement hooks, telemetry.
- Billing/Finance: invoice preview, reconciliation, credits.
- Customer Success/Solutions: discovery, opt-in coordination, support.
- Escalation path: Account -> Solutions Eng -> Product -> Billing -> Legal.

Appendix A — Customer mapping example

Tenant: Acme Retail (tenant-acme)

Routes and recommended tiers:
- /v1/embeddings -> baseline (cost-sensitive analytics)
- /v1/chat/completions -> performance (customer values latency)
- /v1/summarize -> high-fidelity (large-doc summarization quality matters)

Projected cost delta (monthly):
- Baseline billing: $18,400
- Post-mapping projected: $25,720 (+39.7%)
- Recommended action: present two options: staged canary to verify quality improvements or opt-in to lock high-fidelity for 12 months with 10% commitment discount.

Appendix B — FAQ (short)
Q: Will my existing SLAs change?
A: No. Default customer SLAs remain unchanged unless you opt into a higher fidelity tier that brings a new SLO. All changes will be previewed before billing.

Q: Can I opt back to baseline?
A: Yes. Downgrades are supported but may trigger re-training of local caches and transient latency variation. Downgrades within 30 days will be considered for migration credits.

Contacts:
- Product owner: Maya Thompson (mthompson@redwood.ai)
- Billing lead: Ethan Park (ethan.park@redwood.ai)
- Solutions eng: Riya Kapoor (riya.kapoor@redwood.ai)

Related documents and references:
- predictive-batching-cost-optimizer-prd (recommendation engine)
- per-route-slo-creditization-and-billing-prd-2027-09-15
- fidelity-packages-and-customer-migration-playbook-2028-01-24
- console-usage-insights-2-0-release-notes-2027-08-18

Revision history:
- 2028-11-20: Drafted by Maya Thompson.
- 2028-12-12: Added billing multiplier guidance (Ethan Park).
- 2029-02-20: Incorporated canary quality gates and consent flow (Riya Kapoor).
- 2029-03-05: Published with migration checklist and sample manifests.

title: Fidelity Decision Service (FDS) + Client SDK PRD and Requirements
preview:
Overview:

This document defines the product requirements and high-level design for the Fidelity Decision Service (FDS) and its companion Client SDK. FDS is a low-latency decisioning layer that selects per-request fidelity configurations (model variant, quantization profile, batching class, and routing policy) to meet tenant SLOs and cost constraints while preserving output quality. The Client SDK exposes a compact API for applications to express intent and enforcement boundaries.

Audience:
- Product managers and feature owners responsible for fidelity and cost controls
- Runtime and serving engineering teams implementing decisioning logic
- Developer experience and SDK teams building client integrations
- SRE and capacity planning teams evaluating cost and throughput impacts

Problem statement and motivation:
- Customers want predictable latency and cost while maintaining quality. Today, teams manually tune model variants and quantization and bake those choices into services. This is brittle when workloads shift or a model update changes performance.
- FDS centralizes decision logic so fidelity can adapt dynamically based on request intent, tenant-level budgets, SLOs, and runtime signals (queueing, GPU availability, KV cache hit rate).

Goals:
1. Reduce per-token cost for eligible workloads by 15–30% within 3 months of rollout for customers who opt-in, without exceeding quality regression thresholds.
2. Provide p95 decision latency <= 8ms for hosted inference call path in the common case (decision cache hit).
3. Expose a simple Client SDK so product teams can adopt FDS with <1 hour of integration and feature toggles.
4. Support tiered enforcement: advisory, soft-enforce (probabilistic), hard-enforce (guaranteed).
5. Provide audit and traceability: every decision must include reason codes and metric identifiers for observability and post-hoc evaluation.

Non-goals:
- FDS will not replace model-level quality evaluation or offline evals. It does not modify model weights or training.
- Not in scope for initial rollout: cross-region global consensus; FDS will be region-scoped and tolerate eventual consistency for non-critical config changes.

Key concepts and terminology:
- Fidelity: tuple {model_variant, quant_profile, batch_class, response_mode}.
- Intent: a short descriptor from calling app indicating importance (interactive, background, high_precision, experimental).
- Policy: tenant-level or route-level rules mapping intents and SLOs to allowed fidelity sets.
- Decision Cache: a TTL cache keyed by (tenant, route, intent, qos_bucket) to return identical decisions for repeated requests.

Functional requirements (must-have):
- FDS core service must expose a REST + gRPC decision API: Decision(v1) that accepts a request context and returns a fidelity spec and reason code.
- Decision response must include: fidelity_spec (string identifier), max_tokens_override (optional), allowed_retries, fallback_variants (ordered), reason_code, decision_id (uuid), and ttl_seconds.
- Client SDK must provide language bindings for Node, Python, and Java with identical semantics and an option for direct HTTP calls.
- Policy language: support JSON policy objects with expression predicates for tenant quotas, latency buckets, cost targets, and feature flags.
- Decisioning must consider live telemetry: current queue latency, GPU utilization, and KV cache hit-rate. Telemetry inputs must be pluggable via defined metrics interface.
- Audit log: every decision must be logged to the tenant audit stream with decision_id and inputs for at least 30 days (configurable).

Non-functional requirements (SLOs and constraints):
- Decision API availability: 99.9% regionally; 99.95% for Dedicated customers under contractual SLA.
- Decision API latency: p50 <= 1.5ms (cache hit), p95 <= 8ms, p99 <= 25ms in steady state for hosted tier.
- Decision service throughput: scale to 50k decisions/s per region initially; autoscaling target to 200k decisions/s within 12 months.
- SDK cold-start: bundle size < 150KB (minified) for Node SDK; startup path < 12ms for typical environment.
- Permissioning: decisions that could cause data exfil must be marked restricted and blocked for tenants with restricted confidentiality.

API surface (high-level):
- POST /v1/decisions (JSON)
  - Request fields: tenant_id, route, intent, max_latency_ms, cost_budget_per_token_microunits (optional), recent_decision_id (optional cache hint), attributes (map).
  - Response fields: decision_id, fidelity_spec, ttl_seconds, reason_code, diagnostics_url (for trace).
- GET /v1/policies/{tenant_id} (admin)
- POST /v1/simulate-decision (internal) — used by SDK to dry-run a decision given a synthetic telemetry snapshot.

Policy example (JSON):
{
  "policy_id": "default-interactive",
  "intents": ["interactive"],
  "rules": [
    {"predicate": "gpu_util < 0.7 && p95_latency_ms < 150", "allow": ["variant-a", "variant-b"]},
    {"predicate": "gpu_util >= 0.7", "allow": ["variant-b-quantized", "variant-c"]}
  ]
}

Decision cache semantics:
- Key: sha256(tenant_id + route + intent + qos_bucket + small_attrs)
- TTL: default 30s (configurable by tenant).
- Cache invalidation: immediate on policy update or when model variant health degrades beyond threshold.

SDK design notes (must-have features):
- API: FDSClient.decide(tenant, route, intent, opts) -> Decision object
- Local fallback: SDK ships a tiny local heuristic that mirrors the canonical policy grammar so apps can continue if FDS is unreachable. Heuristic must be conservative (prefer higher fidelity).
- Hooks: expose onDecision, onFallback, onTelemetryEmit for integration with tracing.
- Offline simulation: provide CLI tool and local simulator that accepts traces and runs through policy logic to produce decision audit logs.
- Telemetry sampling: SDK must tag 1% of decisions for end-to-end fidelity quality capture and send to the Evaluate pipeline. Sampling rate configurable by tenant.

Architecture and integration points (high-level):
- FDS primary components: API Gateway, Decision Engine (policy evaluator + ML predictor), Decision Cache, Telemetry Ingest, Admin API, Audit Log Writer.
- Integrations: Serving runtime (for applying decisions), Console (policy editing and rollout), Optimize (model/quantization recommendations), SRE metrics (for health and autoscaling).

Data model (compact table):
- Fields: name | type | description
- fidelity_spec | string | identifier for pre-bundled fidelity tuple
- decision_id | uuid | unique request decision id
- reason_code | string | human/machine friendly reason for the selection
- ttl_seconds | int | how long decision is valid in cache

Rollout plan and milestones:
1. Alpha (internal): implement Decision API + Node SDK local simulator, target 2028-06-01
   - Run internal experiments on low-risk tenants and non-critical routes.
2. Closed Beta (selected customers): add Python/Java SDKs, Console policy editor, audit log streaming, target 2028-09-15
   - Support soft-enforce mode and migration playbook for one pilot account.
3. Public Beta: expand to hosted tier customers, increase autoscaling targets, provide usage-based metering for decision calls, target 2028-12-01
4. GA: SLA and Dedicated support, SDK stable 1.0, migration toolkit, target 2029-03-01

Canary and rollback strategy:
- Canary to 5% of traffic, measure: decision correctness (simulated vs live), error rate, p95 latency, downstream model quality regressions (per-eval prompt set).
- Rollback triggers: >1% customer-facing error rate increase attributed to decisioning, >5% regression in evaluation metric on sampled requests, decision service p95 > 3x baseline.

Migration playbook for customers (high-level steps):
1. Inventory routes and map intents: identify interactive vs background vs experimental flows.
2. Baseline capture: collect 24–72 hour telemetry including p50/p95 latency, token counts, KV cache hit rates.
3. Simulate: use SDK simulator to run historical traces against proposed policies.
4. Canary: enable advisory mode for 5% of live traffic and collect quality signals for 3 days.
5. Gradual enforcement: move from advisory -> soft-enforce -> hard-enforce over 4–6 weeks, monitoring quality and cost.

Metrics and success criteria:
- Primary: percent token-cost savings for opted-in routes (target 15–30%).
- Quality: fraction of decisions that lead to measurable downstream eval regression > 0.5% should be < 1%.
- Latency: decision API p95 <= 8ms (hosted).
- Adoption: 30% of new tenants that are cost-sensitive adopt FDS within 6 months of GA.

Cost model and billing considerations:
- Decision calls are lightweight; charge model options: free up to a threshold, per-decision microbilling, or included in Dedicated SLA bundles.
- Track decision-induced savings per-tenant and expose as offset credits in billing UI.

Security, privacy, and compliance:
- Decisions must avoid exposing tenant data. Attributes in decision requests should be schema-limited and redacted in audit logs when marked sensitive.
- For restricted tenants, FDS will only run in private deployment mode with audit log endpoints inside customer VPC.
- Admin APIs protected by RBAC and audit trail.

Testing and QA plan:
- Unit tests for policy evaluator, decision cache, SDK fallback heuristics.
- Integration tests: end-to-end simulation using recorded traffic with golden decisions.
- Performance tests: load test Decision API to target throughput with p99 latency SLOs.
- Regression harness: connect to Eval pipeline and flag any quality regressions above configured thresholds.

Operational playbooks (brief):
- Incident detection: alert on decision_api_error_rate > 0.5% sustained for 5 minutes, or p95 latency > 3x baseline.
- Local mitigation: switch tenant to advisory-only mode or increase TTL for caching to reduce QPS.
- Escalation path: FDS oncall -> Runtime oncall -> Product lead.

Ownership and success metrics (team-level):
- Product: roadmap and adoption targets
- Eng-serving-runtime: apply decisions to runtime and measure end-to-end latency
- Eng-platform: API, autoscaling and observability
- SRE: SLIs/SLOs and runbooks

Open questions and risks:
- Risk: policy complexity could lead to unexpected interactions; mitigation: limit initial grammar and provide simulator + linter.
- Risk: decision latency spike under load; mitigation: aggressive caching + local heuristic fallback.
- Open question: default billing model—free vs per-decision—requires commercial alignment before GA.

Appendix A — Example decision response (JSON):
{
  "decision_id": "8f47a2c2-9f05-4a8b-9cfe-1b2d3e5a6f7c",
  "fidelity_spec": "variant-b-quantized-8bit/fast-batch",
  "ttl_seconds": 30,
  "reason_code": "GPU_HIGH_QUOTA",
  "diagnostics_url": "https://console.internal.example.com/decisions/8f47a2c2"
}

Appendix B — CLI simulator example:
# Simulate historical trace and print aggregate decisions
$ fds-simulate --trace traces/tenant-123-24h.json --policy ./policies/default-interactive.json --output decisions.json

Related work and references:
- See product overview: consumption-sla-playbook-for-regulated-verticals for compliance considerations.
- See engineering design: runtime-architecture/kv-cache-and-prefetching-design for cache coordination notes.

Next steps and immediate action items (owner: Sofia Chen):
- Finalize policy grammar and linter (deadline 2028-05-15).
- Kick off Node SDK alpha sprint (engineering owner: Priya Shah).
- Bench decision API prototype for p95 latency (engineering owner: Dmitri Volkov).

title: Console Usage Insights 2.0 - Release Notes and Migration Guide
preview:
Overview

This document describes the Console Usage Insights 2.0 release (codename: Insightful), the user-facing features, operational changes, breaking/deprecation items, and a migration plan for teams and customers. The goal of 2.0 is to improve per-route observability, reduce noisy telemetry ingestion cost, and provide a first-class drilldown from token-level cost to caller traces.

Highlights (what's new)

- Token-level cost breakdown: per-request token attribution across model, batching, and caching stages in the Console UI and API.
- Route timeline explorer: interactive per-route latency and cost histograms with configurable zoom ranges and percentile overlays.
- Trace integration: native links to Redwood traces and automatic span correlation for Console-visible requests.
- Retention & sampling changes: default storage retention for usage events changes from 90 days to 30 days for free-tier tenants; enterprise tenants keep 365-day retention unless overridden.
- Streaming telemetry ingestion: new bulk upload path to decrease ingestion overhead for high-volume enterprise customers.
- New API endpoints and CLI helpers for exporting filtered usage windows (see API and CLI section).

Detailed changes

1) UI and UX
- New "Insights" top-level nav.
- Per-route timeline shows: p50/p90/p99 latency, tokens consumed, cache-hit ratio, and inferred batching factor.
- Click a timeline bucket to open a side panel with a list of example requests (anonymized IDs), with links to traces.
- CSV/JSON export now supports column-level masking and schema versions v1 and v2.

2) Backend / Telemetry
- Usage ingestion pipeline updated to support a streaming bulk path (Kafka-based) to reduce transient spikes in write amplification.
- Events schema v2: adds fields batch_size_estimate and cache_flags. Legacy schema v1 is still accepted but marked for deprecation (see breaking changes).
- Sampling strategy: default dynamic-sample rate introduced to keep ingestion costs under target thresholds while preserving tail latency signals.

3) API / CLI
- New endpoints (HTTP/REST):
  - GET /v2/insights/routes/{route_id}?start={}&end={}&granularity={} (returns timeline with percentiles)
  - POST /v2/insights/export (bulk export job, supports filters and delivery to S3 compatible endpoints)
- CLI helper: redwood-insights export --route <id> --start <ts> --end <ts> --format json --out path/

Breaking changes and deprecations

The following changes require action during migration. See Migration steps below for sequence and recommended rollback options.

| Item | Impact | Migration window | Action required |
|------|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| Events schema v1 marked deprecated | ingestion of legacy fields will be accepted but all v1-only fields will be dropped by 2028-01-01 | Deprecation starts 2027-10-01 | Update instrumentation to emit events.schema_version = "v2" and include batch_size_estimate and cache_flags |
| Retention default change (free -> 30d) | historical queries older than 30d will be unavailable for free tenants after 2027-10-15 | 2027-09-01 -> 2027-10-15 | Free-tenant owners: opt-in to extended retention or export historic data before retention cutoff |
| Old CSV export endpoint /v1/insights/export | 30-day sunset after GA | 2027-09-01 -> 2027-09-30 | Switch scripts to call /v2/insights/export; see API examples below |

Migration steps (recommended for internal teams and customers)

1) Inventory: list all exporters, SDKs, ingestion clients, and on-prem connectors that send usage events. Include owner and contact. (Owners: Platform Metrics team)
2) Enable verification mode: set a staging flag to emit both schema v1 and v2 payloads for 48–72 hours and capture differences.
3) Update SDKs: upgrade to redwood-telemetry SDK >= 2.0. Ensure new fields batch_size_estimate and cache_flags are populated where possible.
4) Run export smoke tests: call POST /v2/insights/export with a small window and validate CSV/JSON result encoding.
5) Rollout retention opt-ins for customers who need longer history: use the Console "Retention" modal or the API PATCH /v2/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention.
3) Turn on dynamic sampling dashboard: verify ingestion rates and ensure cost alerts are within thresholds.
4) Monitor: set the following alerts (suggested):
   - ingestion.rate > 1.2x baseline for 15m -> paging to Platform Metrics oncall
   - export.job.failure.rate > 5% for 1h -> paging to Platform SRE

Note: Migration order should prioritize instrumentation updates first, then export endpoint switching, then retention opt-ins.

Rollout plan and feature flag strategy

- Canary phase (weeks 0–2): enable Insights 2.0 for 5% of internal tenants behind feature flag insights_v2_canary. Collect telemetry and user feedback.
- Beta phase (weeks 3–6): expand to 25% of tenants; stabilize sampling and export flows.
- GA (week 8): flip default for all produced tenants. Free-tier retention change rolled on GA date for free tenant cohort.
- Emergency rollback: feature flag insights_v2_canary and insights_v2_default allow immediate UI/API rollback. Backend acceptance continues to support v1 flows for 30 days post-GA.

Operational considerations and SLOs

- New SLOs introduced:
  - Insights query latency: 99th percentile query response time < 2s for 1h windows (SLO target 99.9%).
  - Export job success: > 99% completion within SLA window (SLA variable by tier: free=1h, standard=30m, enterprise=10m).
  - Ingestion availability: 99.95% monthly.
- Cost guardrails: dynamic sampling will attempt to keep ingestion cost delta <= 15% vs baseline. If ingestion cost exceeds 15% for a rolling 7-day window, automatic throttling will reduce sample rates and send notifications to tenant admins.

API examples and CLI

- Timeline query (curl):
  curl -X GET "https://api.redwood.com/v2/insights/routes/route_123?start=2027-08-01T00:00:00Z&end=2027-08-02T00:00:00Z&granularity=5m" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY"

- Export job (curl):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.com/v2/insights/export \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
    -d '{"route_ids":["route_123"],"start":"2027-08-01T00:00:00Z","end":"2027-08-02T00:00:00Z","delivery":{"type":"s3","uri":"s3://my-bucket/redwood-insights/"}}'

- CLI (example):
  redwood-insights export --route route_123 --start 2027-08-01T00:00:00Z --end 2027-08-02T00:00:00Z --format json --out ./insights-aug1.json

Troubleshooting checklist

- Missing fields in exports: verify ingestion client upgraded to SDK >=2.0 and that events.schema_version == "v2".
- Export job stuck in queued state: check export-job-worker logs and S3 credentials / bucket policy.
- Sudden ingestion spike: check for misconfigured batchers or a sudden increase in sampling factor; use the dynamic-sampling dashboard to inspect recent adjustments.

Ownership and support

- Product owner: Aisha Patel (product)
- Technical owner: Marco Nguyen (Platform Metrics)
- SRE support: Platform SRE oncall (pager: #platform-sre)
- Support playbook: file a ticket in Jira project RW-INSIGHTS with priority P2 for non-blocking issues and P0 for production data loss or availability incidents.

Rollout timeline and rollback plan

- 2027-08-18: GA for internal tenant cohort, release notes published.
- 2027-09-01: Public GA; documentation and SDKs published.
- 2027-10-01: Events schema v1 marked deprecated (warnings in ingestion logs).
- 2027-11-01: v1 acceptance read-only for 30 days, removal scheduled for 2028-01-01 unless extended by exec approval.

Appendix: Data retention decision rationale

- We reduced the free-tier retention to 30 days to control long-tail storage costs while offering clear opt-in and export mechanisms. Enterprise tenants retain 365 days by default because of regulatory and audit demands. The decision was made after cost modeling that showed a 28% monthly storage reduction for free cohort and negligible effect on SLO signal fidelity for recent 30-day windows.

Appendix: Links and references

- Console docs: /confluence/product-docs/product-overview/console-usage-and-metrics-quickstart
- SDK upgrade guide: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/sdk-guides/telemetry-sdk-upgrade-2-0
- Platform metrics dashboards: /confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/insights-telemetry-dashboards

End of document

Overview

This document describes the Console Usage Insights 2.0 release (codename: Insightful), the user-facing features, operational changes, breaking/deprecation items, and a migration plan for teams and customers. The goal of 2.0 is to improve per-route observability, reduce noisy telemetry ingestion cost, and provide a first-class drilldown from token-level cost to caller traces.

Highlights (what's new)

- Token-level cost breakdown: per-request token attribution across model, batching, and caching stages in the Console UI and API.
- Route timeline explorer: interactive per-route latency and cost histograms with configurable zoom ranges and percentile overlays.
- Trace integration: native links to Redwood traces and automatic span correlation for Console-visible requests.
- Retention & sampling changes: default storage retention for usage events changes from 90 days to 30 days for free-tier tenants; enterprise tenants keep 365-day retention unless overridden.
- Streaming telemetry ingestion: new bulk upload path to decrease ingestion overhead for high-volume enterprise customers.
- New API endpoints and CLI helpers for exporting filtered usage windows (see API and CLI section).

Detailed changes

1) UI and UX
- New "Insights" top-level nav.
- Per-route timeline shows: p50/p90/p99 latency, tokens consumed, cache-hit ratio, and inferred batching factor.
- Click a timeline bucket to open a side panel with a list of example requests (anonymized IDs), with links to traces.
- CSV/JSON export now supports column-level masking and schema versions v1 and v2.

title: Unified Capacity Transition PRD: SLO-aligned Consumption and Dedicated Migration
preview:
Executive summary:

This document defines the product requirements, design constraints, rollout roadmap, pricing impacts, and release notes for the "Unified Capacity Transition" initiative. The initiative enables customers to move from legacy per-token consumption and ad-hoc reserved capacity models to an SLO-aligned consumption paradigm that unifies Dedicated reservations, hosted usage, and private deployments under a single migration playbook.

Goals:
- Provide a predictable migration path for customers on Dedicated capacity and legacy token billing to SLO-aligned bundles.
- Maintain or improve customer latency SLOs (p50 <= 45ms for short sequences, p95 <= 250ms for chat workloads) during and after migration.
- Prevent revenue regressions and make pricing transparent to customers.
- Reduce operational overhead for account teams via automated migration tooling, billing reconciliations, and clear rollback policies.

Non-goals:
- This PRD does not change model internals, kernel selection, or per-model performance optimizations.
- It does not replace existing enterprise contract negotiation workflows; those remain supported.

Stakeholders and ownership:
- Product (owner): Maya Thompson — overall initiative and PRD.
- Eng Platform: Ravi Singh — migration tooling and rollout automation.
- Eng SRE: Lina Gomez — SLO validation, monitoring, and incident readiness.
- Finance: Alisha Verma — pricing modelling and revenue reconciliation.
- Sales/CS: Account teams (named reps) — customer communications and approvals.

Success metrics (90-day post-rollout):
- 85% of migrated customers meet their pre-migration latency SLOs within +/-10% of baseline.
- Migration churn rate <= 2% attributable to performance regressions.
- Average time-to-migrate (from opt-in) <= 14 days for customers with < 200M tokens/month.
- Net new ARR uplift from SLO-aligned bundles >= 10% for migrated cohort within 3 months.

Functional requirements:
1) Migration offer types (API + Console)
- Offer A: "SLO Starter Bundle" — 99th percentile latency SLO not guaranteed; discounted bundle for low-volume customers.
- Offer B: "SLO Standard Bundle" — p95 latency SLO guarantee with transparent overage pricing.
- Offer C: "Dedicated-equivalent Bundle" — reserved capacity equivalence with burst allowance and SLA.

2) Migration UI and API endpoints
- Console: Guided migration wizard (preview, cost estimate, staging run, apply, rollback).
- API: POST /migration/estimate, POST /migration/start, GET /migration/status, POST /migration/rollback.
- API auth: requires account admin role and signed acceptance of new T&Cs.

3) Billing and reconciliation rules
- Billing engine must support dual-metering window for up to 60 days: customers pay both legacy and new bundle for reconciliation, with credits applied automatically.
- Financial holdback: 10% of first-month revenue held in escrow to cover performance crediting if SLOs are missed.

4) Observability and quality guardrails
- Per-account SLO dashboards (latency, error-rate, token consumption, tail-latency breakdown).
- Auto-escalation: if p95 latency degrades > 20% relative to baseline for 6 consecutive hours, flip to mitigation mode (see Runbook).

Non-functional requirements:
- Backwards-compatible APIs: endpoints must not break existing key flows.
- Rollback time target: full rollback under 30 minutes for customers with < 1M QPS-equivalent requests.
- Storage: migration metadata retention for 365 days.
- Data residency: migration operations must honor customer's region and VPC constraints.

Design overview:
- High-level approach: create a "migration orchestration" service that coordinates cost estimates, staged test runs against a canary route, and final cutover. The orchestration service communicates with: billing, routing, runtime control plane, monitoring, and the console.

- Components:
  - Migration Orchestrator (service): tracks state machine (estimate -> staging -> validate -> cutover -> reconcile -> archive).
  - Estimate Engine: runs simulation using historical token traces + model profiles to produce cost and SLO risk assessments.
  - Staging Proxy: routes a configurable percentage of production traffic to the new bundle variant for validation.
  - Billing Reconciler: coordinates dual-metering and credits.
  - SLO Validator: compares live metrics to baseline and flags pass/fail.

API contract examples:
- Estimate request (JSON)
```
POST /v1/migration/estimate
{
  "account_id": "acct_123",
  "lookback_days": 30,
  "models": ["redwood/gemini-13b", "open/guanaco-7b"],
  "target_bundle": "slo-standard"
}
```
- Example estimate response (abridged)
```
{
  "monthly_cost": "USD 12,400",
  "predicted_p95_delta": "+12%",
  "recommended_configuration": {"burst_capacity": "2000 rps", "quantization_profile": "q3"}
}
```

Migration phases and timeline (high level):
- Phase 0 (Prep, 0-2 weeks): product sign-off, legal T&Cs update, pricing modelling finalized, pilot cohort selected.
- Phase 1 (Pilot, 2-8 weeks): 5–10 reference customers, staging runs, end-to-end billing validation.
- Phase 2 (Scale rollout, 8-20 weeks): self-serve console enablement, sales enablement docs, automated reconciliations.
- Phase 3 (General availability, 20+ weeks): GA launch, public docs, migration incentives sunset.

Rollout steps (detailed numbered procedure):
1. Prepare account profile and collect 30-day telemetry snapshot.
2. Run Estimate Engine to generate cost + SLO risk.
3. Present migration plan to customer via Console and Sales.
4. If customer accepts, create staging proxy and route 1–5% traffic for 48 hours.
5. Run automated SLO validation. If pass, increment to 25% for 72 hours.
6. Final cutover window is scheduled; during cutover route 100% traffic and enable Billing Reconciler.
7. Monitor for 72 hours; if any auto-escalation triggers, run rollback (documented in Runbook).
8. After 60-day dual-billing window, finalize reconciliation and archive migration metadata.

Runbook: auto-escalation and rollback (engineer-facing):
- Trigger conditions:
  - p95 latency increase > 20% vs baseline for 6h.
  - Error rate increase > 1% absolute for 1h.
  - Token consumption variance > 30% and billing delta > 15% projected.
- Immediate actions:
  1) On-call SRE receives page with affected account_id and baseline.
  2) Migration Orchestrator toggles staging proxy to previous route.
  3) Billing Reconciler marks account for manual review and applies interim credits.
  4) Post-incident triage within 48 hours and customer-facing status update.

Pricing and packaging implications:
- Principle: preserve transparency and provide a clear economic mapping between token usage and bundle capacity.
- Example pricing tiers (illustrative):
| Bundle | Monthly commit | Included tokens/mo | Burst | SLA | Overages |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|-------|-----|----------|
| Starter | $1,200 | 50M | 500 rps | best-effort | $0.00007/token |
| Standard | $10,000 | 500M | 5k rps | p95 latency SLO | $0.00006/token |
| Dedicated-equivalent | $45,000 | 3B | 20k rps | SLA-backed | negotiated |

- Migration credits and incentives:
  - 30% credit on the first month for pilots.
  - Volume commitment discounts continue to apply post-migration.

Roadmap and milestones (quarterly themes):
- Q2 2027: Pilot cohort execution, Estimate Engine MVP, console wizard alpha.
- Q3 2027: Billing Reconciler beta, expanded pilot, early self-serve flows.
- Q4 2027: Scale rollout, analytics dashboards GA, CS/sales playbooks.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Customers experience tail-latency regressions after bundling.
  - Mitigation: Staging proxy with progressive traffic ramp + canary performance tuning.
- Risk: Billing reconciliation disputes inflate finance workload.
  - Mitigation: Automate credits, provide clear reconciliation reports, finance SLA with account teams.
- Risk: Migration tooling introduces new surface area for incidents.
  - Mitigation: Circuit-breaker patterns, per-account toggles, and 30-day metadata retention for audit.

Developer notes and implementation constraints:
- Orchestrator state machine must be idempotent; design to tolerate at-least-once events.
- Ensure all calls to runtime control plane include reason and correlation_id for audit.
- Migration UI must surface estimated risk categories (Low/Medium/High) and the primary driver (latency, token variance, model mismatch).

Planned release notes (to be included in product release):
- Added migration wizard to Console to transition customers to SLO-aligned bundles.
- New API endpoints: /v1/migration/estimate, /v1/migration/start, /v1/migration/status, /v1/migration/rollback.
- Billing: dual-metering window support and automatic crediting for migration reconciliations.

Appendices:
A. Example migration checklist (for CS and Sales)
1) Confirm customer's legal acceptance and billing contact.
2) Run Estimate and review predicted p95 delta.
3) Schedule staging window and collect required runbooks.
4) Assign SRE on-call for the cutover window.
5) Validate reconciliation report post-60 days.

B. Example CLI snippet to start a migration (internal tooling)
```
redwood migrate start --account acct_123 --bundle slo-standard --staging-percent 5 --contact "cs-rep@example.com"
```

C. Ownership and contacts
- Product owner: Maya Thompson (maya@redwood.ai)
- Engineering lead: Ravi Singh (ravi@redwood.ai)
- SRE lead: Lina Gomez (lina@redwood.ai)
- Billing contact: Alisha Verma (alisha@redwood.ai)

Document history and next steps:
- 2027-06-18: PRD draft created.
- 2027-06-20: Engineering review added; Estimate Engine API contract expanded.
- Next: finalize legal T&Cs and begin pilot recruitment (target start 2027-07-05).

Related resources:
- Migration orchestration technical design (link to eng doc to be added).
- Billing reconciler spec (finance link).

- Phase 2 (Scale rollout, 8-20 weeks): self-serve console enablement, sales enablement docs, automated reconciliations.
- Phase 3 (General availability, 20+ weeks): GA launch, public docs, migration incentives sunset.

Rollout steps (detailed numbered procedure):
1. Prepare account profile and collect 30-day telemetry snapshot.
2. Run Estimate Engine to generate cost + SLO risk.
3. Present migration plan to customer via Console and Sales.
4. If customer accepts, create staging proxy and route 1–5% traffic for 48 hours.
5. Run automated SLO validation. If pass, increment to 25% for 72 hours.
6. Final cutover window is scheduled; during cutover route 100% traffic and enable Billing Reconciler.
7. Monitor for 72 hours; if any auto-escalation triggers, run rollback (documented in Runbook).
8. After 60-day dual-billing window, finalize reconciliation and archive migration metadata.

Runbook: auto-escalation and rollback (engineer-facing):
- Trigger conditions:
  - p95 latency increase > 20% vs baseline for 6h.
  - Error rate increase > 1% absolute for 1h.
  - Token consumption variance > 30% and billing delta > 15% projected.
- Immediate actions:
  1) On-call SRE receives page with affected account_id and baseline.
  2) Migration Orchestrator toggles staging proxy to previous route.
  3) Billing Reconciler marks account for manual review and applies interim credits.
  4) Post-incident triage within 48 hours and customer-facing status update.

Pricing and packaging implications:
- Principle: preserve transparency and provide a clear economic mapping between token usage and bundle capacity.
- Example pricing tiers (illustrative):
| Bundle | Monthly commit | Included tokens/mo | Burst | SLA | Overages |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|-------|-----|----------|
| Starter | $1,200 | 50M | 500 rps | best-effort | $0.00007/token |
| Standard | $10,000 | 500M | 5k rps | p95 latency SLO | $0.00006/token |
| Dedicated-equivalent | $45,000 | 3B | 20k rps | SLA-backed | negotiated |

- Migration credits and incentives:
  - 30% credit on the first month for pilots.
  - Volume commitment discounts continue to apply post-migration.

Roadmap and milestones (quarterly themes):
- Q2 2027: Pilot cohort execution, Estimate Engine MVP, console wizard alpha.
- Q3 2027: Billing Reconciler beta, expanded pilot, early self-serve flows.
- Q4 2027: Scale rollout, analytics dashboards GA, CS/sales playbooks.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Customers experience tail-latency regressions after bundling.
  - Mitigation: Staging proxy with progressive traffic ramp + canary performance tuning.
- Risk: Billing reconciliation disputes inflate finance workload.
  - Mitigation: Automate credits, provide clear reconciliation reports, finance SLA with account teams.
- Risk: Migration tooling introduces new surface area for incidents.
  - Mitigation: Circuit-breaker patterns, per-account toggles, and 30-day metadata retention for audit.

Developer notes and implementation constraints:
- Orchestrator state machine must be idempotent; design to tolerate at-least-once events.
- Ensure all calls to runtime control plane include reason and correlation_id for audit.
- Migration UI must surface estimated risk categories (Low/Medium/High) and the primary driver (latency, token variance, model mismatch).

Planned release notes (to be included in product release):
- Added migration wizard to Console to transition customers to SLO-aligned bundles.
- New API endpoints: /v1/migration/estimate, /v1/migration/start, /v1/migration/status, /v1/migration/rollback.
- Billing: dual-metering window support and automatic crediting for migration reconciliations.

Appendices:
A. Example migration checklist (for CS and Sales)
1) Confirm customer's legal acceptance and billing contact.
2) Run Estimate and review predicted p95 delta.
3) Schedule staging window and collect required runbooks.
4) Assign SRE on-call for the cutover window.
5) Validate reconciliation report post-60 days.

B. Example CLI snippet to start a migration (internal tooling)
```
redwood migrate start --account acct_123 --bundle slo-standard --staging-percent 5 --contact "cs-rep@example.com"
```

C. Ownership and contacts
- Product owner: Maya Thompson (maya@redwood.ai)
- Engineering lead: Ravi Singh (ravi@redwood.ai)
- SRE lead: Lina Gomez (lina@redwood.ai)
- Billing contact: Alisha Verma (alisha@redwood.ai)

Document history and next steps:
- 2027-06-18: PRD draft created.
- 2027-06-20: Engineering review added; Estimate Engine API contract expanded.
- Next: finalize legal T&Cs and begin pilot recruitment (target start 2027-07-05).

Related resources:
- Migration orchestration technical design (link to eng doc to be added).
- Billing reconciler spec (finance link).

title: Predictive Bundling & SLA Credit Algorithm Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines the predictive bundling model and SLA crediting algorithm used to (1) recommend bundled credit products for tenants, (2) apply automated SLA credits when latency or availability SLOs are missed, and (3) simulate the downstream financial impact of different bundling strategies. The goal is to enable predictable customer billing while preserving fairness and operational incentives across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployment modes.

Key objectives:
- Provide a transparent, auditable algorithm for SLA credit calculations tied to measurable telemetry signals.
- Surface predictive bundle recommendations that balance expected token consumption, latency SLOs, and cost-to-serve.
- Offer a simulation harness (offline and lightweight online mode) to estimate revenue and credit exposure before packaging changes.

Scope:
- Applies to all Hosted API customers and Dedicated plans that opt-in to usage-bundles. Private deployments may use the same algorithm as a reference but must explicitly opt into centralized billing behavior.
- Covers per-route and per-tenant SLOs for latency (p50/p95/p99) and availability (error budget burn).
- Excludes paper credits/goodwill adjustments which remain a manual CS workflow.

Definitions and primitives:
- Unit: a billing unit equivalent to 1,000 embedding tokens or 1,000 generation tokens depending on product SKU.
- Bundle: named package with N units + SLO promise (e.g., \"Baseline-50k-20ms\") and an associated monthly price and credit terms.
- Credit pool: per-tenant ledger where automatically applied SLA credits are deposited and visible on invoices.
- Exposure: estimated future credit liability (USD) under current observed SLO miss rates.

Algorithm overview (high-level):
1) Consumption forecast: estimate next-period token usage per tenant using exponential smoothing + feature adjustments (seasonality, recent growth spikes, marketing campaigns).
2) Cost projection: map forecasted consumption to expected cost-to-serve using model variant and quantization profile mapping (SKU->cost-per-unit matrix).
3) Bundle scoring: for candidate bundles compute a score = utility(consumption_coverage, cost_savings, latency_hedge) - risk(exposure, SLA-violation-rate).
4) SLA credit mechanism: when observed SLOs in a billing window fall below threshold, compute credit = min(max_credit_cap, severity_weight * usage_fraction * bundle_price).
5) Simulation: run monte-carlo scenarios for the next 12 billing windows and produce an expected credit liability distribution.

Telemetry inputs and sources:
- Primary signals: route latency histograms (p50/p95/p99), 5xx/4xx rates, sustained error budget burn (30-day), token counts by sku and route, model variant mapping, quantization profile labels.
- Data sources: Console telemetry: /metrics/latency-hist, /metrics/error-rate, billing ingestion: /billing/token-usage, runtime mapping: /service/variant-map.

Config table (algorithm knobs):
| config_key | description | default | allowed_values | owner |
|------------|-------------|---------|----------------|-------|
| forecast.window_days | lookback window for consumption forecast | 28 | 7-90 | product-data |
| forecast.alpha | smoothing factor for exponential smoothing | 0.25 | 0.01-0.5 | product-data |
| bundle.max_credit_ratio | max fraction of bundle price that can be auto-credited per billing period | 0.2 | 0.0-1.0 | pricing |
| sla.p95_threshold_ms | maximum p95 latency allowed for covered routes | 200 | 50-2000 | runtime-product |
| risk.exposure_decay_days | window over which past SLA misses decay for exposure calc | 90 | 30-365 | finance |
| scoring.weight_latency | relative importance of latency hedge in bundle score | 1.0 | 0.0-3.0 | product |
| scoring.weight_cost_savings | relative importance of cost savings in bundle score | 1.0 | 0.0-3.0 | product |
| simulation.scenario_count | monte-carlo scenario count | 2000 | 100-10000 | product-data |

SLA credit formula (detailed):
- Inputs: observed_miss_rate (fraction of requests in billing window that exceeded threshold), average_usage_in_window (units), bundle_price, severity_factor (0.5/1.0/1.5 for minor/major/critical).
- Raw credit = observed_miss_rate * average_usage_in_window * (bundle_price / bundle_capacity_units) * severity_factor.
- Credit applied = min(Raw credit, bundle_price * bundle.max_credit_ratio, tenant.credit_pool_balance + manual_adjustment_cap).
- Example: tenant T on a 50k-unit bundle at $12/month, observed_miss_rate = 0.06 over the month, average_usage_in_window = 30k units, severity_factor=1.0 => Raw credit = 0.06 * 30k * (12/50k) = $0.0432 * 30k? (See worked example below.)

Worked numeric example (simplified):
- Bundle: 50,000 units, price = $12/month. bundle_price per unit = $12 / 50,000 = $0.00024/unit.
- Tenant usage in window: 30,000 units. observed_miss_rate = 6% (0.06). severity_factor = 1.0.
- Raw credit = 0.06 * 30,000 * 0.00024 = 0.06 * 7.2 = $0.432.
- Assuming bundle.max_credit_ratio = 0.2 => max_credit = $12 * 0.2 = $2.40. Applied credit = min(0.432, 2.40) = $0.432.

Rollout and operational runbook (step-by-step):
1) Staging validation (product-data): run the prediction and simulation harness against last 12 months for a representative tenant cohort (20 tenants spanning small/medium/large). Validate prediction MAPE < 20% on usage and exposure estimate within 15% of observed credit payouts.
2) Canary pricing experiment (growth): publish opt-in bundles to 5% of newly onboarded tenants for one billing cycle. Monitor: credit payouts, change in churn intent, average ticket volume to CS.
3) Production enablement (pricing + finance): enable predictive bundling recommendation in Console for all self-serve tenants. Provide opt-out toggle.
4) Auto-credit enforcement (billing): enable automatic SLA crediting for bundles for tenants who explicitly accept terms. For those who do not accept, flag credits as \"pending\" for manual CS review.
5) Post-launch audits (quarterly): finance and audit run reconciliation comparing simulated exposure to realized credits and adjust scoring knobs if divergence > 20%.

Monitoring and success criteria:
- Primary metrics: monthly auto-credit payout (USD), credit frequency (percent of subscriptions receiving >0 credit), MAPE of consumption forecast, change in average per-unit realized price.
- SLOs for the algorithm: forecast_mape < 25% (target), applied_credit_false_positive_rate < 1% (customers disputing automatic credits), model_stability (no >10% score swings week-to-week for identical inputs).

Audit and dispute process:
- All automatic credits must generate a structured invoice line item and an audit record in /audits/sla-credits with keys: tenant_id, window_start, window_end, raw_credit_calc, applied_credit, reason_code, operator_override (if any).
- Disputes: CS agent receives an automated ticket if customer disputes > $5 or requests an explanation. The ticket must include the Monte-Carlo scenario snapshot for the billing window and the raw telemetry used for the calculation.

Implementation notes and pseudo-code snippet:
```
# Simplified pseudo-code for monthly credit calc
for tenant in billing_window.tenants:
  forecast = Forecast(tenant.usage_history, window=forecast.window_days, alpha=forecast.alpha)
  bundle = RecommendBundle(forecast, tenant.route_profile, scoring_weights)
  observed_miss_rate = ComputeMissRate(tenant.latency_histograms, sla.p95_threshold_ms)
  raw_credit = observed_miss_rate * tenant.avg_usage * (bundle.price / bundle.capacity_units) * SeverityFactor(tenant.incident_severity)
  applied_credit = min(raw_credit, bundle.price * bundle.max_credit_ratio)
  AuditLog(tenant, raw_credit, applied_credit, bundle.id)
  if tenant.accepts_auto_credit:
    ApplyCreditToInvoice(tenant, applied_credit)
  else:
    CreatePendingCredit(tenant, applied_credit)
```

Security and compliance considerations:
- Credit computation and simulated exposures are financial artifacts. Access to the simulation harness results and raw telemetry must be RBAC-controlled (roles: pricing-analyst, finance-auditor, cs-manager).
- Logs used for audits must be retained for 2 years to satisfy revenue recognition and potential disputes.

Open questions and future work:
- Should we allow negative credits (upsell penalties) when usage exceeds forecast by >200% and latency remains within SLO? (Finance requested a guardrail discussion.)
- Integrate model-based anomaly detection to tag consumption spikes that are non-billable (testing, bot traffic).
- Evaluate replacing exponential smoothing with a hierarchical time-series model for large enterprise tenants.

Ownership and contacts:
- Product: Nina Rao (playbook owner)
- Product-data / Forecasting: Lukas Meyer
- Pricing and Finance: Priya Desai
- Runtime / Telemetry: Marcus Lin
- Customer Success escalation: Talia Brooks

Appendices:
- Appendix A: Timeline for rollout (Gantt summary)
  - Week 0-4: Data integration and simulation validation
  - Week 5-8: Canary experiment with opt-in tenants
  - Week 9-12: Console UI exposure and opt-out paths
  - Week 13: Production enablement and audit window

- Appendix B: Simulation output sample (CSV columns): tenant_id, scenario_id, month, simulated_credit_usd, percentile(5/50/95), forecast_units, observed_miss_rate

- Appendix C: References and related pages
  - Pricing playbooks and previous bundles: /confluence/product-docs/pricing-and-packaging/preemptible-capacity-crediting-guidelines-2026-07-19
  - Consumption simulation toolkit: /confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/consumption-sim-harness

If you plan to implement changes to knobs in the live system, create a PR against infra-configs and include a simulation snapshot comparing baseline vs proposed within the PR description.
