title: PRD: Developer-facing Fidelity Negotiation API
preview:
Overview

Purpose
This PRD defines a developer-facing Fidelity Negotiation API that enables Redwood customers to request, negotiate, and commit to output fidelity levels at request time. The API exposes deterministic negotiation primitives for: 1) proposing a preferred fidelity (quality/cost tradeoff), 2) receiving a server-side fidelity offer (with expected latency and cost estimate), and 3) committing to a selected fidelity before generation begins.

Why this matters
- Reduces overprovisioned latency and cost by making fidelity an explicit contract between client and server.
- Enables finer-grained observability and billing alignment: requests are tagged with negotiated fidelity and can be aggregated for SLOs and chargeback.
- Supports graceful degradation strategies (fallbacks, cached responses) with clear semantic guarantees to clients.

Goals (MUST)
- Provide a simple REST/SDK surface for negotiation that can be included in existing generation flows with <=2 additional RPCs in the common-case.
- Surface server-side latency and token-cost estimates as first-class values in the negotiation response.
- Ensure negotiation is idempotent and safe for retries.
- Integrate with existing routing & billing stack so negotiated fidelity is recorded on request traces and invoices.

Non-Goals (NOT in this PRD)
- Full implementation of adaptive negotiation loops (multi-round ML-driven bargaining). That is scoped to Optimize Conductor iterations.
- Large language-model-internal fidelity retuning; the API surfaces model variants and profiles only.

Background and context
- Existing product feature: latency-tiered routing and model fallback. Customers today select a model or rely on routing rules; there is no explicit programmatic negotiation of fidelity per request.
- Related technical spec: response-fidelity-negotiation-protocol-spec (design-specs). This PRD covers product behavior, acceptance criteria, business rules, and rollout plan.

User stories
1) As a developer, I want to request a lower-cost fidelity for bulk background jobs so I can reduce costs without changing prompts.
2) As a product engineer, I want a deterministic contract so I can rely on latency guarantees for user-visible flows.
3) As an SRE, I want negotiated fidelity to appear in traces and dashboards so we can monitor fidelity-level SLOs.

API surface (high level)
- POST /v1/fidelity/negotiate
  - Purpose: client proposes a fidelity preference and receives an offer.
  - Request fields (JSON):
    - client_id (string) — customer tenant id (inferred from API key when used).
    - prompt_hash (string, optional) — hash of prompt to allow cached-offer suggestions.
    - desired_fidelity (string) — one of [high, balanced, low, custom].
    - max_latency_ms (int, optional) — the maximum acceptable latency client will tolerate.
    - cost_sensitivity (float, optional) — [0..1] where 1 = cost-first, 0 = quality-first.
    - context_hints (string, optional) — hooks for customer-specific constraints (e.g., "safety_priority").
  - Response fields (JSON):
    - offer_id (string) — idempotency token for this negotiation.
    - offered_fidelity (string) — fidelity selected by the platform.
    - estimated_latency_ms (int) — p50 latency estimate for this offer.
    - estimated_token_cost_usd (string) — decimal string cost estimate.
    - compatible_variants (array of strings) — model variant ids the offer can be satisfied by.
    - fallback_policy (string) — human-readable policy key (e.g., "fallthrough:balanced->low").
    - expires_at (ISO8601) — when the offer should be considered stale.

- POST /v1/fidelity/commit
  - Purpose: client commits to an offered fidelity before generation; server attaches negotiated metadata to subsequent generation request.
  - Request fields (JSON):
    - offer_id (string) — previously returned token.
    - generation_request_id (string) — id the client will pass with the subsequent generation call.
  - Response fields (JSON):
    - commit_status (string) — one of [accepted, rejected, expired].
    - negotiation_trace_id (string) — correlates to tracing and billing.

- GET /v1/fidelity/offers/{offer_id}
  - Purpose: inspect offer metadata for debugging and dashboards.

Integration with generation surface
- Generation endpoint (existing) accepts negotiation metadata header: X-Redwood-Negotiation: <negotiation_trace_id> or an explicit field generation.negotiation.offer_id. The runtime applies the negotiated fidelity to select model variant, quantization, and batching policy.

Authorization and authentication
- Negotiation APIs require the same API keys/SCIM tokens as generation APIs.
- Offer visibility: offers are scoped to tenant; a tenant cannot read another tenant's offers.

SLOs and operational targets
- Negotiation p50 latency: <= 20ms (cache hit), p95 <= 120ms (cold path).
- Commit p50 latency: <= 10ms.
- Offer expiration default: 30s.
- Availability target for negotiation endpoints: 99.95% within hosted region.

Telemetry and metrics
Instrument the following metrics (metric names are illustrative):
- fidelity.negotiate.requests_total (tags: outcome, desired_fidelity, offered_fidelity)
- fidelity.negotiate.latency_ms.p50/p95
- fidelity.commit.success_ratio (per-tenant)
- fidelity.offer.usage_ratio (how many offers lead to commits)
- billing.fidelity_estimated_cost_{fidelity} (aggregate estimates vs actuals)
Tracing: attach negotiation_trace_id to generation traces and billing events.

Acceptance criteria (for GA)
- API contract documented and SDKs (Python/Node) implement negotiate/commit primitives.
- Negotiation metrics appear in console dashboards and per-route telemetry.
- 95% of production traffic that uses negotiation experiences <= 50ms extra end-to-end latency when negotiation is included.
- Billing pipeline records negotiated_fidelity on at least 95% of negotiated requests.

Rollout plan
1) Canary (2 weeks): internal teams + 3 pilot customers opt-in flag. Monitor latency, offer acceptance, and billing correctness.
2) Gradual rollout (4–6 weeks): opt-in by tenant with auto-scaling of negotiation service. Add SDK helpers to surface offer lifecycle.
3) GA: on-by-default for new SDK releases; documented migration notes for customers.

Backward compatibility and migration guidance
- For existing customers who do not call negotiation endpoints, system continues to serve using current routing heuristics.
- Customers can progressively adopt negotiation by adding negotiate()+commit() calls pre-generation; SDKs will provide interceptors to add negotiation automatically when enabled.

Security and privacy considerations
- Offer estimates include cost numbers; do not include any PII. Prompt hashes are optional and must be one-way hashed on the client side before transmission when customers opt into prompt-level caching.
- Rate limits: default negotiation endpoints limited to 1000 requests/min per tenant; can be adjusted for Dedicated tenants by contract.

Dependencies and blocked work
- Runtime: ability to tag generation requests with negotiated_fidelity (serving runtime change). Owner: eng-serving-runtime.
- Billing: schema addition for negotiated_fidelity field in invoice events. Owner: finance-and-legal.
- SDKs: Python and Node negotiation helpers. Owner: eng-developer-experience.

Ownership and stakeholders
- Product owner: Jordan Patel (product).
- Tech lead: Priya Natarajan (eng-serving-runtime).
- SDK owner: Mateo Ruiz (eng-developer-experience).
- Billing owner: Claire Martin (finance-and-legal).

Open questions (to resolve before implementation)
1) Offer expiration default — 30s vs 60s tradeoffs for cached offers in high-latency regions.
2) How should cost_sensitivity map to concrete quantization/variant choices? Product to propose a mapping table.
3) Should tenants be able to create persisted fidelity policies (server-side) that bypass negotiation?

Acceptance tests (example)
- End-to-end: customer calls /negotiate -> receives non-expired offer -> calls /commit -> generation request includes negotiation_trace_id -> trace shows applied variant and billing records negotiated_fidelity.
- Telemetry: metrics roll up by offered_fidelity and match observed latency bins.

Appendix: Example negotiate request/response
Request:
{
  "desired_fidelity": "balanced",
  "max_latency_ms": 200,
  "cost_sensitivity": 0.6
}
Response:
{
  "offer_id": "offr_01G1XYZ",
  "offered_fidelity": "balanced",
  "estimated_latency_ms": 95,
  "estimated_token_cost_usd": "0.0032",
  "compatible_variants": ["rwd-v2-balanced-q4f16", "rwd-v2-balanced-q8"],
  "fallback_policy": "fallthrough:balanced->low",
  "expires_at": "2027-05-10T15:22:03Z"
}

Related references
- response-fidelity-negotiation-protocol-spec (design-specs) — low-level protocol and state machine.
- billing-schema-change-ticket-#3421 (finance-and-legal) — invoice schema changes for negotiated_fidelity.

Next steps
- Resolve open questions by 2027-05-18.
- Review API contract with eng-serving-runtime and SDK owners.
- Prepare canary customer list and documentation for pilots.
text:
Overview

Purpose
This PRD defines a developer-facing Fidelity Negotiation API that enables Redwood customers to request, negotiate, and commit to output fidelity levels at request time. The API exposes deterministic negotiation primitives for: 1) proposing a preferred fidelity (quality/cost tradeoff), 2) receiving a server-side fidelity offer (with expected latency and cost estimate), and 3) committing to a selected fidelity before generation begins.

Why this matters
- Reduces overprovisioned latency and cost by making fidelity an explicit contract between client and server.
- Enables finer-grained observability and billing alignment: requests are tagged with negotiated fidelity and can be aggregated for SLOs and chargeback.
- Supports graceful degradation strategies (fallbacks, cached responses) with clear semantic guarantees to clients.

Goals (MUST)
- Provide a simple REST/SDK surface for negotiation that can be included in existing generation flows with <=2 additional RPCs in the common-case.
- Surface server-side latency and token-cost estimates as first-class values in the negotiation response.
- Ensure negotiation is idempotent and safe for retries.
- Integrate with existing routing & billing stack so negotiated fidelity is recorded on request traces and invoices.

Non-Goals (NOT in this PRD)
- Full implementation of adaptive negotiation loops (multi-round ML-driven bargaining). That is scoped to Optimize Conductor iterations.
- Large language-model-internal fidelity retuning; the API surfaces model variants and profiles only.

Background and context
- Existing product feature: latency-tiered routing and model fallback. Customers today select a model or rely on routing rules; there is no explicit programmatic negotiation of fidelity per request.
- Related technical spec: response-fidelity-negotiation-protocol-spec (design-specs). This PRD covers product behavior, acceptance criteria, business rules, and rollout plan.

User stories
1) As a developer, I want to request a lower-cost fidelity for bulk background jobs so I can reduce costs without changing prompts.
2) As a product engineer, I want a deterministic contract so I can rely on latency guarantees for user-visible flows.
3) As an SRE, I want negotiated fidelity to appear in traces and dashboards so we can monitor fidelity-level SLOs.

API surface (high level)
- POST /v1/fidelity/negotiate
  - Purpose: client proposes a fidelity preference and receives an offer.
  - Request fields (JSON):
    - client_id (string) — customer tenant id (inferred from API key when used).
    - prompt_hash (string, optional) — hash of prompt to allow cached-offer suggestions.
    - desired_fidelity (string) — one of [high, balanced, low, custom].
    - max_latency_ms (int, optional) — the maximum acceptable latency client will tolerate.
    - cost_sensitivity (float, optional) — [0..1] where 1 = cost-first, 0 = quality-first.
    - context_hints (string, optional) — hooks for customer-specific constraints (e.g., "safety_priority").
  - Response fields (JSON):
    - offer_id (string) — idempotency token for this negotiation.
    - offered_fidelity (string) — fidelity selected by the platform.
    - estimated_latency_ms (int) — p50 latency estimate for this offer.
    - estimated_token_cost_usd (string) — decimal string cost estimate.
    - compatible_variants (array of strings) — model variant ids the offer can be satisfied by.
    - fallback_policy (string) — human-readable policy key (e.g., "fallthrough:balanced->low").
    - expires_at (ISO8601) — when the offer should be considered stale.

- POST /v1/fidelity/commit
  - Purpose: client commits to an offered fidelity before generation; server attaches negotiated metadata to subsequent generation request.
  - Request fields (JSON):
    - offer_id (string) — previously returned token.
    - generation_request_id (string) — id the client will pass with the subsequent generation call.
  - Response fields (JSON):
    - commit_status (string) — one of [accepted, rejected, expired].
    - negotiation_trace_id (string) — correlates to tracing and billing.

- GET /v1/fidelity/offers/{offer_id}
  - Purpose: inspect offer metadata for debugging and dashboards.

Integration with generation surface
- Generation endpoint (existing) accepts negotiation metadata header: X-Redwood-Negotiation: <negotiation_trace_id> or an explicit field generation.negotiation.offer_id. The runtime applies the negotiated fidelity to select model variant, quantization, and batching policy.

Authorization and authentication
- Negotiation APIs require the same API keys/SCIM tokens as generation APIs.
- Offer visibility: offers are scoped to tenant; a tenant cannot read another tenant's offers.

SLOs and operational targets
- Negotiation p50 latency: <= 20ms (cache hit), p95 <= 120ms (cold path).
- Commit p50 latency: <= 10ms.
- Offer expiration default: 30s.
- Availability target for negotiation endpoints: 99.95% within hosted region.

Telemetry and metrics
Instrument the following metrics (metric names are illustrative):
- fidelity.negotiate.requests_total (tags: outcome, desired_fidelity, offered_fidelity)
- fidelity.negotiate.latency_ms.p50/p95
- fidelity.commit.success_ratio (per-tenant)
- fidelity.offer.usage_ratio (how many offers lead to commits)
- billing.fidelity_estimated_cost_{fidelity} (aggregate estimates vs actuals)
Tracing: attach negotiation_trace_id to generation traces and billing events.

Acceptance criteria (for GA)
- API contract documented and SDKs (Python/Node) implement negotiate/commit primitives.
- Negotiation metrics appear in console dashboards and per-route telemetry.
- 95% of production traffic that uses negotiation experiences <= 50ms extra end-to-end latency when negotiation is included.
- Billing pipeline records negotiated_fidelity on at least 95% of negotiated requests.

Rollout plan
1) Canary (2 weeks): internal teams + 3 pilot customers opt-in flag. Monitor latency, offer acceptance, and billing correctness.
2) Gradual rollout (4–6 weeks): opt-in by tenant with auto-scaling of negotiation service. Add SDK helpers to surface offer lifecycle.
3) GA: on-by-default for new SDK releases; documented migration notes for customers.

Backward compatibility and migration guidance
- For existing customers who do not call negotiation endpoints, system continues to serve using current routing heuristics.
- Customers can progressively adopt negotiation by adding negotiate()+commit() calls pre-generation; SDKs will provide interceptors to add negotiation automatically when enabled.

Security and privacy considerations
- Offer estimates include cost numbers; do not include any PII. Prompt hashes are optional and must be one-way hashed on the client side before transmission when customers opt into prompt-level caching.
- Rate limits: default negotiation endpoints limited to 1000 requests/min per tenant; can be adjusted for Dedicated tenants by contract.

Dependencies and blocked work
- Runtime: ability to tag generation requests with negotiated_fidelity (serving runtime change). Owner: eng-serving-runtime.
- Billing: schema addition for negotiated_fidelity field in invoice events. Owner: finance-and-legal.
- SDKs: Python and Node negotiation helpers. Owner: eng-developer-experience.

Ownership and stakeholders
- Product owner: Jordan Patel (product).
- Tech lead: Priya Natarajan (eng-serving-runtime).
- SDK owner: Mateo Ruiz (eng-developer-experience).
- Billing owner: Claire Martin (finance-and-legal).

Open questions (to resolve before implementation)
1) Offer expiration default — 30s vs 60s tradeoffs for cached offers in high-latency regions.
2) How should cost_sensitivity map to concrete quantization/variant choices? Product to propose a mapping table.
3) Should tenants be able to create persisted fidelity policies (server-side) that bypass negotiation?

Acceptance tests (example)
- End-to-end: customer calls /negotiate -> receives non-expired offer -> calls /commit -> generation request includes negotiation_trace_id -> trace shows applied variant and billing records negotiated_fidelity.
- Telemetry: metrics roll up by offered_fidelity and match observed latency bins.

Appendix: Example negotiate request/response
Request:
{
  "desired_fidelity": "balanced",
  "max_latency_ms": 200,
  "cost_sensitivity": 0.6
}
Response:
{
  "offer_id": "offr_01G1XYZ",
  "offered_fidelity": "balanced",
  "estimated_latency_ms": 95,
  "estimated_token_cost_usd": "0.0032",
  "compatible_variants": ["rwd-v2-balanced-q4f16", "rwd-v2-balanced-q8"],
  "fallback_policy": "fallthrough:balanced->low",
  "expires_at": "2027-05-10T15:22:03Z"
}

Related references
- response-fidelity-negotiation-protocol-spec (design-specs) — low-level protocol and state machine.
- billing-schema-change-ticket-#3421 (finance-and-legal) — invoice schema changes for negotiated_fidelity.

Next steps
- Resolve open questions by 2027-05-18.
- Review API contract with eng-serving-runtime and SDK owners.
- Prepare canary customer list and documentation for pilots.

title: Enterprise fidelity surcharge and grace period policy
preview:
Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference's enterprise fidelity surcharge policy and associated grace period rules. Its goal is to provide a predictable commercial mechanism for covering incremental cost when customers request fidelity guarantees (higher-cost model variants, pinned quantization levels, or reserved warm capacity) while giving a clear transition window for customers migrating across fidelity bands.

Summary (one paragraph)
Enterprise customers may opt into fidelity guarantees for mission-critical routes. When a customer selects a fidelity guarantee that increases runtime cost materially (as defined in the Surcharge Thresholds table), a fidelity surcharge may be applied to their invoice. Customers receive a configurable grace period during onboarding or migration where surcharges are delayed, partly discounted, or phased in to reduce churn and provide time to optimize usage.

Scope
- Applies to: Enterprise and Dedicated customers with contract value >= $50k ARR or explicit SLO add-on contracts.
- Excludes: Self-serve hosted customers on public free/trial tiers unless explicitly included in a signed addendum.
- Applies to: per-route fidelity guarantees, reserved warm pool attachments, and quantization lock requests.

Definitions
1. Fidelity guarantee: any contractual commitment to maintain a specific model variant, quantization level, KV cache hit rate, or reserved capacity that increases serving cost relative to a baseline.
2. Baseline consumption: the customer’s prior month weighted token-average cost using default (non-guaranteed) variants.
3. Surchargeable delta: the portion of incremental unit cost above the Surcharge Threshold that is eligible for surcharge.

Policy details
- Surcharge Thresholds (decision rule): If a fidelity change increases estimated unit cost by >= 12% relative to the baseline, the difference above 12% becomes potential surchargeable delta.
- Surcharge rate: default 75% of surchargeable delta for the first 12 months of a contract; negotiable in enterprise agreements.
- Minimum invoice impact: surcharge will not be applied if projected monthly surcharge < $500 without explicit customer opt-in.

Grace period model (three modes)
1. Ramp-in (default): a linear phase from 0% to full surcharge over the chosen grace window (30/60/90 days). Example: 90-day window applies 33% of the surcharge in month 1, 66% in month 2, 100% in month 3.
2. Discounted delay: customer pays 0% for the grace window, then full surcharge on day (grace_end + 1). Reserved for strategic customers.
3. Hybrid (customer-engineered): negotiated mix of partial immediate charge + shorter ramp.

SLA and time-of-effect rules
- Effective date: fidelity surcharge is effective on the date the fidelity guarantee transitions to an enforced state (e.g., model variant pinned on production).
- Retroactivity: no retroactive surcharges for changes that occurred prior to executed contract amendment.
- Auto-revert: if the customer rolls back fidelity within the grace window, any invoiced surcharge during that window will be credited in the next invoice.

Pricing examples (illustrative)
| Scenario | Baseline unit cost | Guaranteed unit cost | Delta (%) | Surchargeable Delta | Applied surcharge (monthly) | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| A: small change | $0.0008 | $0.0009 | 12.5% | 0.5% unit | $0 (below minimum) | No surcharge applied by min-invoice rule |
| B: moderate change | $0.0008 | $0.0010 | 25% | 13% unit | $1,170 | 75% of 13% * consumed tokens (example 12M tokens) |
| C: reserved warm pool | $0.0012 | $0.0019 | 58% | 46% unit | $8,280 | Large enterprise reserved capacity example |

Operational playbook (ops + sales + billing)
1. Sales signals: During contract negotiation, AE flags required fidelity guarantees in deal desk form and chooses default grace window (30/60/90).
2. Solutioning: SE produces a technical fidelity impact estimate (template below) and attaches to the quote.
3. Legal/Finance: include fidelity surcharge clause in contract appendix B with agreed surcharge rate and grace mode.
4. Provisioning: On activation, product-platform tags the customer routes with metadata {fidelity: guaranteed, surcharge_rate: X, grace_window_days: Y}.
5. Billing: The billing pipeline evaluates the tagged routes each billing cycle, computes estimated surchargeable delta, applies ramp logic, and emits provisional invoice lines with the suffix: "Fidelity Surcharge (provisional)".
6. Communication: Automated email to billing contact on first provisional surcharge and final invoice.
7. Dispute handling: If customer disputes within 21 days, mark invoice line as disputed and halt collection while dispute is triaged.

Technical fidelity impact estimate (SE template)
- Inputs required from SE: route(s), baseline model variant, proposed guarantee (model/quantization/warm pool), expected hourly qps, expected session/profile length, measured token distribution if available.
- Output fields: estimated % unit cost increase, recommended grace_window (30/60/90), estimated monthly surcharge at given consumption bands (Low/Medium/High).

Example SE estimate (YAML snippet)
---
route: /v1/chat/interactive
baseline_variant: rwd-lite-1
proposed_variant: rwd-pico-4q
qps_estimate: 12
sessions_per_minute: 4
avg_tokens_per_session: 260
estimated_unit_cost_baseline: 0.00085
estimated_unit_cost_guaranteed: 0.00140
estimated_delta_percent: 64.7
recommended_grace_window_days: 60
monthly_surcharge_low: $1,200
monthly_surcharge_medium: $5,600
monthly_surcharge_high: $14,500
---

Billing integration rules (implementation notes)
- Billing system will receive a dedicated metadata feed from the platform tagging per-route fidelity attributes. Fields required: customer_id, route_id, fidelity_tag, effective_date, grace_window_days, surcharge_rate.
- On each billing cycle compute: adjusted_unit_cost = baseline_unit_cost + max(0, (guaranteed_unit_cost - baseline_unit_cost) - threshold_delta). surcharge_line = adjusted_unit_cost * surcharge_rate * consumed_tokens * ramp_factor.
- Ramp factor = linear interpolation based on days since effective_date / grace_window_days for Ramp-in mode.

Customer communications (templates)
- First provisional: Subject: "Provisional fidelity surcharge applied to your account" Body: short explanation, expected monthly impact, link to console cost insights and how to opt-out.
- Final invoice: Subject: "Final invoice: fidelity surcharge details" Body: line-itemized surcharge table, link to dispute form.

Monitoring and KPIs
- Adoption rate: % of enterprise customers with fidelity guarantees (goal: 12% by EOY).
- Average surcharge per customer and median monthly surcharge.
- Dispute rate: % of surcharge invoices disputed (goal: <3%).
- Churn delta: monthly churn rate for customers with new fidelity surcharges vs matched controls.
- Time-to-optimization: median days from surcharge start to measured 10% reduction in surcharge via batching/quantization changes.

Metrics feed and dashboards
- Events: fidelity_guarantee.added, fidelity_guarantee.activated, fidelity_guarantee.reverted, fidelity_surcharge.invoiced, fidelity_surcharge.credited.
- Dashboards: Pricing -> Fidelity Surcharge Summary (per-customer, monthly), Billing -> Dispute Tracker, Product -> Time-to-optimization heatmap.

Customer experience and mitigations
- Provide a one-click downgrade path in Console to revert fidelity guarantees (with confirm and billing preview).
- Suggest optimization playbook automatically (top 3 levers: batch window, quantization profile, prefix cache tuning).
- Offer a one-time credit equal to 25% of first-month surcharge for customers who complete a joint optimization workshop with Solutions Engineering within the grace window.

Rollout plan (90 days)
1. Week 0–2: Finalize legal clause and billing metadata contract. Run deal desk training.
2. Week 3–6: Implement billing metadata API and ramp-factor computation in billing pipeline (staging).
3. Week 7–9: QA + finance signoff, dry-run invoices for a sample cohort.
4. Week 10–12: GA to new enterprise deals, automated customer communications enabled.

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: High dispute volume increases A/R workload. Mitigation: pre-billing preview emails, clear console cost previews, and AE/CS preparedness training.
- Risk: Surprise cost for customers. Mitigation: require explicit customer acceptance in contract—no surprise billing for default self-serve customers.
- Risk: Engineering backlog for metadata feed. Mitigation: scoped MVP: route-level tags only, expand to variant-level in Q2.

Ownership and contacts
- Product owner: Priya Natarajan (product).
- Billing engineering owner: Aisha Patel (finance-legal & eng).
- Solutions engineering contact: Connor Li (go-to-market).
- Legal reviewer: Maria Svensson (finance-legal).

Related docs and appendices
- Appendix A: Contract clause language (copy/paste ready)
- Appendix B: SE estimation spreadsheet template (CSV)
- Appendix C: Billing schema (proto) and sample messages

Appendix A (contract language snippet)
"Customers electing fidelity guarantees that result in an incremental unit price above Redwood Inference's Surcharge Threshold may be subject to a fidelity surcharge as described in Appendix B. Surjective application of such surcharge will be subject to the agreed grace window and dispute rights set forth in the SOW."

Next steps
- Gather legal feedback by 2027-03-31.
- Complete billing API implementation by 2027-04-15 (staging).
- Run pilot with 3 enterprise customers during April 2027.

Document change log
- 2027-03-20: Draft created by Priya Natarajan.
- 2027-03-28: Added ramp model and technical SE template after review with Solutions Engineering.
- 2027-04-01: Incorporated billing pipeline notes and reviewer feedback.

title: per-route-slo-creditization-and-billing-prd-2027-09-15
preview:
Overview

This PRD defines the Per-Route SLO Creditization feature: a metering and billing model that maps customer-defined SLOs and route-level policies to fractional credit allocations. The goal is to let engineering teams and finance owners tie SLAs (latency, availability, error budget) directly to a customer’s committed consumption bundle so that billing reflects the cost of meeting higher SLOs without forcing coarse-grained reserved capacity purchases.

Motivation and background

Problem: Customers currently choose between broad reserved capacity (Dedicated) or pay-as-you-go hosted pricing. There is no first-class way to express that route / model X requires 99.9% latency SLO at 50 RPS while route Y is best-effort and can be served by cheaper fallbacks. This leads to overprovisioning, unpredictable bills, and inefficient routing decisions.

Opportunity: Introduce a per-route creditization layer that: 1) allows customers to attach SLO profiles to routes, 2) translates SLO strictness to a fractional credit multiplier, 3) surfaces consumption and projected spend by route, and 4) integrates with billing to charge at SLO-adjusted rates.

Goals (measurable)

- Reduce over-reservation-related spend for customers by enabling route-level SLO differentiation; target 15% unit cost reduction for customers who adopt per-route creditization within 6 months of rollout.
- Provide precise billing visibility: expose per-route spend estimates in Console with <5% error compared to actual invoice for first 90 days.
- Maintain platform reliability: feature must not increase 99th percentile request latency by >2% on control clusters.

Non-goals

- This PRD does not introduce entirely new pricing tiers; it introduces a metering layer and billing multiplier that maps to existing bundles.

User personas and primary user stories

- Platform Engineer (FinOps): "I want to assign SLO-backed credit weights to API routes so that cost maps to importance."
- Product Manager: "I want per-route spend dashboards and forecasts to decide which routes to prioritize for optimization."
- Account Manager: "I want to justify a Dedicated upsell by showing a route is consistently above its soft SLO multiplier."

Functional requirements

1) Route SLO profiles: UI + API to create SLO profiles with fields: name, latency_slo_ms (p50/p95/p99), availability_slo_pct, error_budget_pct, fallback_priority (low|medium|high), and modifier_tags (list).

2) Credit multiplier mapping: system must translate SLO profile to a credit multiplier in range [0.5, 4.0]. Mapping is configurable via a server-side table: stricter latency, higher availability -> larger multiplier. Default mapping must be documented and overridable via enterprise config.

3) Route assignment: customers can attach an SLO profile to any ingestion route or model variant via Console, REST API, or Infrastructure-as-Code (example CLI shown below). Assignment must support bulk operations and labels.

4) Real-time metering: serving runtime must emit per-request metering events that include route_id, model_variant, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms, error_flag, and applied_multiplier. Events are consumed by billing pipeline to compute SLO-adjusted consumption.

5) Console visibility: Cost Explorer and route dashboards must show: raw token consumption, applied multiplier, adjusted credits consumed, 30d rolling spend estimate, and forecast at current traffic.

6) Billing integration: Billing pipeline must accept adjusted credits and produce invoices mapping adjusted credits to existing per-credit price. Accounting must preserve raw consumption for audits.

7) Compatibility & fallback: If incident or region fallback triggers a downgrade to a cheaper variant, the applied multiplier must reflect the active SLO (and may be reduced per config). All fallback events must be logged and included in the allocated credit calculations for transparency.

8) Auditability: Every change to SLO profiles and route assignments must be tracked in an immutable audit log (who, when, previous_values).

Non-functional requirements

- Performance: emitting metering events must add <1ms median overhead and not increase 99th percentile latency by more than 2% on benchmarked paths.
- Scalability: Support 50K routes per tenant and 10k events/sec aggregated ingestion for metering without loss.
- Security: SLO profile management requires role-based access control: manage_slo_profiles permission.

API & CLI examples

1) Create SLO profile (HTTP JSON payload):
{
  "profile_name": "checkout-critical-99p9",
  "latency_slo_ms": {"p50": 30, "p95": 150, "p99": 500},
  "availability_slo_pct": 99.9,
  "error_budget_pct": 0.1,
  "fallback_priority": "high",
  "modifier_tags": ["checkout","payments"]
}

2) Assign SLO to route (CLI):
redwood route assign --route-id r_7f3a --slo-profile checkout-critical-99p9 --apply-to model:variant:v1

Billing mapping (described table)

The following is the canonical mapping table the billing pipeline will use (stored as config in billing-service). Values are illustrative and can be tuned in rollout: 

| SLO Bucket | Multiplier | Rationale |
|------------|------------|-----------|
| best-effort | 0.5 | low latency/availability requirements; allow large batching and cold variants |
| standard | 1.0 | baseline; same as existing PAYG rate |
| assured | 1.8 | higher availability and tighter p95/p99; requires more reserved capacity or higher-precedence routing |
| critical | 4.0 | strict 99.9-99.99 targets and dedicated resource guarantees |

Acceptance criteria

- API/Console: Create/update/delete profile flows work with end-to-end tests.
- Metering: Serving runtime emits events with applied_multiplier and events can be consumed by billing pipeline in staging with end-to-end checksum parity against simulated traffic.
- Console: Per-route cost view shows adjusted credits and matches billing pipeline within 5% in staging over a 7-day window.
- Load: Feature works under 10k events/sec for a tenant in performance tests.
- Security: RBAC enforced with unit tests and access logs enabled.

Monitoring & SLOs for the feature itself

- Event delivery success rate to billing pipeline: target 99.95% over rolling 7d. Alert at <99.9%.
- Applied multiplier correctness: audit test that 1000 sampled events per day have correct multiplier mapping; alert on >0.5% discrepancy.
- Console sync latency: per-route dashboard should show updated 1-minute aggregation within 90s of ingestion; alert at 3m.

Rollout plan and migration path

1) Alpha (internal): Enable for Redwood internal tenants only. Validate end-to-end billing parity using synthetic traffic. Duration: 4 weeks.
2) Beta (opt-in customers): Invite 5 customers across SMB/Enterprise with active FinOps contacts. Provide support runbooks and weekly syncs. Duration: 8–12 weeks.
3) Public launch (GA): Gradual ramp across all hosted customers using feature flag. Default SLO multiplier mapping remains conservative. Announce via Console banner and migration guide.

Migration considerations

- Default behavior: Routes without an SLO profile will continue to be billed as 'standard' (multiplier=1.0).
- Historical invoicing: We will not retroactively apply multipliers to prior consumption.
- Enterprise overrides: Enterprise customers can opt for a custom multiplier mapping; this requires contractual amendments and will be handled via Sales/Finance.

Operational playbooks (summary)

- High failure rate in event delivery: Fail open flag -> continue billing from raw tokens and generate reconciliation report for the tenant.
- Discrepancy >5% between Console and Billing: Trigger automated reconciliation job and notify billing ops; throttle route-level multipliers until resolved.

Ownership and stakeholders

- Product (owner): Asha Patel (this doc).
- Eng infra: Liam Chen (billing pipeline), Priya Nair (serving runtime metering).
- Finance/Billing: Maya Rodriguez.
- Sales/AM: Ethan Park (customer communication and enterprise overrides).

Open questions and decisions needed

1) Multiplier range and bucket boundaries: proposal above is starting point but require Finance sign-off.
2) Handling of cross-tenant routed traffic in multi-tenant clusters: requires SRE input for costing.
3) Discounts and promotions: how do existing token credits interact with adjusted credits? Require legal and finance to confirm.

Appendix: related configs and storage

- SLO profiles stored in config-store with versioning; each change creates an immutable profile version (profile_vX).
- Metering event schema: json-lines with fields [tenant_id, route_id, profile_version, tokens_in, tokens_out, latency_ms, timestamp, applied_multiplier].
- Billing pipeline schema will store both raw_tokens and adjusted_credits for auditability.

Next steps

- Alignment session with Finance to finalize multiplier table (agenda: revenue impact model, sample accounts).
- Prototype: 4-week eng spike to implement metering emission and billing-service support.
- Draft Console UX flows and developer API docs for review.

title: Embeddings Retention & Tiered Storage PRD
preview:
Overview:

This PRD defines a system for configurable retention and tiered storage for customer embeddings stored in Redwood Inference. The goal is to reduce per-customer storage cost, control long-term operational expense, and provide clear controls for compliance (data deletion/retention) while preserving retrieval latency and quality for production retrieval workloads.

Background and problem statement:

- Customers increasingly store large collections of vectors (embeddings) for semantic search, personalization, agent memory, and analytics.
- Hot storage costs are rising as vector stores grow; many vectors are infrequently accessed but must often be retained for regulatory or business reasons.
- Current single-tier storage forces a cost/latency tradeoff: keep everything hot (low latency, high cost) or move to cold (low cost, high latency and reindexing overhead).
- Operators need predictable unit economics per embedding, and product teams need customer-facing controls to tune retention vs cost.

Objectives (must):

1. Provide a tiered storage model with at least three tiers: HOT (low-latency SSD), WARM (object store + approximate index), and COLD (archival object store).
2. Expose customer-configurable retention policies (time-based TTL, usage-based decay, manual labels) with safe defaults and per-tenant overrides.
3. Maintain retrieval SLOs for HOT and WARM tiers: HOT 95th pct <= 50ms for top-k queries; WARM 95th pct <= 350ms for cached ANN fallthrough.
4. Implement transparent billing adjustments and meter usage by storage tier and API access pattern.
5. Provide admin tooling for forced deletion, export, and evidence of deletion for compliance.

Non-goals (out of scope):

- Replacing third-party vector DBs customer-hosted — this PRD concerns Redwood-managed embeddings only.
- Supporting arbitrary custom indexing algorithms per tenant in the initial rollout.

Success metrics (key results):

- Reduce average per-embedding monthly storage cost across eligible tenants by 40% within 6 months of GA.
- At least 60% of coldable embeddings are moved to WARM or COLD within 30 days of rollout for customers with >100M vectors.
- Zero critical incidents attributable to tiered storage in first 90 days post-GA.

Stakeholders and owners:

- Product lead: Maya Chen (owner)
- Eng lead (storage & infra): Rafael Ortega
- Serving runtime/ANN: Priya Natarajan
- Billing & metering: Evan Brooks
- Security & compliance reviewer: Lena Hofstad
- Customer-success playbooks: Nora Patel

User personas and primary user stories:

1. Data platform engineer at a SaaS customer: “I want to configure a 90-day hot retention for my chat-memory embeddings and archive older vectors to reduce costs.”
2. ML engineer: “I want to ensure recalls for our most active documents stay sub-50ms while older content is still searchable, but with higher latency.”
3. Security/compliance officer: “I need an auditable deletion log and export capability to satisfy a DSAR.”
4. Redwood finance: “We need storage-tier metering to bill accurately and provide cost-optimization recommendations.”

Detailed requirements (numbered):

Functional requirements:

FR-1: Tier definitions and semantics (MUST)
- HOT: fully pinned embeddings on SSD-backed instances, immediate availability, full-precision index.
- WARM: embeddings stored in object storage with an approximate ANN index (e.g. IVF/HNSW shards cached on demand). Expected cold-miss penalty when moving from WARM to HOT is a rehydrate operation.
- COLD: immutable archival blobs with metadata only; queries require reindexing or restore to WARM/HOT (asynchronous, minutes to hours).

FR-2: Retention policies (MUST)
- Tenant-level default TTL (integer days) with overrides per-collection.
- Usage-based decay policy: if a vector is not accessed within X days and has <Y accesses in Z days, move to next colder tier.
- Label-based exemptions: "regulatory", "favorites", and "pinned" tags exempt items from automatic sinking.

FR-3: APIs and UI (MUST)
- REST/SDK: POST /embeddings/collections/{id}/retention-policy — create/update policy.
- GET endpoints for tier metrics and per-collection storage breakdown.
- Console: retention editor, projected monthly cost delta visualization, and per-collection preview.

FR-4: Billing and metering (MUST)
- Meter bytes-month per-tier and API access calls that trigger rehydrates.
- Expose projected vs actual cost impact in console and usage reports.

FR-5: Compliance and deletion (MUST)
- Expose bulk deletion API with job tracing and an append-only deletion audit log per-tenant.
- For DSAR, provide export endpoints that produce time-stamped snapshots and deletion receipts.

FR-6: Migration and safety controls (MUST)
- Implement a simulated

title: PRD: Developer Simulation Kit & Telemetry Bridge for Preflight Testing
preview:
Summary

This PRD proposes a Developer Simulation Kit (DevSim) and a Telemetry Bridge that together provide engineers with an offline, high-fidelity preflight environment for: workload simulation, latency/cost forecasting, fidelity experiments, and telemetry capture that mirrors production signals. The goal is to reduce time-to-safe-deploy for engine and product teams by catching regressions and cost surprises earlier.

Context and motivation

- Teams repeatedly run into late-stage surprises when moving workloads from test harnesses to hosted/dedicated/private environments: unexpected tail-latency spikes, different token costs due to batching/caching behavior, and gaps in observability.
- Current sandboxes either (a) are lightweight and fast but underestimate cost/latency, or (b) are production clones that are expensive and slow to provision.
- DevSim + Telemetry Bridge is a developer-focused sandbox designed to be: portable (local/CI/cloud), inexpensive to run, and representative of production runtime behaviors (batching, KV cache warm-up, quantization variants).

Goals (what success looks like)

1. Provide a reproducible simulation that approximates hosted inference latencies and token cost within +/-10% for canonical workloads.
2. Surface fidelity regressions via synthetic and recorded prompt sets before production rollout.
3. Emit telemetry that is structurally compatible with production dashboards and alerts so preflight runs can be used in continuous gating.
4. Reduce mean time to detect cost/latency regressions during release validation from days to hours.

Non-goals

- Not intended to replace full private deployment validation for regulated customers.
- Not a replacement for long-duration soak tests or stress tests at production scale.

User personas

- Platform engineers: want to validate runtime kernel changes and batching policies.
- ML engineers: want to test quantization/fidelity changes against representative prompts.
- Product engineers: want quick cost/latency estimates for feature experiments.

High-level product concept

DevSim consists of three components:

1) Local Simulation Runtime (LSR)
- Lightweight containerized runtime that mimics production serving behavior: continuous batching, KV cache emulation (cold/warm cycles), quantization toggles, and failure injection.
- Configurable hardware profile (e.g., V100/RTX6000 equivalent timings) with pluggable latency/cost profiles derived from production benchmarking.

2) Workload Runner
- CLI and SDK for running recorded or synthetic workloads with control over concurrency, sequence length distributions, prompt sets, and session stickiness.

3) Telemetry Bridge
- Streaming adapter that maps DevSim events to the production telemetry schema and can either forward to a staging ingestion endpoint or write to a staging S3 bucket in the same format as production.

Detailed requirements

A. Functional requirements (MUST)
- FR-1: LSR must implement the same API surface as hosted inference (generate/chat/embeddings) with identical request/response contracts.
- FR-2: Workload Runner must accept a "recording" input (see Recording Format below) and replay with configurable timing and concurrency.
- FR-3: Telemetry Bridge must emit the following canonical event types: request_start, request_end, token_emitted, batch_formed, cache_hit, cache_miss, fallback_invoked, model_variant_selected.
- FR-4: DevSim must provide a cost model that estimates per-request token cost given batch size, quantization variant, and sequence length.
- FR-5: DevSim must be runnable in CI (Docker image < 2GB) and via a managed cloud offering for larger experiments.

B. Non-functional requirements (SHOULD)
- NFR-1: Simulation accuracy: mean latency and token-cost estimator for a standard benchmark (see Appendix A) must be within +/-10% of production for the 50th and 95th percentiles.
- NFR-2: Telemetry event schema must be 1:1 compatible with production staging ingestion endpoints to allow reuse of dashboards and alert rules.
- NFR-3: Default run should complete a 10k-request recorded replay in under 30 minutes on a 4-vCPU, 16GB host for cost/latency summary reports.

C. Operational requirements (MUST)
- OR-1: DevSim images and Telemetry Bridge must be signed and versioned; versions must map to production runtime versions.
- OR-2: Access to forwarding-to-production telemetry must be gated (RBAC) and require an approval workflow.
- OR-3: Telemetry retention for DevSim runs stored in staging must be at least 90 days.

Recording format (simple JSON schema)

- A recording contains: metadata (name, created_by, model_id), prompts (array of session objects), and annotations (optional ground-truth metrics). Example snippet: 

{
  "name": "checkout-flow-2026-10-01",
  "created_by": "ux-team",
  "model_id": "redwood-4x-q8",
  "sessions": [
    {"session_id":"s1","turns":[{"role":"user","text":"Find me the best deal on flights"},{"role":"system","text":"context: premium user"}]}
  ]
}

API & CLI examples

- Run a local replay with the CLI: 

redwood-devsim replay --recording checkout-flow-2026-10-01.json --concurrency 8 --profile v100-equivalent --telemetry forward://staging

- SDK usage (python): 

from redwood_devsim import Runner
runner = Runner.from_recording('checkout-flow.json')
report = runner.run(concurrency=4, duration_seconds=1800)
print(report.summary())

Telemetry Bridge event schema (fields)

All fields are sent as top-level keys in the event JSON (production-compatible).
- event_type (string): one of request_start|request_end|token_emitted|batch_formed|cache_hit|cache_miss|fallback_invoked|model_variant_selected
- timestamp (ISO8601)
- request_id (uuid)
- session_id (string)
- model_id (string)
- variant (string)
- batch_size (int)
- token_count (int)
- latency_ms (int)
- cost_estimate_micros (int)  # micro-units of currency for precision
- tags (array of strings)

Example event:

{
  "event_type": "request_end",
  "timestamp": "2026-10-20T15:23:12.123Z",
  "request_id": "a3f2-...",
  "session_id": "s1",
  "model_id": "rw-4x-q8",
  "variant": "q8-hybrid",
  "batch_size": 4,
  "token_count": 128,
  "latency_ms": 82,
  "cost_estimate_micros": 4200,
  "tags": ["preflight","checkout-flow"]
}

Dashboards and alerts (required)

- Dashboard: DevSim Preflight Summary
  - Panels: latency p50/p95, cost-estimate distribution, batch-size histogram, cache hit rate, model_variant distribution, mismatch-rate vs production baseline.
- Alert: preflight-latency-regression
  - Trigger: p95 latency deviation > 20% vs baseline AND absolute p95 > 400ms (over 1h window).
- Alert: cost-estimate-drift
  - Trigger: mean estimated cost deviation > 15% vs last production 7d rolling baseline.

Quality & acceptance criteria

- QC-1: Run three canonical recordings (chat-short, chat-long, embeddings-bulk). Each run must meet NFR-1 accuracy target.
- QC-2: Telemetry events from DevSim replay ingested into staging must be queryable with the same saved queries as production (no schema adaptation).
- QC-3: Developer onboarding: a new engineer can run the default replay and inspect the preflight dashboard within 20 minutes following README.

Rollout plan

1. Alpha (4 weeks): internal platform and ML teams. Manual sign-up for staging telemetry forwarding.
2. Beta (8 weeks): expand to product teams; add CI integration templates for GitHub Actions and GitLab.
3. GA (12+ weeks): public documentation, console integration to surface preflight reports in the deployment flow, Optimize integration for cost-optimization suggestions.

Rollout checklist

- [ ] LSR Docker images built and published to staging registry and versioned.
- [ ] Telemetry Bridge endpoint deployed to staging and covered by RBAC.
- [ ] Three canonical recordings validated and checked into recordings repo.
- [ ] Preflight dashboards (staging) and alert rules created.
- [ ] CI integration templates added to docs.

Dependencies & integrations

- Telemetry ingestion pipeline (observability team) — must accept staging forwarding with identical schema.
- Cost model calibration dataset from runtime benchmarking (runtime team).
- Console engineering to surface preflight run summaries and attach to rollout approvals.

Risks and mitigations

- Risk: Simulation diverges from production over time as runtime kernels evolve.
  - Mitigation: Enforce image/version parity; run an automated weekly calibration job that compares baseline recordings run in DevSim vs a small production cluster and produces calibration deltas.
- Risk: Telemetry forwarding misused and pollutes production metrics.
  - Mitigation: RBAC + tagging + require explicit approval for forwarding to any non-staging ingestion endpoint.

Ownership and contacts

- Product owner: Maya Chen (product)
- Tech lead: Aaron Li (platform engineering)
- Runtime interface: Priya Nair (serving runtime)
- Observability contact: Ethan Park (eng-platform)

Cost & licensing considerations

- Local DevSim is free for engineers. Managed cloud runs will consume credits; pricing model proposal: small runs (<=10k requests) free during alpha/beta, then a metered credit consumption model integrated into existing consumption billing for customers running large preflight experiments.

Appendix A: Benchmark workloads

- chat-short: 128 tokens avg, 1k sessions, concurrency 16
- chat-long: 1k tokens avg, 200 sessions, concurrency 8
- embeddings-bulk: 8k items, avg size 256 tokens

Appendix B: Example CI step (GitHub Actions)

name: Preflight replay
on: [push]
jobs:
  preflight:
    runs-on: ubuntu-22.04
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Setup Docker
        run: sudo systemctl start docker
      - name: Run preflight replay
        run: |
          docker run --rm -v ${{github.workspace}}:/data redwood/devsim:2026-11-01 \
            replay --recording /data/checkout-flow-2026-10-01.json --concurrency 8 --telemetry forward://staging

Appendix C: FAQs

Q: How closely will DevSim match spot GPU performance?
A: DevSim targets representative timings derived from benchmarking; it is not a real-time substitute for heterogeneous spot performance. Use DevSim to catch relative regressions and for cost trend estimation, not exact millisecond parity.

Q: Can DevSim mutate recordings (add noise, redact PII)?
A: Yes — the recording ingestion pipeline supports anonymization rules and synthetic perturbations to simulate user variance.

Next steps

- Approve scope and acceptance criteria (product review).
- Begin alpha implementation: containerized LSR + minimal Telemetry Bridge + three canonical recordings.

References

- Observability cost/latency guide: /confluence/product-docs/product-overview/observability-cost-latency-tradeoffs-guide-2025-06-09
- Offline cost estimator PRD: /confluence/product-docs/requirements/prd-offline-cost-estimator-sdk-and-simulation-playground-2025-10-12
text:
Summary

This PRD proposes a Developer Simulation Kit (DevSim) and a Telemetry Bridge that together provide engineers with an offline, high-fidelity preflight environment for: workload simulation, latency/cost forecasting, fidelity experiments, and telemetry capture that mirrors production signals. The goal is to reduce time-to-safe-deploy for engine and product teams by catching regressions and cost surprises earlier.

Context and motivation

- Teams repeatedly run into late-stage surprises when moving workloads from test harnesses to hosted/dedicated/private environments: unexpected tail-latency spikes, different token costs due to batching/caching behavior, and gaps in observability.
- Current sandboxes either (a) are lightweight and fast but underestimate cost/latency, or (b) are production clones that are expensive and slow to provision.
- DevSim + Telemetry Bridge is a developer-focused sandbox designed to be: portable (local/CI/cloud), inexpensive to run, and representative of production runtime behaviors (batching, KV cache warm-up, quantization variants).

Goals (what success looks like)

1. Provide a reproducible simulation that approximates hosted inference latencies and token cost within +/-10% for canonical workloads.
2. Surface fidelity regressions via synthetic and recorded prompt sets before production rollout.
3. Emit telemetry that is structurally compatible with production dashboards and alerts so preflight runs can be used in continuous gating.
4. Reduce mean time to detect cost/latency regressions during release validation from days to hours.

Non-goals

- Not intended to replace full private deployment validation for regulated customers.
- Not a replacement for long-duration soak tests or stress tests at production scale.

User personas

- Platform engineers: want to validate runtime kernel changes and batching policies.
- ML engineers: want to test quantization/fidelity changes against representative prompts.
- Product engineers: want quick cost/latency estimates for feature experiments.

High-level product concept

DevSim consists of three components:

1) Local Simulation Runtime (LSR)
- Lightweight containerized runtime that mimics production serving behavior: continuous batching, KV cache emulation (cold/warm cycles), quantization toggles, and failure injection.
- Configurable hardware profile (e.g., V100/RTX6000 equivalent timings) with pluggable latency/cost profiles derived from production benchmarking.

2) Workload Runner
- CLI and SDK for running recorded or synthetic workloads with control over concurrency, sequence length distributions, prompt sets, and session stickiness.

3) Telemetry Bridge
- Streaming adapter that maps DevSim events to the production telemetry schema and can either forward to a staging ingestion endpoint or write to a staging S3 bucket in the same format as production.

Detailed requirements

A. Functional requirements (MUST)
- FR-1: LSR must implement the same API surface as hosted inference (generate/chat/embeddings) with identical request/response contracts.
- FR-2: Workload Runner must accept a "recording" input (see Recording Format below) and replay with configurable timing and concurrency.
- FR-3: Telemetry Bridge must emit the following canonical event types: request_start, request_end, token_emitted, batch_formed, cache_hit, cache_miss, fallback_invoked, model_variant_selected.
- FR-4: DevSim must provide a cost model that estimates per-request token cost given batch size, quantization variant, and sequence length.
- FR-5: DevSim must be runnable in CI (Docker image < 2GB) and via a managed cloud offering for larger experiments.

B. Non-functional requirements (SHOULD)
- NFR-1: Simulation accuracy: mean latency and token-cost estimator for a standard benchmark (see Appendix A) must be within +/-10% of production for the 50th and 95th percentiles.
- NFR-2: Telemetry event schema must be 1:1 compatible with production staging ingestion endpoints to allow reuse of dashboards and alert rules.
- NFR-3: Default run should complete a 10k-request recorded replay in under 30 minutes on a 4-vCPU, 16GB host for cost/latency summary reports.

C. Operational requirements (MUST)
- OR-1: DevSim images and Telemetry Bridge must be signed and versioned; versions must map to production runtime versions.
- OR-2: Access to forwarding-to-production telemetry must be gated (RBAC) and require an approval workflow.
- OR-3: Telemetry retention for DevSim runs stored in staging must be at least 90 days.

Recording format (simple JSON schema)

- A recording contains: metadata (name, created_by, model_id), prompts (array of session objects), and annotations (optional ground-truth metrics). Example snippet: 

{
  "name": "checkout-flow-2026-10-01",
  "created_by": "ux-team",
  "model_id": "redwood-4x-q8",
  "sessions": [
    {"session_id":"s1","turns":[{"role":"user","text":"Find me the best deal on flights"},{"role":"system","text":"context: premium user"}]}
  ]
}

API & CLI examples

- Run a local replay with the CLI: 

redwood-devsim replay --recording checkout-flow-2026-10-01.json --concurrency 8 --profile v100-equivalent --telemetry forward://staging

- SDK usage (python): 

from redwood_devsim import Runner
runner = Runner.from_recording('checkout-flow.json')
report = runner.run(concurrency=4, duration_seconds=1800)
print(report.summary())

Telemetry Bridge event schema (fields)

All fields are sent as top-level keys in the event JSON (production-compatible).
- event_type (string): one of request_start|request_end|token_emitted|batch_formed|cache_hit|cache_miss|fallback_invoked|model_variant_selected
- timestamp (ISO8601)
- request_id (uuid)
- session_id (string)
- model_id (string)
- variant (string)
- batch_size (int)
- token_count (int)
- latency_ms (int)
- cost_estimate_micros (int)  # micro-units of currency for precision
- tags (array of strings)

Example event:

{
  "event_type": "request_end",
  "timestamp": "2026-10-20T15:23:12.123Z",
  "request_id": "a3f2-...",
  "session_id": "s1",
  "model_id": "rw-4x-q8",
  "variant": "q8-hybrid",
  "batch_size": 4,
  "token_count": 128,
  "latency_ms": 82,
  "cost_estimate_micros": 4200,
  "tags": ["preflight","checkout-flow"]
}

Dashboards and alerts (required)

- Dashboard: DevSim Preflight Summary
  - Panels: latency p50/p95, cost-estimate distribution, batch-size histogram, cache hit rate, model_variant distribution, mismatch-rate vs production baseline.
- Alert: preflight-latency-regression
  - Trigger: p95 latency deviation > 20% vs baseline AND absolute p95 > 400ms (over 1h window).
- Alert: cost-estimate-drift
  - Trigger: mean estimated cost deviation > 15% vs last production 7d rolling baseline.

Quality & acceptance criteria

- QC-1: Run three canonical recordings (chat-short, chat-long, embeddings-bulk). Each run must meet NFR-1 accuracy target.
- QC-2: Telemetry events from DevSim replay ingested into staging must be queryable with the same saved queries as production (no schema adaptation).
- QC-3: Developer onboarding: a new engineer can run the default replay and inspect the preflight dashboard within 20 minutes following README.

Rollout plan

1. Alpha (4 weeks): internal platform and ML teams. Manual sign-up for staging telemetry forwarding.
2. Beta (8 weeks): expand to product teams; add CI integration templates for GitHub Actions and GitLab.
3. GA (12+ weeks): public documentation, console integration to surface preflight reports in the deployment flow, Optimize integration for cost-optimization suggestions.

Rollout checklist

- [ ] LSR Docker images built and published to staging registry and versioned.
- [ ] Telemetry Bridge endpoint deployed to staging and covered by RBAC.
- [ ] Three canonical recordings validated and checked into recordings repo.
- [ ] Preflight dashboards (staging) and alert rules created.
- [ ] CI integration templates added to docs.

Dependencies & integrations

- Telemetry ingestion pipeline (observability team) — must accept staging forwarding with identical schema.
- Cost model calibration dataset from runtime benchmarking (runtime team).
- Console engineering to surface preflight run summaries and attach to rollout approvals.

Risks and mitigations

- Risk: Simulation diverges from production over time as runtime kernels evolve.
  - Mitigation: Enforce image/version parity; run an automated weekly calibration job that compares baseline recordings run in DevSim vs a small production cluster and produces calibration deltas.
- Risk: Telemetry forwarding misused and pollutes production metrics.
  - Mitigation: RBAC + tagging + require explicit approval for forwarding to any non-staging ingestion endpoint.

Ownership and contacts

- Product owner: Maya Chen (product)
- Tech lead: Aaron Li (platform engineering)
- Runtime interface: Priya Nair (serving runtime)
- Observability contact: Ethan Park (eng-platform)

Cost & licensing considerations

- Local DevSim is free for engineers. Managed cloud runs will consume credits; pricing model proposal: small runs (<=10k requests) free during alpha/beta, then a metered credit consumption model integrated into existing consumption billing for customers running large preflight experiments.

- chat-long: 1k tokens avg, 200 sessions, concurrency 8
- embeddings-bulk: 8k items, avg size 256 tokens

Appendix B: Example CI step (GitHub Actions)

on: [push]
jobs:

    runs-on: ubuntu-22.04
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Setup Docker
        run: sudo systemctl start docker
      - name: Run preflight replay
        run: |
          docker run --rm -v ${{github.workspace}}:/data redwood/devsim:2026-11-01 \
            replay --recording /data/checkout-flow-2026-10-01.json --concurrency 8 --telemetry forward://staging

Appendix C: FAQs

Title: SLO-driven Prefetch Adapter (PRD) — prefetch-adapter-design-and-acceptance-criteria

Summary:
This PRD defines the requirements, success criteria, and rollout plan for the SLO-driven Prefetch Adapter: a lightweight runtime component that proactively primes model KV caches, session state, and short-lived model variants based on per-route SLOs, observed traffic shape, and cost constraints. The Adapter's goal is to reduce tail latency for critical routes while keeping incremental cost within configured budgets.

Overview and motivation
- Problem: Some customer routes (interactive chat, autocomplete) experience intermittent tail latency and cold-KV misses that push requests over their SLOs. Reactive strategies (retry/backoff, fallbacks) mitigate user impact but do not eliminate user-visible latency spikes.
- Opportunity: We can trade a small, predictable additional inference cost for consistently meeting latency SLOs by prefetching hot KV prefixes, short model warmups, and pinned light-weight variants before expected spikes.
- Scope: This PRD covers the Adapter's product requirements (signals, policies, constraints), acceptance criteria, telemetry/SLI mapping, and an incremental rollout/playbook. Implementation details live in the companion design-spec.

Goals (measurable)
1) Reduce 95th/99th percentile response latency for SLO-tagged routes by at least 30% under representative traffic bursts (measured in synthetic and production trials).
2) Keep incremental per-route token-equivalent cost increase below configured budget (default 3% of monthly usage) for 95% of customers by default.
3) Surface a transparent cost/capacity signal in Console: projected prefetch cost, current warmable prefix hit ratio, and SLO risk index.

Non-goals
- This PRD does not mandate changes to core model kernels or quantization strategies.
- Not intended for long-running batch evacuation or cross-region capacity rebalancing.

User stories
- As a Platform Engineer, I want the Adapter to keep my high-priority customer routes within the 100ms p95 SLO during promotional traffic spikes.
- As a Product Manager, I want a safe default policy that keeps cost low for small teams, and a knob to increase prefetch aggressiveness for enterprise customers with strict latency SLOs.
- As an SRE, I need clear observability so I can detect runaway prefetch loops and rollback per-tenant policies quickly.

Requirements (functional)
1) Signal inputs (must ingest):
   - Per-route SLO configuration (latency percentiles and target windows).
   - Real-time route traffic metrics (RPS, concurrent sessions, sequence length distribution).
   - Historical warm-hit rates (KV prefix hit, model-variant hit) and cost-per-prefetch estimate.
   - Out-of-band signals: scheduled promotions, customer calendar events (optional).
2) Policy engine:
   - Decide which sessions/routes to prefetch and when using a prioritization score = f(SLO_gap, expected_RPS_delta, cost_budget_remaining).
   - Respect tenant-level hard caps (max prefetch tokens/min, daily token cap).
   - Support conservative default policy and configurable enterprise presets (aggressive, balanced, conservative).
3) Prefetch operations (runtime actions):
   - Warm KV cache prefixes up to a configurable lookahead length (default: 64 tokens of KV).
   - Invoke a light-weight model short-warm run (single forward pass for precompilation/KV population) optionally against a low-cost variant when appropriate.
   - Tag prefetched state with TTL and eviction policy aligned with runtime eviction (session-anchored vs global).
4) Safety and failure modes:
   - Instrument per-tenant circuit-breaker: if cost > threshold or prefetch miss-rate rises, auto-backoff by tier.
   - No request should be delayed waiting for prefetch; prefetch is asynchronous and best-effort.
   - Prefetch actions must never expose private customer data to other tenants; all operations respect tenancy.

Requirements (non-functional)
- Latency: decision loop must add <5ms median overhead to routing path when consulted synchronously; default mode is asynchronous to avoid critical path.
- Scalability: adapter control-plane should support 10k+ active route policies with second-granularity decisioning at 10% CPU overhead of existing routing tier.
- Observability: provide SLIs, per-route metrics, and audit logs.
- Security: follow current VPC/KMS controls for any prefetch state touching customer data.

Acceptance criteria (tests and metrics)
1) Lab test: synthetic traffic generator with burst profiles (1s ramp to 10x RPS for 60s) demonstrates >=30% reduction in p95 latency for SLO-tagged routes when adapter enabled vs disabled, while incremental token cost remains below 3%.
2) Canary: Run a 2-week canary with 20 enterprise tenants. Success = no more than 0.5% of canary tenants exceeding their prefetch cost cap and at least 20% of tenants seeing p95 improvement >=25%.
3) Production rollback test: Simulate runaway prefetch by injecting a spike in synthetic sessions; automated circuit-breaker must trigger within 2 minutes and restore normal behavior within 5 minutes of error detection.

SLO mappings and policy defaults
| SLO profile | Default prefetch aggressiveness | Cost cap (monthly) | Notes |
|------------|-------------------------------:|-------------------:|-------|
| Latency-critical (p95 < 100ms) | High (lookahead 64, short-warm model) | 5% | Enterprise default |
| Performance (p95 < 200ms) | Medium (lookahead 32) | 3% | Default for paid plans |
| Cost-sensitive (no strict latency) | Low (lookahead 8) | 1% | Free tier and small customers |

Configuration example (runtime YAML)
----
prefetch_policy:
  route_selector: "/api/v1/chat/*"
  slo_target_p95_ms: 120
  aggressiveness: medium
  daily_token_cap: 500000
  lookahead_kv_tokens: 32
  fallback_variant: "small-quant-v1"
  circuit_breaker_thresholds:
    cost_rate_increase_pct: 150
    error_budget_breach_minutes: 5
----

Architecture and interactions (high level)
- The Adapter is composed of: a lightweight policy engine (control plane), a runtime prefetch worker (sidecar or centralized service depending on deployment mode), and a Console-facing cost and SLO dashboard.
- Interacts with: Routing service (to receive route metrics and be consulted), Serving runtime (to call prefetch endpoints that populate KV/model state), Billing (to estimate and report prefetch token charges), and Optimize (for policy suggestions).

Rollout plan and milestones
1) Q2 2026 — Design & spike: deliver design-spec, prototype prefetch worker, unit tests, and local lab benchmarks.
2) Q3 2026 — Internal lab validation: integrate with synthetic traffic harness and Optimize cost simulator; iterate policy defaults.
3) Q4 2026 — Canary: deploy to limited enterprise customers with opt-in flag; run 4-week canary and collect telemetry.
4) Q1 2027 — GA (gradual rollout): default conservative policy enabled for paid plans with opt-out. Console UX: cost preview and manual controls.

Telemetry, dashboards and alerts
- Required metrics (per-route, per-tenant): prefetch_ops_total, prefetch_success_ratio, prefetched_tokens, incremental_cost_estimate, p50/p95/p99 latency delta vs baseline, circuit_breaker_tripped_total.
- Dashboards: Prefetch Overview (global), Tenant Prefetch Summary, Route Risk Index heatmap.
- Alerts: circuit-breaker-trips > 3 in 15m for a tenant, prefetched_tokens rate > 2x baseline, incremental_cost_estimate > configured cap.

Operational playbook (runbook excerpts)
1) Incident: sudden spike in prefetched_tokens and tenant cost complaints:
   1.1) Check Tenant Prefetch Summary -> circuit-breaker state.
   1.2) If breaker active, open CS ticket and notify SE.
   1.3) If unchecked, manually toggle tenant prefetch policy to conservative and engage the prefetch backoff API.
2) Rollback: revert policy defaults in control plane and escalate to runtime team if root cause is a model-variant regression.

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: Prefetch may waste tokens and increase customer bills. Mitigation: conservative defaults, explicit caps, prefetch cost preview in Console and a 14-day grace period for GA customers.
- Risk: Prefetch interacts poorly with eviction semantics causing stale KV. Mitigation: align TTLs with serving runtime and include an eviction validation test in QA.

Dependencies
- Serving runtime prefetch API (runtime-bridge) — infra team
- Billing hooks for prefetch attribution — finance/engineers
- Console cost preview UX — developer experience team
- Policy suggestion integration with Optimize — Optimize team

Ownership and stakeholders
- Product owner: Maya Chen (product)
- Engineering leads: Ethan Park (runtime), Leah Goldberg (control plane)
- SRE: Ravi Malhotra
- DevEx: Nina Ortega

Appendix: experiment ideas
- A/B variant: prefetching vs reactive warmup for large completions; measure token cost vs p99 tail latency.
- Calendar-aware prefetch: allow customers to upload events (product launches) and simulate warmups ahead of time.

Related docs
- See design-spec: /confluence/product-docs/design-specs/hybrid-cache-coordinator-design-spec-2029-06-15 (coordination patterns)
- See requirements: /confluence/product-docs/requirements/predictive-warmup-coldstart-economics-playbook-2027-03-10 (cost experiments)

Contact:
- Primary: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood-inference.internal)
- Reviewers: Ethan Park, Leah Goldberg

Document history:
- 2026-05-11: initial draft
- 2026-08-02: added acceptance criteria and canary thresholds after lab validation
- 2026-11-02: updated rollout dates and added Console UX requirements
