Acceptance criteria (tests and metrics)
1) Lab test: synthetic traffic generator with burst profiles (1s ramp to 10x RPS for 60s) demonstrates >=30% reduction in p95 latency for SLO-tagged routes when adapter enabled vs disabled, while incremental token cost remains below 3%.
2) Canary: Run a 2-week canary with 20 enterprise tenants. Success = no more than 0.5% of canary tenants exceeding their prefetch cost cap and at least 20% of tenants seeing p95 improvement >=25%.
3) Production rollback test: Simulate runaway prefetch by injecting a spike in synthetic sessions; automated circuit-breaker must trigger within 2 minutes and restore normal behavior within 5 minutes of error detection.

SLO mappings and policy defaults
| SLO profile | Default prefetch aggressiveness | Cost cap (monthly) | Notes |
|------------|-------------------------------:|-------------------:|-------|
| Latency-critical (p95 < 100ms) | High (lookahead 64, short-warm model) | 5% | Enterprise default |
| Performance (p95 < 200ms) | Medium (lookahead 32) | 3% | Default for paid plans |
| Cost-sensitive (no strict latency) | Low (lookahead 8) | 1% | Free tier and small customers |

Configuration example (runtime YAML)
----
prefetch_policy:
  route_selector: "/api/v1/chat/*"
  slo_target_p95_ms: 120
  aggressiveness: medium
  daily_token_cap: 500000
  lookahead_kv_tokens: 32
  fallback_variant: "small-quant-v1"
  circuit_breaker_thresholds:
    cost_rate_increase_pct: 150
    error_budget_breach_minutes: 5
----

Architecture and interactions (high level)
- The Adapter is composed of: a lightweight policy engine (control plane), a runtime prefetch worker (sidecar or centralized service depending on deployment mode), and a Console-facing cost and SLO dashboard.
- Interacts with: Routing service (to receive route metrics and be consulted), Serving runtime (to call prefetch endpoints that populate KV/model state), Billing (to estimate and report prefetch token charges), and Optimize (for policy suggestions).

Rollout plan and milestones
1) Q2 2026 — Design & spike: deliver design-spec, prototype prefetch worker, unit tests, and local lab benchmarks.
2) Q3 2026 — Internal lab validation: integrate with synthetic traffic harness and Optimize cost simulator; iterate policy defaults.
3) Q4 2026 — Canary: deploy to limited enterprise customers with opt-in flag; run 4-week canary and collect telemetry.
4) Q1 2027 — GA (gradual rollout): default conservative policy enabled for paid plans with opt-out. Console UX: cost preview and manual controls.

Telemetry, dashboards and alerts
- Required metrics (per-route, per-tenant): prefetch_ops_total, prefetch_success_ratio, prefetched_tokens, incremental_cost_estimate, p50/p95/p99 latency delta vs baseline, circuit_breaker_tripped_total.
- Dashboards: Prefetch Overview (global), Tenant Prefetch Summary, Route Risk Index heatmap.
- Alerts: circuit-breaker-trips > 3 in 15m for a tenant, prefetched_tokens rate > 2x baseline, incremental_cost_estimate > configured cap.

Operational playbook (runbook excerpts)
1) Incident: sudden spike in prefetched_tokens and tenant cost complaints:
   1.1) Check Tenant Prefetch Summary -> circuit-breaker state.
   1.2) If breaker active, open CS ticket and notify SE.
   1.3) If unchecked, manually toggle tenant prefetch policy to conservative and engage the prefetch backoff API.
2) Rollback: revert policy defaults in control plane and escalate to runtime team if root cause is a model-variant regression.

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: Prefetch may waste tokens and increase customer bills. Mitigation: conservative defaults, explicit caps, prefetch cost preview in Console and a 14-day grace period for GA customers.
- Risk: Prefetch interacts poorly with eviction semantics causing stale KV. Mitigation: align TTLs with serving runtime and include an eviction validation test in QA.

Dependencies
- Serving runtime prefetch API (runtime-bridge) — infra team
- Billing hooks for prefetch attribution — finance/engineers
- Console cost preview UX — developer experience team
- Policy suggestion integration with Optimize — Optimize team

Ownership and stakeholders
- Product owner: Maya Chen (product)
- Engineering leads: Ethan Park (runtime), Leah Goldberg (control plane)
- SRE: Ravi Malhotra
- DevEx: Nina Ortega

Appendix: experiment ideas
- A/B variant: prefetching vs reactive warmup for large completions; measure token cost vs p99 tail latency.
- Calendar-aware prefetch: allow customers to upload events (product launches) and simulate warmups ahead of time.

Related docs
- See design-spec: /confluence/product-docs/design-specs/hybrid-cache-coordinator-design-spec-2029-06-15 (coordination patterns)
- See requirements: /confluence/product-docs/requirements/predictive-warmup-coldstart-economics-playbook-2027-03-10 (cost experiments)

Contact:
- Primary: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood-inference.internal)
- Reviewers: Ethan Park, Leah Goldberg

Document history:
- 2026-05-11: initial draft
- 2026-08-02: added acceptance criteria and canary thresholds after lab validation
- 2026-11-02: updated rollout dates and added Console UX requirements

title: Adaptive Inference Feedback Loop - Design Spec
preview:
Overview

This design specification describes the Adaptive Inference Feedback Loop (AIFL), a runtime-to-control-plane closed loop that continuously observes production signals, evaluates fidelity and cost/latency tradeoffs, and proposes or applies configuration updates to the serving runtime. The goal is to improve unit economics while preserving or improving customer-facing quality and latency SLOs.

Goals
- Reduce per-token cost by 10–30% on workloads with stable patterns through automated batching, local-prefetch adjustments, and selective quantization fallbacks.
- Prevent customer-facing quality regressions: automatic changes must not increase the evaluation regression score (see Quality Profiler) beyond configurable thresholds (default: 0.5% absolute for high-importance prompt sets).
- Honor latency SLOs: p95 latency must remain below tenant-configured SLO (default 300 ms for interactive routes).
- Provide transparent audit trail and human-in-the-loop approvals for risky changes.

Scope
- Included: Hosted and Dedicated runtime variants; model-variant switching for compatible variants (e.g., q8 -> q4, fp16 -> q8); batching and batching-window tuning; kv-prefetch heuristics; dynamic routing to fallback variants/regions.
- Excluded (v1): on-prem private deployments where customers manage their own control plane (support planned in v2).

High-level Architecture
- Signal Collector (redwood-signal): centralized pipeline that ingests telemetry (latency, tokens, errors), lightweight eval captures (loss/accuracy metrics), and labeled feedback (customer NPS, developer triage).
- Quality Profiler (quality-profiler): batch offline evals and lightweight online checks against golden prompt sets; computes rolling quality delta per model-variant and route.
- Decision Engine (fidelity-decider): policy engine that consumes signals, applies rules/ML policy, and emits Candidate Actions (e.g., change-quantization, increase-batch-size, route-to-variant, enable-prefetch).
- Control Plane API (aifl-control): receives Candidate Actions, applies governance checks (SLO guardrails, impact simulation), and either auto-applies or schedules a canary rollout via the Runtime Agent.
- Runtime Agent (rt-agent): per-cluster agent that performs local validation, applies config changes, and reports results back to the Signal Collector.
- Simulation Sandbox (aifl-sim): offline simulator that estimates cost/latency/quality impact of actions using historical traces.

Data flows and APIs
1) Telemetry -> redwood-signal: protobuf stream with fields: trace_id, tenant_id, route, model_variant, seq_len, tokens, latency_ms_p50/p95/p99, error_types[], eval_metrics{prompt_set_id:loss,accuracy}, feedback_tags[].
2) Quality Profiler pulls recent eval captures every 30m and computes delta_vs_baseline.
3) fidelity-decider has a REST API: POST /v1/policies/evaluate with body {"tenant_id":"...","route":"...","window_minutes":30}. It returns list of actions: [{"action_id":"...","type":"change_quant","variant":"gptx-q4_8bit","expected_cost_delta":"-0.12","expected_p95_delta_ms":"+10","quality_risk":"0.3"}].
4) Control plane validates action via /v1/simulate and then /v1/apply (which may be auto or manual).

Decision rules and policy examples
- Rule: If p95 > SLO and model_variant has higher-latency kernels enabled, suggest reducing batch-window to next lower tier.
- Rule: If rolling quality delta > 0.5% (regression) for critical prompt set -> block any automatic quantization changes; create an incident and notify ML-Owners.
- Rule: If cost per 1000 tokens for a route exceeds tenant_budget_threshold and quality_risk <= 0.2 and p95 delta <= 15ms -> propose switching to cheaper variant with canary 5% traffic for 2 hours.

Config table (primary tunable parameters)
- batch_window_ms: default 15, allowed 1-200, impact: latency vs throughput.
- batching_enabled: default true, toggle route level.
- quant_profile: default fp16, alternatives q8/q4 with calibrated quality scores.
- prefetch_enabled: default false, warm_prefetch_count: default 4.
- canary_fraction: default 0.05 (5%), canary_duration_minutes: default 120.

Example candidate action (JSON)
{
  "action_id": "aifl-20261102-0453",
  "tenant_id": "tenant-123",
  "route": "/v1/chat/completions",
  "type": "change_quant",
  "from": "fp16-gptx",
  "to": "q8-gptx",
  "expected_cost_delta": "-0.18",
  "expected_p95_delta_ms": "+12",
  "quality_risk": "0.25"
}

Governance and Guardrails
- SLO guard: any action that predicts p95 increase > tenant_p95_slo_margin (default 20%) is blocked.
- Quality guard: per-tenant critical prompt sets (customer-supplied or Redwood-managed) have a max-acceptable regression threshold. Auto actions that exceed it are blocked.
- Audit trail: every Candidate Action persisted in change-log with before/after configs and simulation outputs. Immutable event id and signed by control plane.
- Human-in-the-loop: for actions with quality_risk > 0.4 or cost benefit < $0.05/1k tokens, require a product or ML owner approval.

Rollout plan (v1)
1) Dev & internal canary (2 weeks): run AIFL in observe-only mode for internal tenants; surface suggested actions in Console UI.
2) Selected beta customers (4–6 weeks): opt-in tenants, actions run as canary (default 1% traffic) with an automatic rollback policy.
3) Broader developer beta (8–12 weeks): expand to Dedicated customers with agreed budget floors.
4) GA: controls in Console + API for opt-in/opt-out and per-route guardrails.

Telemetry, SLOs, and metrics to monitor
- Key metrics: action_accept_rate, rollback_rate, cost_savings_realized ($/1k tokens), p95_change_after_action, quality_regression_events.
- SLO targets for v1: rollback_rate < 2% across all auto-applied actions; false-positive-quality-block < 1%.
- Dashboards: per-tenant and global views in Console under Observability > Adaptive Inference.

Operational runbook (high level)
1) If rollback_rate > 5% for any tenant in 1h: automatically pause auto-apply for that tenant and page oncall SRE and ML Owner.
2) If quality_profiler reports regression > 1% on critical prompt set: open an incident and run replay analysis in aifl-sim.
3) Manual remediation steps: revert the last applied change_id via aifl-control /v1/revert, and run targeted canary with adjusted thresholds.

Security and Compliance notes
- All action proposals and simulation outputs are tagged with tenant_id and stored in audit-logs (retention 1 year by default).
- Sensitive prompt data is redacted; only hashed prompt identifiers are sent to Quality Profiler.
- For regulated tenants (confidentiality=restricted), auto-apply is disabled and suggested actions require tenant signed approval.

Pricing and packaging implications
- Feature will be an add-on to Redwood Optimize: "Adaptive Inference" as a subscription SKU for Dedicated and Hosted.
- Pricing model options (to be decided): revenue share on realized savings (e.g., 20% of quantifiable savings), flat monthly fee, or per-tenant seat-based fee.
- Billing telemetry: cost_savings_realized is tracked and reported; any revenue-share billing requires double-confirmed audit logs.

Ownership and milestones
- Product Owner: Maya Thompson
- Technical Lead: Diego Morales
- ML Owner: Ava Chen
- SRE Lead: Samir Patel
- Milestone calendar: prototype (Q4 2026), internal canary (Q1 2027), beta (Q2 2027), GA (Q3 2027).

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: Over-aggressive automation causes customer-visible regressions. Mitigation: conservative default thresholds, mandatory human approvals above risk bands, long beta.
- Risk: Simulator mismatch underestimates latency impact. Mitigation: holdback percentage for canaries, continuous calibration against real traffic.
- Risk: Data privacy concerns from eval capture. Mitigation: hashing/redaction and restricted-mode for regulated tenants.

Open questions (to be resolved before beta)
- Pricing model selection and legal implications for revenue-share.
- Exact thresholds for auto-apply vs require-approval (propose a cross-functional working group).
- Support for Private (on-prem) deployments: timeline and required customer agents.

Appendices
- Appendix A: Simulation inputs and typical trace fields (listed earlier).
- Appendix B: Example Console UI wireframe (textual): Observability > Adaptive Inference: Suggested actions table with columns [time, tenant, route, action_type, expected_cost_delta, expected_p95_delta_ms, quality_risk, status, apply_button].
- Appendix C: CLI snippets
  - List outstanding candidate actions: redwoodctl aifl list --tenant tenant-123 --status pending
  - Apply a candidate action manually: redwoodctl aifl apply --action-id aifl-20261102-0453 --approve

Related pages and references
- PRD: prd-cost-simulator-and-visibility-ux-2026-08-01 (analysis and simulator baseline)
- Design: local-prefetch-proxy-design-spec-2026-10-04 (prefetch coordination)
- Observability: consumption-telemetry-provenance-and-crediting-spec-2029-12-05

Summary
The Adaptive Inference Feedback Loop is a disciplined, instrumented approach to tightening the loop between production signal and automated runtime configuration. v1 focuses on conservative, auditable changes (batching, quantization, prefetch) with strong quality guardrails and human approvals for higher-risk actions. The aim is measurable cost savings with predictable SLO adherence and clear governance for customers and Redwood teams.

title: Service-tier surge scenario playbook and launch checklist
preview:
Summary
-------
This playbook defines policies, automated controls, and an operational checklist for handling surge events that impact Redwood service tiers (Developer, Standard, Reserved). It focuses on minimizing customer-visible failures, preserving SLOs, and making predictable commercial decisions (credits, throttles, and fallback). This document combines product requirements, rollout checklist, runbook actions, and pricing impact guidelines for engineering, product, SRE, and GTM teams.

Goals
-----
- Maintain 99th percentile latency SLOs for each tier when possible.
- Provide deterministic fallback behavior (variant, fidelity, region) within 200ms decision latency.
- Ensure cost/backstop actions are transparent and reversible.
- Reduce manual escalation during surges through automation and clear ownership.

Scope and definitions
---------------------
- Surge event: a tenant- or system-wide increase in request volume or token rate that causes capacity pressure for >2 minutes.
- Service tiers: Developer (best-effort), Standard (SLO-targeted), Reserved (dedicated capacity + SLA).
- Fidelity fallback: reducing model variant or quantization level to preserve throughput.
- Credit event: a post-facto customer credit to compensate SLA violations.

Signals and thresholds (product requirements)
-------------------------------------------
- Per-route call rate (requests/sec) sustained > 3x baseline for 2 minutes triggers tier evaluation.
- Aggregate token-per-second (TPS) across a host pool exceeding safe_utilization_threshold (75%) for >30s triggers autoscaling and pre-fallback checks.
- Error rate > 1% (5xx or model timeouts) for >60s escalates to incident path.
- Latency p99 surpassing tier SLO + 50ms for 3 minutes triggers automated fidelity fallback.

Configuration table (example values)
-----------------------------------
| parameter | Developer | Standard | Reserved |
|-----------|-----------|----------|----------|
| p99 latency SLO | 600ms | 200ms | 100ms |
| safe_utilization_threshold | 80% | 75% | 70% |
| max-fallback-depth | 2 variants | 1 variant | 0 variants |
| auto-scale cooldown | 45s | 30s | 15s |
| burst-credit-policy | none | 10% credits/day | contractual |

Automated decision flow (high level)
----------------------------------
1) Detect surge via telemetry (ingest from runtime observability).
2) Apply local autoscaling (policy per pool). If scaling meets demand within 90s, action = monitor.
3) If scaling insufficient and p99 > threshold, trigger graded fallback: Developer -> aggressive quantized variant; Standard -> single-step usage of fallback variant + token batching policy; Reserved -> regional failover only.
4) If error-rate remains > 1% after fallback, route to degraded-mode: enqueue noninteractive requests, mark route degraded, notify SRE and account team.
5) If SLA breach confirmed, open credit review workflow.

Operational runbook (step-by-step)
--------------------------------
1. Detection and initial triage (oncall SRE)
   - Action: Validate alerts (console dashboards + /api/v1/health-code).
   - Command: curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $OP_TOKEN" https://console.redwood.internal/api/v1/pools/status | jq '.pools[] | select(.util>0.75)'
   - Owner: SRE team.
2. Engage autoscaling (automated by control plane)
   - Verify: autoscaler events in last 2 minutes.
   - Command: rwctl autoscaler status --pool <pool-id> --last 120s
   - If autoscaler failed: escalate to infra-oncall (Slack: #infra-oncall).
3. Apply fidelity fallback (product-controlled)
   - Trigger: product toggle for route-level fallback ("/api/v1/config/routes/<route>/fallback").
   - Example payload (curl):
     curl -X PATCH https://operator.redwood.internal/api/v1/routes/route-id -H "Authorization: Bearer $OP_TOKEN" -d '{"fallback":"quantized-x4"}'
   - Owner: runtime/product oncall.
4. Controlled throttles and enqueueing
   - Use rate-limit policy: route->policies->burst_throttle = 100r/s default, reduce to 50r/s.
   - Owner: SRE + product.
5. Customer communication and crediting
   - Trigger: Confirmed SLO breach for Standard or Reserved for >60 minutes.
   - Notify: Customer success to draft status update using template: "We detected increased latency affecting X between T1 and T2; actions taken: autoscale, fallback; next steps..."
   - Credit initiation: file ticket to billing with incident id, impact window, and credit calculation (see pricing section).

Escalation matrix and owners
---------------------------
- Stage 0 (automated): runtime control plane (autoscaler/fallback). Owner: runtime-engineering.
- Stage 1 (oncall SRE): verify and execute manual mitigation. Owner: eng-sre.
- Stage 2 (product + account): route prioritization, customer comms, credit decision. Owner: product + customer-success.
- Stage 3 (legal + finance): contractual Reserved SLA breaches. Owner: finance-legal.

Pricing and credit guidance (product & billing)
--------------------------------------------
- Developer tier: no SLA credits. For widespread developer tier disruption, offer goodwill credits (manual approval by customer-success).
- Standard tier: automated credit slab based on severity tier:
  - Severity A (SLO miss 1–4 hours): 5% of daily token spend for impacted route.
  - Severity B (SLO miss 4–12 hours): 15% of daily token spend.
  - Severity C (>12 hours): case-by-case escalation to finance.
- Reserved tier: refer to contract; default action: activate contractual credit pipeline and schedule post-incident RCA within 3 business days.

Credit calculation example (Standard)
----------------------------------
- Impact window: 2026-10-12 12:00 -> 2026-10-12 16:00 (4h).
- Daily token spend for route: $2000/day.
- Severity B credit = 15% of daily = $300.

Rollout checklist (product + eng)
--------------------------------
Pre-launch (2 weeks out):
- [ ] Finalize thresholds with runtime-engineering and SRE.
- [ ] Implement telemetry alerts and dashboard widgets (p99, util, errors).
- [ ] Add fallback variants to model-variant registry and test in canary.
- [ ] Update API docs for route-level fallback controls.
- [ ] Create customer communication templates (developer, standard, reserved).

Launch day:
- [ ] Enable playbook in staging, run simulated surge test (see test plan).
- [ ] Verify autoscaler cooldown and fallback execution under load.
- [ ] Confirm billing automation can create credit tickets.
- [ ] Inform sales and customer-success of behavior and sample messaging.

Post-launch (30 days):
- [ ] Review actual surge incidents and tune thresholds.
- [ ] Run tabletop incident with product, SRE, CS teams.
- [ ] Decide on conservative defaults for next quarter.

Testing and simulation plan
--------------------------
- Synthetic load generator: use loadrunner-redwood (docker image rw/loadrunner:2026-09) to replay token patterns.
- Test scenarios: gradual ramp x3, spike x10, regional outage with traffic shift.
- Acceptance criteria: autoscaler recovers within 120s for Standard; fallback triggers and reduces p99 by >=20%.

Telemetry and dashboards (observability requirements)
--------------------------------------------------
Required panels:
- Per-pool utilization heatmap (1s granularity).
- Per-route p50/p95/p99 + error-rate trend with annotated fallback events.
- Autoscaler events timeline correlated with instance churn.
- Cost delta panel (estimated $/min) while fallback is active.
Retention and alerting: 30d high-resolution for troubleshooting; 365d aggregated for post-incident review.

Documentation and API surface (developer-facing)
---------------------------------------------
- Endpoints:
  - GET /v1/routes/<route>/status -- returns current fallback/limit state.
  - PATCH /v1/routes/<route>/config -- set fallback policy (product-only).
  - POST /v1/incidents/surge -- manual incident trigger (GTM/CS).
- Example SDK snippet (python):
  from redwood import RoutesClient
  rc = RoutesClient(api_key=REDWOOD_KEY)
  rc.update_config(route_id, {"fallback":"quantized-x2", "burst_throttle":50})

Open questions and decisions (to be resolved by Q4 2026)
-----------------------------------------------------
- Should Developer tier receive automated soft-fallbacks to preserve platform stability? (tradeoff: customer surprise).
- Define exact credit percentages for cross-tenant incidents vs single-tenant incidents.
- Whether to surface real-time remaining burst credits to customers in Console.

Appendix A — sample incident timeline
-----------------------------------
- 10:01: alert: pool util 82% (sustained 45s)
- 10:02: autoscaler invoked (failed to allocate GPU due to quota)
- 10:03: product toggle applied: Standard routes -> fallback quantized-x1
- 10:04: p99 reduces from 320ms -> 220ms, errors steady at 0.2%
- 10:10: provisioned additional capacity, fallback removed at 10:12
- Post-incident: credit ticket created, customer-success notified, RCA scheduled.

Contacts and ownership
----------------------
- Product owner: Priya Nair (product)
- Runtime engineering: Marco Alvarez (runtime)
- SRE oncall rota: eng-sre@redwood.internal
- Customer Success lead: Sana Iqbal
- Billing contact: billing-ops@redwood.internal

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-10-10 — initial draft by Priya Nair.
- 2026-10-15 — incorporated SRE thresholds and autoscaler notes (Marco Alvarez).
- 2026-10-21 — finalized credit slabs and launch checklist; published.
text:
Summary
-------
This playbook defines policies, automated controls, and an operational checklist for handling surge events that impact Redwood service tiers (Developer, Standard, Reserved). It focuses on minimizing customer-visible failures, preserving SLOs, and making predictable commercial decisions (credits, throttles, and fallback). This document combines product requirements, rollout checklist, runbook actions, and pricing impact guidelines for engineering, product, SRE, and GTM teams.

Goals
-----
- Maintain 99th percentile latency SLOs for each tier when possible.
- Provide deterministic fallback behavior (variant, fidelity, region) within 200ms decision latency.
- Ensure cost/backstop actions are transparent and reversible.
- Reduce manual escalation during surges through automation and clear ownership.

Scope and definitions
---------------------
- Surge event: a tenant- or system-wide increase in request volume or token rate that causes capacity pressure for >2 minutes.
- Service tiers: Developer (best-effort), Standard (SLO-targeted), Reserved (dedicated capacity + SLA).
- Fidelity fallback: reducing model variant or quantization level to preserve throughput.
- Credit event: a post-facto customer credit to compensate SLA violations.

Signals and thresholds (product requirements)
-------------------------------------------
- Per-route call rate (requests/sec) sustained > 3x baseline for 2 minutes triggers tier evaluation.
- Aggregate token-per-second (TPS) across a host pool exceeding safe_utilization_threshold (75%) for >30s triggers autoscaling and pre-fallback checks.
- Error rate > 1% (5xx or model timeouts) for >60s escalates to incident path.
- Latency p99 surpassing tier SLO + 50ms for 3 minutes triggers automated fidelity fallback.

Configuration table (example values)
-----------------------------------
| parameter | Developer | Standard | Reserved |
|-----------|-----------|----------|----------|
| p99 latency SLO | 600ms | 200ms | 100ms |
| safe_utilization_threshold | 80% | 75% | 70% |
| max-fallback-depth | 2 variants | 1 variant | 0 variants |
| auto-scale cooldown | 45s | 30s | 15s |
| burst-credit-policy | none | 10% credits/day | contractual |

Automated decision flow (high level)
----------------------------------
1) Detect surge via telemetry (ingest from runtime observability).
2) Apply local autoscaling (policy per pool). If scaling meets demand within 90s, action = monitor.
3) If scaling insufficient and p99 > threshold, trigger graded fallback: Developer -> aggressive quantized variant; Standard -> single-step usage of fallback variant + token batching policy; Reserved -> regional failover only.
4) If error-rate remains > 1% after fallback, route to degraded-mode: enqueue noninteractive requests, mark route degraded, notify SRE and account team.
5) If SLA breach confirmed, open credit review workflow.

Operational runbook (step-by-step)
--------------------------------
1. Detection and initial triage (oncall SRE)
   - Action: Validate alerts (console dashboards + /api/v1/health-code).
   - Command: curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $OP_TOKEN" https://console.redwood.internal/api/v1/pools/status | jq '.pools[] | select(.util>0.75)'
   - Owner: SRE team.
2. Engage autoscaling (automated by control plane)
   - Verify: autoscaler events in last 2 minutes.
   - Command: rwctl autoscaler status --pool <pool-id> --last 120s
   - If autoscaler failed: escalate to infra-oncall (Slack: #infra-oncall).
3. Apply fidelity fallback (product-controlled)
   - Trigger: product toggle for route-level fallback ("/api/v1/config/routes/<route>/fallback").
   - Example payload (curl):
     curl -X PATCH https://operator.redwood.internal/api/v1/routes/route-id -H "Authorization: Bearer $OP_TOKEN" -d '{"fallback":"quantized-x4"}'
   - Owner: runtime/product oncall.
4. Controlled throttles and enqueueing
   - Use rate-limit policy: route->policies->burst_throttle = 100r/s default, reduce to 50r/s.
   - Owner: SRE + product.
5. Customer communication and crediting
   - Trigger: Confirmed SLO breach for Standard or Reserved for >60 minutes.
   - Notify: Customer success to draft status update using template: "We detected increased latency affecting X between T1 and T2; actions taken: autoscale, fallback; next steps..."
   - Credit initiation: file ticket to billing with incident id, impact window, and credit calculation (see pricing section).

Escalation matrix and owners
---------------------------
- Stage 0 (automated): runtime control plane (autoscaler/fallback). Owner: runtime-engineering.
- Stage 1 (oncall SRE): verify and execute manual mitigation. Owner: eng-sre.
- Stage 2 (product + account): route prioritization, customer comms, credit decision. Owner: product + customer-success.
- Stage 3 (legal + finance): contractual Reserved SLA breaches. Owner: finance-legal.

Pricing and credit guidance (product & billing)
--------------------------------------------
- Developer tier: no SLA credits. For widespread developer tier disruption, offer goodwill credits (manual approval by customer-success).
- Standard tier: automated credit slab based on severity tier:
  - Severity A (SLO miss 1–4 hours): 5% of daily token spend for impacted route.
  - Severity B (SLO miss 4–12 hours): 15% of daily token spend.
  - Severity C (>12 hours): case-by-case escalation to finance.
- Reserved tier: refer to contract; default action: activate contractual credit pipeline and schedule post-incident RCA within 3 business days.

Credit calculation example (Standard)
----------------------------------
- Impact window: 2026-10-12 12:00 -> 2026-10-12 16:00 (4h).
- Daily token spend for route: $2000/day.
- Severity B credit = 15% of daily = $300.

Rollout checklist (product + eng)
--------------------------------
Pre-launch (2 weeks out):
- [ ] Finalize thresholds with runtime-engineering and SRE.
- [ ] Implement telemetry alerts and dashboard widgets (p99, util, errors).
- [ ] Add fallback variants to model-variant registry and test in canary.
- [ ] Update API docs for route-level fallback controls.
- [ ] Create customer communication templates (developer, standard, reserved).

Launch day:
- [ ] Enable playbook in staging, run simulated surge test (see test plan).
- [ ] Verify autoscaler cooldown and fallback execution under load.
- [ ] Confirm billing automation can create credit tickets.
- [ ] Inform sales and customer-success of behavior and sample messaging.

Post-launch (30 days):
- [ ] Review actual surge incidents and tune thresholds.
- [ ] Run tabletop incident with product, SRE, CS teams.
- [ ] Decide on conservative defaults for next quarter.

Testing and simulation plan
--------------------------
- Synthetic load generator: use loadrunner-redwood (docker image rw/loadrunner:2026-09) to replay token patterns.
- Test scenarios: gradual ramp x3, spike x10, regional outage with traffic shift.
- Acceptance criteria: autoscaler recovers within 120s for Standard; fallback triggers and reduces p99 by >=20%.

Telemetry and dashboards (observability requirements)
--------------------------------------------------
Required panels:
- Per-pool utilization heatmap (1s granularity).
- Per-route p50/p95/p99 + error-rate trend with annotated fallback events.
- Autoscaler events timeline correlated with instance churn.
- Cost delta panel (estimated $/min) while fallback is active.
Retention and alerting: 30d high-resolution for troubleshooting; 365d aggregated for post-incident review.

Documentation and API surface (developer-facing)
---------------------------------------------
- Endpoints:
  - GET /v1/routes/<route>/status -- returns current fallback/limit state.
  - PATCH /v1/routes/<route>/config -- set fallback policy (product-only).
  - POST /v1/incidents/surge -- manual incident trigger (GTM/CS).
- Example SDK snippet (python):
  from redwood import RoutesClient
  rc = RoutesClient(api_key=REDWOOD_KEY)
  rc.update_config(route_id, {"fallback":"quantized-x2", "burst_throttle":50})

Open questions and decisions (to be resolved by Q4 2026)
-----------------------------------------------------
- Should Developer tier receive automated soft-fallbacks to preserve platform stability? (tradeoff: customer surprise).
- Define exact credit percentages for cross-tenant incidents vs single-tenant incidents.
- Whether to surface real-time remaining burst credits to customers in Console.

Appendix A — sample incident timeline
-----------------------------------
- 10:01: alert: pool util 82% (sustained 45s)
- 10:02: autoscaler invoked (failed to allocate GPU due to quota)
- 10:03: product toggle applied: Standard routes -> fallback quantized-x1
- 10:04: p99 reduces from 320ms -> 220ms, errors steady at 0.2%
- 10:10: provisioned additional capacity, fallback removed at 10:12
- Post-incident: credit ticket created, customer-success notified, RCA scheduled.

Contacts and ownership
----------------------
- Product owner: Priya Nair (product)
- Runtime engineering: Marco Alvarez (runtime)
- SRE oncall rota: eng-sre@redwood.internal
- Customer Success lead: Sana Iqbal
- Billing contact: billing-ops@redwood.internal

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-10-10 — initial draft by Priya Nair.
- 2026-10-15 — incorporated SRE thresholds and autoscaler notes (Marco Alvarez).
- 2026-10-21 — finalized credit slabs and launch checklist; published.

title: Fidelity Scaling Advisor — product brief and rollout playbook
preview:
Overview

This document describes the Fidelity Scaling Advisor (FSA): a product brief, high-level requirements, design constraints, pricing & packaging guidance, rollout plan, and initial release notes. FSA is an in-product advisor that helps customers choose and automatically apply fidelity (model variant, quantization, batching, and prefix-caching) and consumption settings to meet target cost, latency, or quality objectives. FSA is intended to complement Redwood Optimize and the Console recommendations by providing an opinionated, lightweight decision service surfaced at the point of consumption.

Goals

- Help customers systematically trade cost, latency, and output fidelity with minimal manual tuning.
- Reduce per-token spend for common production workloads by 15–40% without measurable quality regressions for supported workloads.
- Provide transparent decision rationale (why a candidate configuration was chosen) and rollback if customer-visible quality dips are detected.
- Integrate with existing telemetry, rollout, and billing systems to enable SLO-aligned pricing experiments.

Scope and non-goals

Included:
- Per-route fidelity recommendations (default, conservative, aggressive) surfaced in Console and API/SDK.
- An auto-apply mode (opt-in) that adjusts fidelity within configured SLO limits.
- Explanations and simple A/B experiment primitives for customers.
- Telemetry metrics for cost, latency, and eval-driven quality signals.

Excluded (initial release):
- Large-scale multi-tenant auctioning of capacity.
- Complex per-customer ML retraining of fidelity predictors.

Audience

Primary: Platform/product engineers and ML engineers who manage production inference costs and quality. Secondary: Solutions engineers and customer success for onboarding higher-touch customers.

Product primitives and UX

1) Console card and route-level toggles
- Route settings: default fidelity profile, target objective (min-cost | target-latency | fixed-quality), and guardrail thresholds (max-latency-ms, min-similarity-score).
- Recommendation card: "Fidelity Scaling Advisor recommends: use variant v2-q8 + prefix-cache=enabled to save ~28% cost at +2% simulated quality delta."

2) API/SDK: on-request hints and auto-apply mode
- New optional request field: fidelity_hint: {"mode":"advisor-suggest"} (example shown in SDK docs).
- Auto-apply mode: customers opt-in per-route to allow the advisor to change runtime config subject to configured SLOs.

3) Explainability and audit logs
- Every advisor decision is logged with a short rationale string and reference to the evaluation prompt-set used for simulation.
- Console exposes a timeline of applied changes and detected regressions for the last 90 days.

High-level requirements (must-have)

- RQ-01: Recommendation latency <= 250ms for synchronous decisions; <= 2s for batched offline simulations.
- RQ-02: Decision space includes model variant, quantization profile, batch size suggestion, and prefix/KV cache usage.
- RQ-03: Advisor must provide a confidence score in range [0.0, 1.0] and a short rationale string <= 280 chars.
- RQ-04: Auto-apply must be reversible within 5 minutes and provide safety rollbacks when quality regressions cross thresholds.
- RQ-05: All recommendations must be linked to simulated cost and quality delta estimates based on customer prompt-set or default benchmark sets.

Design constraints and architecture notes

- Decision service is implemented as a stateless microservice that calls the Optimize scoring engine and the runtime capacity/variant catalog. It must cache recent route profiles and prompt-set embeddings for simulation.
- For privacy, prompt-sets and evaluation traces used for simulation must follow KMS and data-retention policies: ephemeral simulation traces discarded in 30 days unless customer explicitly retains.
- The service will rely on the existing model performance profiles stored in the model catalog (latency curves and token-cost estimates) to compute delta estimates.

Decision flow (simplified)

1. Customer creates route and attaches a prompt-set (optional) or picks default.
2. FSA pulls route telemetry (latency, tokens/predictions distribution), and runs a batch simulation across candidate configurations.
3. Generate ranked recommendations with cost/latency/quality deltas and confidence.
4. Present in Console and allow user to accept, schedule, or enable auto-apply.

Telemetry and success metrics

- Primary metrics: reduction in tokens-per-response cost (median across adopters), fraction of routes with >15% cost reduction, number of auto-apply-enabled routes.
- Safety metrics: % of routes with quality regression > 5% (measured via eval prompt-sets), # of automatic rollbacks triggered, mean time-to-rollback.
- Observability: decision latency, simulation job failures, recommendation acceptance rate.

SLOs and thresholds

| Metric | Target | Alert threshold | Owner |
| --- | ---: | --- | --- |
| Recommendation latency (p95) | <= 250ms | > 500ms | FSA team |
| Auto-apply rollback time | <= 5m | > 15m | Eng SRE |
| Simulation job failure rate | < 1% | > 3% | Eng Platform |

Data inputs and configuration table

- Route config fields (Console + API):
  - fidelity_profile (string): name of recommended profile (e.g., "conservative", "balanced", "aggressive").
  - objective (enum): ["min-cost","target-latency","fixed-quality"].
  - guardrails (json string): {"max_latency_ms": 200, "min_similarity_score": 0.85}.
  - prompt_set_id (string): optional attachment for offline sims.

Pricing and packaging guidance

- FSA will be introduced as a freemium feature for hosted customers: basic recommendations in Console are free up to 5 routes.
- Paid tier "Advisor Pro": includes unlimited route recommendations, historical simulations (90 days), and a per-tenant monthly quota of auto-apply decision credits.
- Suggested pricing signals: base feature included in Redwood Optimize Premium bundle; Advisor Pro add-on at $X/month (tbd) or included for Dedicated customers with >1 committed GPU pool.

- Billing model for auto-apply credits: each auto-apply configuration change counts as 1 decision credit; batched scheduled rollouts count as 1 credit per 1000 routed requests simulated. Credits will be visible in Console under Optimization usage.

Roadmap milestones (Q4 2026 – Q2 2027)

- M1 (Q4 2026): MVP launch — Console recommendations, route UI, offline simulation pipeline, basic API fields.
- M2 (Q1 2027): Auto-apply beta with safety rollback and explainability. Integrations with billing to surface cost estimates.
- M3 (Q2 2027): Advisor Pro packaging, historical simulation retention, A/B experiment primitives, per-route experiment reporting.

Rollout and customer migration playbook

1. Internal beta (2 weeks): product, SRE, AE/CS teams use FSA on internal routes and Canary tenants.
2. Early access (4–6 weeks): invite 10 existing Optimize customers who have >100M tokens/month and active cost-optimization requests.
3. Public beta (8–12 weeks): open to all hosted accounts with Console access; monitor safety metrics daily.
4. GA: enable free tier + Advisor Pro marketing; update pricing docs and marketplace listing.

Migration steps for customers with existing Optimize recommendations:

1. Import existing Optimize rule-sets into FSA simulation context.
2. Run parallel simulations and compare deltas for a 7-day window; surface differences in Console.
3. Offer one-click migration to accept FSA recommendations or schedule a staged rollout.

Safety and incident playbook

- Immediate rollback: auto-apply will revert to the previous stable configuration if eval-driven quality regression > configured threshold for more than 3 consecutive evaluation windows.
- Pager policy: SRE on-call if > 5% of auto-apply routes trigger rollback within 24h or if recommendation latency p95 exceeds 500ms.

Release notes — v0.9 (MVP, 2026-10-21)

Highlights:
- Console recommendation card for per-route fidelity suggestions.
- API field fidelity_hint to request advisor suggestions.
- Offline simulation engine and baseline prompt-set library (privacy-mode enabled).
- Initial telemetry dashboards and SLO alerts.

Known limitations:
- Simulations use historical telemetry and model profiles; predictions may not capture recent upstream model changes.
- Initial quality signal relies on user-supplied prompt-sets; default sets are generic and may under-represent edge-case quality regressions.

Ownership and contacts

- Product owner: Marina Cortez (product).
- Engineering lead: Amit Reddy (eng-platform).
- Customer success lead: Jordan Blake (customer-success).

Open decisions and risks

- Pricing model for Advisor Pro is undecided and requires finance approval (open: pricing_floor and credit-sizing).
- Risk: cross-route simulation load could tax simulation cluster under heavy adoption; mitigation: rate-limit per-tenant simulation jobs and prioritize high-traffic routes.

Appendix: example SDK snippet (pseudo-code)

POST /v1/generate
{
  "route_id": "invoices-extract",
  "fidelity_hint": "advisor-suggest",
  "input": "Extract customer name and total from receipt..."
}

Response (partial):
{
  "advisor_decision": {
    "profile": "balanced-v2-q8",
    "confidence": 0.82,
    "rationale": "Estimated -28% cost, median latency +12ms, <1% QA delta vs baseline",
    "simulation_id": "sim-20261021-001"
  }
}

References and related reading

- Model performance profiles (internal): /eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/model-profiles
- Redwood Optimize product brief: /product-docs/product-overview/redwood-optimize-conductor-launch-playbook-2026-04-01
- Privacy & retention policy: /security-and-compliance/data-management/retention-policy-2025

Revision history

- 2026-09-15: Draft created by Marina Cortez.
- 2026-10-02: Added SLO table and telemetry definitions (Amit Reddy).
- 2026-10-21: Finalized MVP release notes and rollout playbook (published).

    "profile": "balanced-v2-q8",
    "confidence": 0.82,
    "rationale": "Estimated -28% cost, median latency +12ms, <1% QA delta vs baseline",
    "simulation_id": "sim-20261021-001"
  }
}

title: Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant — Product Brief
preview:
Overview

This brief describes the Developer Fidelity Guardrail Assistant (DFGA), a product module in Redwood Optimize intended to help developers detect, understand, and automatically remediate fidelity regressions and cost-risk in early-stage integrations. The DFGA combines lightweight local simulation, telemetry-driven heuristics, and in-console prescriptive actions so teams can move from exploratory API usage to production-safe consumption with minimal manual tuning.

Goals

- Reduce time-to-safe-production for developer integrations by detecting fidelity vs. cost regressions during onboarding and early ramp.
- Provide actionable remediation suggestions (config changes, candidate model variants, quantization presets) scoped to a customer’s observed workload.
- Integrate with Console, SDKs, and CI pipelines: capture preflight checks, post-deploy regressions, and staged rollout gates.

Non-goals

- Replacing full-scale model evaluation labs or human-in-the-loop QA. DFGA provides guardrails and prescriptive templates, not exhaustive evaluations.
- Automatic model retraining or dataset management.

Key user personas

1. Developer (PLG onboarding): wants fast feedback on whether the default consumption profile will meet cost and fidelity expectations.
2. ML/Platform engineer (Customer): needs deterministic preflight checks and a repeatable set of remediation actions for rollouts.
3. Solutions engineer (Redwood): uses DFGA reports to advise customers during Dedicated or Private onboarding.

Core capabilities

1) Preflight simulation sandbox (local & hosted)
- Run a 50–500 sample prompt set in a lightweight local emulator or hosted synthetic runner that mirrors customer traffic shape (length distribution, stop tokens).
- Outputs: per-prompt fidelity delta (against a golden baseline), token-rate cost estimate, latency percentiles.

2) Telemetry-driven anomaly detection
- Live monitoring of promptset drift and sudden fidelity regressions using rolling-window comparisons (p95 of loss-like similarity metrics and Delta-ROUGE/BLEU-style scores for structured outputs).
- Alert triggers: fidelity drop > 5% absolute over baseline for 15m, cost-per-1000-tokens increase > 20% sustained for 30m.

3) Prescriptive remediation and playbooks
- Config suggestions: batch-size, max-new-tokens, prefix-caching TTL, KV prefetch policy.
- Model recommendations: fall back to quantized 4-bit variant for short-form responses, or to a stronger small-parameter variant for high-fidelity long-form content.
- Deployment actions: staged canary rollout with 10/30/60% traffic split, automatic rollback thresholds.

4) CI / Pre-merge checks and SDK hooks
- CLI: redwood dfga run --promptset ./onboard/prompts.json --baseline gptx-base --profile ci-fast
- SDK: dfga.client.preflightCheck(promptSet, {profile: 'interactive', maxLatencyMs:600}) -> returns pass/fail and remediation tokens.

Acceptance criteria (MVP)

- Preflight sandbox executes within 20s for 100 prompts when run in hosted-fast mode.
- DFGA produces a deterministic remediation plan (one of: change-config, switch-variant, canary-plan) with estimated cost delta and expected fidelity delta.
- Console surfaces a clear pass/fail badge and a downloadable single-page PDF report.

SLOs and thresholds

| Metric | Target (MVP) | Alert threshold | Owner |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Preflight run latency (100 prompts, hosted) | < 20s | > 40s | product/infra |
| False-positive remediation suggestions (manual override rate) | < 15% | > 25% | product/research |
| Preflight determinism score (same input -> same plan) | 95% | < 90% | eng-platform |

Telemetry and observability requirements

- Instrument events: preflight.start, preflight.finish, remediation.generated, remediation.applied, regression.alert, canary.rollback.
- Trace context to surface model-variant, quantization profile, and sequence-length bucket.
- Dashboard: per-customer preflight pass-rate, average recommended cost delta, remediation adoption rate.

Data model / Output schema (stringified example for operator teams)

- preflight_id (uuid)
- promptset_name (string)
- baseline_variant (string)
- suggested_actions (list of strings)
- estimated_monthly_delta_usd (string, formatted)
- fidelity_delta_summary (string)

Example remediation payload (text sample)

{
  "preflight_id": "pf_123e4567",
  "baseline_variant": "redwood-neo-embed-1",
  "suggested_actions": ["set_batch_size:8","kv_prefetch:enabled","switch_variant:redwood-neo-embed-1-q4"],
  "estimated_monthly_delta_usd": "-120.00",
  "fidelity_delta_summary": "median similarity drop 2.6% on short prompts; recommended quantized fallback for <256 token responses"
}

Rollout plan (6-week phased)

1. Week 0–1: Internal alpha: feature flags enabled for Sandbox tenants, manual remediation application only. Owners: product, eng-developer-experience.
2. Week 2–3: Beta: invite selected customers; add Console UI for preflight results and remediation preview. Add telemetry dashboards. Owners: product, eng-platform, support.
3. Week 4: Public launch (soft): self-serve in Console for all new accounts, opt-in toggle for existing accounts. Provide migration notes for Dedicated customers. Owners: product, go-to-market.
4. Week 5–6: Stabilization and feedback: iterate on false-positive heuristics, add SDK pre-merge hooks, and integrate with onboarding templates. Owners: product, research.

Security and privacy considerations

- Prompt and sample data stored encrypted at rest when hosted; default retention 7 days for DFGA preflight artifacts.
- Option: tenant-managed S3 sink for audit exports in Private deployments.
- Access controls: RBAC for remediation actions; only users with "deployment-admin" role can apply suggested canary plans.

Operational runbooks (high level)

- If preflight runner latency > 40s consistently: check hosted emulator node pool (dfga-runner), restart pool via infra playbook, and notify eng-infra.
- If remediation adoption rate drops > 20% month-over-month: create a support ticket for top 5 customers and run a walkthrough.

Dependencies and integration matrix

- Console: results UI and PDF export (ticket: EXP-873)
- Optimize telemetry pipeline: ingestion and aggregation for preflight metrics (ticket: OPT-412)
- SDK hooks and CLI distribution (ticket: DX-221)
- Dedicated onboarding flow: mapping remediation actions to customer policies (ticket: DED-109)

Open questions and risks

- How to define a lightweight but meaningful fidelity baseline for multi-turn, stateful chat prompts? Proposal: support two baseline modes (golden-sample and synthetic-aggregated).
- Risk: over-reliance on DFGA recommendations could cause blind rollout; mitigate with explicit canary and rollback thresholds and manual approval by default.

Appendix — example Console UX copy

Title: "Preflight check: fidelity & cost guardrails"
Short: "DFGA analyzed your sample prompts and recommends a low-cost quantized fallback for short responses. Expected monthly savings: $120. Confidence: medium."
Actions: [Preview remediation] [Apply canary (10%)] [Dismiss]

References

- Onboarding and Cost Advisor Playbook (internal) — /product-docs/product-overview/onboarding-and-cost-advisor-playbook-2026-07-28
- Fidelity Staging SDK and Eval Playbook — /product-docs/requirements/prd-fidelity-staging-sdk-and-eval-playbook-2026-12-22
- Telemetry ingestion spec — /eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/telemetry-ingestion-spec-2026-05-12

Owner and contact

- Product owner: Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- Program lead: Marcus Liu
- Slack channel: #product-dfga

title: developer-sandbox-consumption-notebook-onboarding-playbook-2026-10-01
preview:
Overview:
This notebook is a practical playbook for product, developer-experience, and engineering teams to onboard developers to Redwood’s local developer sandbox and consumption UX. It combines step-by-step onboarding flows, telemetry and SLO mappings, configuration snippets, and rollout guidance so teams can: 1) accelerate developer time-to-first-call, 2) surface accurate consumption estimates early, and 3) collect telemetry that enables billing and product analytics.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
  - Provide a single, repeatable onboarding flow for the local sandbox (DevSandbox) that mirrors hosted consumption semantics.
  - Ensure consumption telemetry maps cleanly to billing/credit models and SLA tiers.
  - Reduce common developer confusion around fidelity, model variants, and warm capacity.
- Non-goals:
  - This playbook is not a detailed security hardening guide for private on-prem sandboxes (see eng-private-deployments).

Audience:
- Developer platform engineers, product managers for developer UX, solutions engineers, and developer advocates.

Scope and prerequisites:
- Applies to the local developer sandbox (Docker-based lightweight runtime) and hosted sandbox feature (Dev Playground).
- Prerequisites for developer machines:
  - Docker 20.10+ installed and user in docker group.
  - node 18+ or Python 3.10+ for SDK examples.
  - Redwood CLI v0.8.0+ installed (see CLI install below).

Quickstart (developer):
1) Install Redwood CLI:
   - curl -sSL https://redwood.example/install.sh | bash
2) Start local sandbox:
   - redwood sandbox start --profile minimal
3) Run a sample generation with hosted-consumption semantics:
   - redwood call \"--model rdx-small --prompt 'Summarize the release notes in two bullets' --budget 50-tokens\"
4) Inspect local consumption dashboard (http://localhost:4100/sandbox/dashboard) to verify token usage and approximate cost.

Onboarding checklist (for product and DX teams):
1. Preflight: add the sandbox link to Quickstarts and generate fresh examples for the most common languages (JS/Python).
2. Telemetry contract: confirm events and attributes are instrumented as described below.
3. Consumption estimator: ship a first-pass estimator UI component and hook to the telemetry endpoint.
4. Docs: update API examples and a troubleshooting section for the three most common failures (port in use, insufficient memory, model pull failure).
5. Support: add a canned response template in Zendesk and a troubleshooting playbook for Solutions Engineers.

Telemetry schema and mapping (human-readable table):
- NOTE: The sandbox emits the same canonical events as hosted inference but includes sandbox.* namespace. Map these to billing-friendly attributes in the ingestion pipeline.

Event | Attributes (key:example) | Mapped billing field
------|------------------------|-------------------
sandbox.session_start | session_id:abc123, user_id:uid-998 | session_id
sandbox.request | request_id:rq-01, model:rdx-small, tokens_in:12, tokens_out:88, fidelity:standard | tokens_total (tokens_in + tokens_out), model_variant
sandbox.estimate_view | estimator_id:est-1, estimated_tokens:200, region:us-west | preflight_estimate_flag
sandbox.error | code:MODEL_PULL_FAILED, retryable:true | incident_tag

SLO and consumption mapping (table):
- Dev-facing SLOs are relaxed but must be captured so customers understand delta vs production.

Metric | Dev sandbox target | Production mapping
-------|-------------------|------------------
median_latency
(per token) | 25ms (local) | user-facing latency estimate
error_rate | <2% | feeds into fallback heuristics
token_accuracy_estimate | N/A (approx) | used only for fidelity guidance
consumption_estimate_accuracy | ± 20% | displayed to customers as an estimate, not billed

UX flows and consumption semantics:
- Flow A: New developer (zero-touch)
  1. Developer clicks Quickstart -> chooses language sample.
  2. CLI downloads a small local runtime and a model shim (no full weights).
  3. First call runs against a lightweight emulator that returns deterministic outputs and a conservative token estimate.
  4. The sandbox dashboard shows an \"Estimate\" badge (not billed).
- Flow B: Experienced developer (fidelity preview)
  1. Developer opts into fidelity preview in the UI.
  2. Sandbox pulls a quantized model binary for a short-lived runtime container and runs a real inference (limited by local CPU/GPU if present).
  3. Telemetry tags calls with fidelity=preview and includes approximate cost delta.

Configuration examples:
- Minimal redwood.yaml for sandbox profile:
  ---
  profile: minimal
  runtime: local-emulator
  model_shim: rdx-small-shim:v1
  telemetry_sink: https://ingest.redwood.internal/sandbox
  limits:
    max_tokens: 1024
    cpu_shares: 1024
    memory_mb: 2048
  ---

- CLI example to start with a fidelity preview (for developers with GPU):
  redwood sandbox start --profile fidelity-preview --gpu auto --max-tokens 512

Observability and instrumentation checklist (engineering):
- Ensure the following are emitted from sandbox runtime: sandbox.session_start, sandbox.request, sandbox.estimate_view, sandbox.error, sandbox.session_end.
- Include the following attributes on sandbox.request: request_id, session_id, user_id (hashed), model, model_variant, tokens_in, tokens_out, fidelity, local_cpu, local_gpu, estimate_delta_percent.
- Add debug-level telemetry to capture model pull time and container startup duration for lab experiments.

Billing and product considerations (product owners):
- Sandbox should never generate billable events by default. Instead:
  - All sandbox consumption is billed only if the customer explicitly opts-in to \"Bill sandbox runs\" in the admin console.
  - When opt-in is active, sandbox events are flagged with billing_context:sandbox_opt_in and mapped via the same pipeline as hosted events.
- UI must display the distinction between \"Estimate\" and \"Billable\" with an inline tooltip explaining variance.

Rollout plan (staged):
1. Alpha (internal teams): release to 15 internal developers. Collect telemetry for 2 weeks.
2. Private beta (selected customers): enable opt-in billing for 3 pilot customers for 1 month. Track estimate accuracy and support tickets.
3. Public beta: open to all self-serve users with billing opt-in UI behind a soft gate and detailed docs.
4. GA: enable wider UX and link to onboarding checklists in Console.

Launch success criteria (must pass all):
- 90% of alpha participants can complete Quickstart in <15 minutes.
- Estimate accuracy median error <= 25% in private beta.
- <5 critical support tickets per pilot tenant related to environment setup.

Operational runbook (high level):
1. If a sandbox runtime crashes repeatedly for a user:
   - Check sandbox.error events for MODEL_PULL_FAILED or OOM.
   - If MODEL_PULL_FAILED, verify model_shim tag and registry health; escalate to runtime-oncall.
2. If estimate variance >50% for a tenant:
   - Disable billing opt-in for that tenant and open a support ticket.
3. If telemetry ingestion for sandbox fails:
   - Fall back to local log capture and enqueue events for retry; notify DX team and update status page.

Owner and support matrix:
- Product owner: Asha Verma (product)
- DX engineering lead: Marcus Lee (engineering)

title: Interactive Cost Signal Orchestrator (Design Spec)
preview:
## Overview

This design spec describes the Interactive Cost Signal Orchestrator (ICSO): a lightweight, runtime-oriented control plane that ingests low-latency cost and fidelity signals and emits per-request routing and fidelity directives for interactive LLM applications. ICSO is intended to be used by Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments to reduce unit cost while preserving customer SLOs in conversational and transactional workloads.

ICSO coordinates with the serving runtime, the policy store, and the telemetry pipeline to make deterministic, auditable decisions that can be tuned per-tenant and per-route.

## Goals

- Reduce average cost-per-user-interaction by up to 18% for targeted conversational routes without increasing 95th-percentile latency by more than 5%.
- Provide a declarative policy model that product teams can use to bind cost/fidelity rules to routes and tenants.
- Emit decisions in <20ms at runtime for interactive requests (end-to-end decision path).
- Produce rich telemetry for post-hoc analysis and offline simulation.

## Non-goals

- ICSO will not replace long-running batch optimization (e.g., offline quantization recommendations).
- It will not perform model training or quality evaluation — only routing and fidelity control decisions.

## High-level architecture

- Ingest: lightweight signal collectors forward condensed metrics (tokens, per-token-cost-estimate, latency, KV cache hit, previous-fidelity-tags) to ICSO via gRPC/HTTP.
- Policy Engine: evaluates ordered rules (tenant -> route -> global) and produces a decision object.
- Decision Router: stamps responses with directives (model_variant, quant_profile, max_tokens, streaming_mode, fallback_priority) and returns to the runtime.
- Async Recorder: writes compact decision+signal records to the telemetry cluster for auditing and model retraining.

Diagram (text):
```
[client] -> [edge proxy] -> [serving runtime] -> [ICSO decision call]
                                    \\                          |
                                     --> [policy engine] ------>
                                    \\                          v
                                     --> [telemetry recorder] -> [metrics lake]
```

## API surface

1) Decision call (sync)
- Endpoint: POST /v1/decide
- Payload (JSON):
```
{
  "tenant_id": "string",
  "route_id": "string",
  "request_id": "uuid",
  "signals": {
    "tokens_in": 120,
    "estimated_remaining_tokens": 800,
    "avg_latency_ms": 42.1,
    "kv_cache_hit": true,
    "last_model_variant": "gptx-8k-fp16"
  }
}
```
- Response (JSON, <20ms typical):
```
{
  "decision_id": "uuid",
  "model_variant": "gptx-8k-quant8",
  "quant_profile": "q8-lite",
  "max_output_tokens": 256,
  "streaming": true,
  "fallback_priority": ["gptx-8k-quant8","gptx-8k-fp16","gptx-4k-quant8"],
  "reason": "policy:tenant-cost-safety,score:0.72"
}
```
- Latency SLA: 95% of calls < 20ms, 99% < 40ms.

2) Async telemetry ingestion
- Endpoint: POST /v1/record-decision (batched)
- Records persist decisions for auditing, simulation input, and model feedback loops.

## Policy model and language

- Policies are declarative JSON documents stored in the policy store. Policies are evaluated in priority order.
- Supported predicates: token_count, rolling_avg_latency_ms, kv_cache_hit, external_quality_flag, time_of_day, tenant_override.
- Supported actions: set_model_variant, set_quant_profile, set_max_tokens, enable_streaming, force_fallback, tag_decision.

Example policy (JSON snippet):
```
{
  "id": "tenant_alpha_cost_safety_v1",
  "priority": 100,
  "match": {"tenant_id": "tenant_alpha", "route_id": "chat.gen"},
  "when": {"tokens_in": {"gte": 100}, "rolling_avg_latency_ms": {"lt": 150}},
  "then": {"set_quant_profile": "q8-lite", "max_output_tokens": 300, "tag_decision": "cost_safety"}
}
```

Policy lifecycle notes:
- Policies are packaged in PRs and validated by a policy CI that runs salted simulation workloads.
- Policy changes are feature-flagged and can be canaried to a percentage of traffic.

## Decision logic and scoring

ICSO computes a compact score per candidate action using:
- cost_estimate (tokens * model_unit_cost) normalized by tenant budget weight
- latency_risk (estimated added latency for chosen model variant)
- quality_impact (static mapping from model_variant -> expected quality delta for route, based on offline evals)

Score = w_cost * normalized_cost + w_latency * latency_risk + w_quality * (1 - quality_impact)

Weights (w_cost, w_latency, w_quality) are configurable per-tenant. Default weights aim to preserve quality (w_quality=0.6, w_latency=0.25, w_cost=0.15).

## Configuration table (examples)
| config_key | type | default | description |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| decision_timeout_ms | integer | 30 | Max time to wait for policy evaluation before returning default decision |
| max_candidates | integer | 3 | Number of fallback variants to consider |
| telemetry_batch_ms | integer | 5000 | Window to batch async telemetry writes |
| default_weights | string | "0.15,0.25,0.6" | default cost/latency/quality weights |

## Runtime contracts with serving runtime

- The runtime must include compact signal headers on the decision call and must apply returned directives within the same request lifecycle.
- If the decision call times out, the runtime applies a conservative default decision and records a timeout flag.
- A standardized header X-ICSO-Decision-Id must be returned in responses for traceability.

## Rollout plan

1) Canary (2 weeks) - Internal tenants + 2 beta customers: feature flag enabled, telemetry-only mode (decisions computed but not applied).
2) Shadow (3 weeks) - Serve decisions in parallel; record divergence metrics between runtime-applied and ICSO-recommended actions.
3) Partial rollout (4 weeks) - Apply decisions for 10% of traffic for low-risk routes (non-critical conversational routes).
4) Ramp (6 weeks) - Gradual increase to 50% for targeted routes with weekly gating criteria.
5) General availability - After meeting cost reduction and quality gates.

Gating criteria for each stage:
- No regression in route-level quality >0.5% (measured via pre-specified eval prompts).
- No increase in 95p latency >5% for target routes.
- Cost improvement >=8% for canary group or other business-specified thresholds.

## Testing & validation

- Unit tests for policy parsing and evaluation engine.
- Deterministic integration tests with a serving runtime simulator (simulate timeouts, high-latency backends, cache-hits).
- Perf tests to validate decision latency under target QPS.
- Runbook-based experiments: A/B traffic with synthetic workloads and real user traffic (consent from customer if applicable).

## SLOs & metrics

- Decision latency: P95 < 20ms (target), P99 < 50ms (target).
- Decision accuracy: percentage of decisions that result in the expected fallback path when applied (target > 97% for non-timeout cases).
- Cost reduction uplift: tracked per-route and per-tenant (goal stable >= 8–18% depending on route).
- Telemetry fidelity: decision records delivered to metrics lake within 60s 99% of the time.

Prometheus / metrics emitted:
- icso.decision_latency_ms{quantile}
- icso.decision_timeouts_total
- icso.decisions_by_action_total{action}
- icso.cost_estimate_reduction_ratio

## Operational runbooks (summary)

1) If decision latency spikes (> P95 50ms for >10m): escalate to eng-platform, toggle decision_timeout_ms lower, move ICSO to shadow mode for impacted routes.
2) If cost reduction delta disappears: run divergence analysis vs. offline simulator; rollback recent policy changes.
3) If quality regressions detected: disable automated quant_profile actions and revert to quality-first default weights.

## Security & compliance

- All decision calls are authenticated using mutual TLS in-hosted and dedicated deployments. Private deployments may use customer-managed certs.
- Decisions and signal records stored in telemetry lake respect tenant data retention policies; no raw PII should be stored in decision payloads.

## Ownership & roadmap

- Component Owner: Product (Mariana Gomez) — policy model and product requirements.
- Engineering Lead: Ethan Park — runtime integration and infra.
- Observability Lead: Priya Nair — metrics, dashboards, and CI for policy validation.

Planned features post-GA:
- Reinforcement of decision scoring using live quality feedback loops.
- Policy editor in Console with safe simulation mode.
- Local developer SDK for running ICSO logic in dev sandboxes.

## Appendix

A) Example decision record (telemetry payload)
```
{
  "decision_id": "uuid",
  "tenant_id": "tenant_alpha",
  "route_id": "chat.gen",
  "request_id": "uuid",
  "signals": {"tokens_in": 210, "kv_cache_hit": false},
  "decision": {"model_variant": "gptx-8k-quant8","quant_profile":"q8-lite","reason":"policy:tenant_alpha_cost_safety_v1"},
  "latency_ms": 18.4,
  "timestamp": "2026-09-30T15:24:12Z"
}
```

B) FAQ (short)
- Q: What if a tenant requires fixed model variants?
  - A: ICSO honors tenant_override policies with priority > 1000; these are configured through the policy store and cannot be overridden by lower-priority rules.
- Q: How do we audit a decision?
  - A: Use the X-ICSO-Decision-Id header to look up the decision record in the telemetry lake.

C) Related docs
- See related PRD: /confluence/product-docs/requirements/predictive-batching-cost-optimizer-prd-2026-03-01
- Serving runtime contract: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/kernel-selection-guidelines

## Next steps

- Approve design and schedule a two-week spike to implement the policy engine prototype and perf harness.
- Create policy CI pipeline and sandbox integration tests as part of the spike deliverables.
