title: Graceful fidelity degradation PRD
preview:
Title: Graceful fidelity degradation PRD

Overview:
This PRD describes the user-facing and technical requirements for a Graceful Fidelity Degradation feature: a platform-level capability that reduces model output fidelity in controlled ways when customer-configured SLOs (latency, error rate, cost budget) are at risk. The objective is to preserve availability and acceptable latency by switching to lower-fidelity model variants, structured output with summarization, or token-budgeted responses while guaranteeing predictable cost and automated rollback when pressure abates.

Background and problem statement:
- Problem: Production applications using Redwood Inference occasionally face capacity or latency pressure (regional outages, batch-surge traffic, cold-starts for private deployments). Current fallbacks are coarse (full model variant swap or throttling) and can cause large quality regressions or customer surprise.
- Opportunity: Provide a predictable, observable, and recoverable fidelity-degradation surface that application teams can opt into per-route to maintain availability and aligned unit economics.

Goals (success criteria):
1. Preserve availability for 99.9% of routed requests under transient capacity pressure by reducing per-request compute in controlled steps.
2. Provide measurable fidelity/quality delta metrics (ROUGE/BLEU/embedding-similarity or custom eval sets) exposed in Console for every degradation step.
3. Ensure automated rollback to full-fidelity within 30s once SLO metrics return to normal.
4. Surface cost delta and estimated token savings in the Console and usage APIs.
5. Allow per-tenant and per-route policy configuration with sensible defaults.

Non-Goals:
- This feature is not a substitute for long-term capacity provisioning. It will not change billing semantics for token accounting; it surfaces estimated savings but does not change metering.
- It does not implement model training or quality-improvement techniques beyond variant selection and structured output templates.

Target personas and use-cases:
- Product engineers building chat assistants who require high availability across regions.
- Platform/infra teams wanting graceful SLO-preserving behavior without manual playbooks.
- Customer success teams enabling this for enterprise customers with strict uptime SLAs.

User stories:
- As a product engineer, I want to configure a per-route fidelity policy so my chat route degrades to a summarization-only response when latency SLO is threatened.
- As an SRE, I want automated, observable rollback so I do not need to manually re-enable full-fidelity.
- As a product manager, I want Console charts that show quality delta for each degradation step compared to golden prompts.

High-level approach and design summary:
- Policy model: per-tenant, per-route JSON policy with prioritized ordered steps (e.g., model-variant -> quantized-variant -> structured-output -> token-cap). Each step includes trigger conditions, max duration, and permissible quality-budgets.
- Runtime enforcement: Serving runtime will evaluate policies in a sidecar decision path before scheduling a request. Decisions are tagged in traces and logs for observability.
- Observability: New Console widgets, structured event logs, and trace spans for decisions. Quality metrics are computed asynchronously via lightweight eval proxies and batched against stored prompt-sets.

Functional requirements (must-have):
1. Policy schema (example) - stored in Config Service and exposed in API:

Example policy JSON (user-visible):

{
  "max_latency_ms": 250,
  "steps": [
    {"id": "variant-a-quant", "type": "model_variant", "variant": "llama-70b-q4a", "triggers": {"p95_latency_ms": 300}},
    {"id": "summarize-only", "type": "structured_output", "template": "summarize_first_paragraph", "triggers": {"p95_latency_ms": 400}},
    {"id": "token-cap-200", "type": "token_cap", "max_tokens": 200, "triggers": {"region_capacity_utilization": 0.85}}
  ]
}

2. API: PATCH /v1/routes/{route_id}/fidelity-policy to create/update policies.
3. Console: a policy editor UI with preview of expected behavior for each step and simulated quality deltas using tenant prompt-sets.
4. Runtime tags: Every degraded request must emit a decision tag with step id and rationale.
5. Rollback: automatic step reversal when trigger conditions clear for 3 consecutive evaluation windows (configurable, default 3 x 10s).
6. Opt-in: Default tenant state = disabled. Admins can enable per-tenant compliance mode (required by some enterprise customers).
7. Observability: Time-series metrics: fidelity.degradation.count, fidelity.degradation.duration, fidelity.quality.delta.mean, fidelity.rollback.count.
8. Alerts: SLO burn alert when automated degradation triggers > 10% of requests for > 5 minutes for a tenant.

Non-functional requirements (NFRs):
- Latency overhead: decision layer must add <= 3ms p95 to overall request path when policy checks are cached.
- Storage: Config entries per route should be < 5KB.
- Security: Policies are tenant-scoped; only route owners or org admins can modify. Policy changes produce audit log entries.
- Scalability: Decision service must support 10k policy evaluations/sec in hosted control-plane.

Quality and evaluation metrics:
- Primary: % availability preserved during simulated pressure (target 99.9%).
- Secondary: mean quality delta measured by tenant eval-set (target median delta <= 12% for first fidelity step).
- Cost: estimated per-request compute savings aggregated over a week when enabled.

Policy schema and validation rules (table):
| Field | Type | Description | Constraints | Default |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| max_latency_ms | int | Target latency SLO for route | >50 and <5000 | 250 |
| steps | list | Ordered degradation steps | 1..5 steps | n/a |
| step.id | string | Unique step id | Alphanumeric + dashes | required |
| step.type | enum | model_variant, structured_output, token_cap | one of allowed types | required |
| step.triggers | object | Trigger predicates | See triggers table | required |

Triggers supported (examples):
- p95_latency_ms > X
- region_capacity_utilization > 0.Y
- error_rate_5m > Z

Rollout plan and milestones:
1. Discovery & API design (4w): finalize schema, UX mockups, security review. Owner: Product.
2. Control-plane implementation (6w, parallel): policy store, validation, audit logs. Owner: Eng - Platform.
3. Runtime integration (8w, parallel): sidecar decision path and tagging. Owner: Eng - Serving Runtime.
4. Telemetry & Console (6w): ingest metrics, add Console charts, preview. Owner: Eng - Platform + DX.
5. Canary (4w): enable for 5 pilot tenants (internal and 2 customers) with manual ops rollout.
6. GA and docs (2w): runbook, migration guide, public docs.

Total expected lead time to GA: ~6 months when run in parallel across teams.

Canary strategy and criteria:
- Canary targets: internal app (redwood-console), two low-risk customers (consent obtained).
- Success criteria: degrade invocation latency saved >=20% on average when triggered, quality delta within targets on golden prompts, rollback operates within 30s.

Observability and traces:
- Trace annotation: fidelity.step_id, fidelity.trigger_reason, fidelity.pre_estimated_cost_savings.
- New dashboards: Per-tenant fidelity events, per-route decision timelines, quality-delta over time.
- Light eval proxy: asynchronously scores a sample of degraded responses vs golden outputs and writes fidelity.quality.delta metrics.

Console UX and admin surfaces:
- Policy editor (advanced mode) + simple toggle view for quick enable/disable.
- Preview pane that runs 10 selected tenant prompts through simulated steps and highlights the expected change and calculated similarity score.
- Billing view: projected weekly savings and number of degraded requests.

Pricing & packaging considerations:
- Feature will be available to Dedicated and Hosted customers; Private customers require on-prem runtime compatibility (see Dependencies).
- Default offering: included for paid hosted accounts.
- Enterprise add-on: compliance-mode support (audit retention > 1 year) and SRE-assisted rollout for a fee.
- Pricing note: this feature only provides estimated savings; it does not change per-token metering. For enterprise contracts that purchase Dedicated reserved capacity, assist with a consultancy bundle to define safe policies and guardrails.

Migration guidance for existing customers:
1. Audit existing fallback policies and route-level fallbacks.
2. Create tenant fidelity policy in a staging environment and run Console preview.
3. Schedule controlled canary; monitor fidelity.quality.delta metrics and SLO burn.
4. If unacceptable quality delta, tighten triggers or reduce allowed steps.

Operational runbook (short):
1. Detect: Alert fires when fidelity.degradation.count ratio > 0.1 for 5m.
2. Investigate: Check Console traces for step IDs and triggers.
3. Mitigate: If quality regressions exceed thresholds, temporarily set route.policy = disabled for tenant via PATCH API.
4. Remediate: If regional capacity is the root cause, trigger cross-region failover per existing runbook.
5. Postmortem: Capture decision timeline and attach to incident.

Security, privacy, and compliance considerations:
- Policies are tenant-scoped with RBAC. Audit logs record author, timestamp, and diff.
- For regulated customers, disabling automated degradation is permitted via compliance-mode.
- The light eval proxy only uses anonymized prompt-sets unless tenant opts in to provide labeled prompts.

Dependencies and risks:
- Control-plane policy store and Config Service availability.
- Serving runtime integration complexity (must maintain <=3ms added latency).
- Risk of customer surprise: need strong Console previews and defaults.

Open questions (to resolve in discovery):
- Should token-cap steps be metered differently in billing reports? (Finance)
- What are acceptable default quality deltas per model family? (Applied ML)
- How to limit cross-tenant test data exposure for light eval proxy? (Security)

Ownership and contacts:
- Product Owner: Maya Thompson (product)
- Eng - Platform: Owen Martinez
- Eng - Serving Runtime: Ethan Park
- Applied ML / Quality: Rhea Kapoor
- Customer Success contact for pilot: Priya Desai

Appendices:
A. Example CLI to preview a route policy:

$ curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/routes/rt-123/preview-fidelity \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' \
  -d '{"policy": {"max_latency_ms":250, "steps": [{"id":"summ","type":"structured_output","template":"summarize_first_paragraph","triggers": {"p95_latency_ms":350}}]}}'

Returns: JSON with simulated outputs, similarity scores, and estimated cost delta.

B. Minimal policy validation pseudo-rules:
1. steps.length between 1 and 5.
2. each step.id unique.
3. triggers reference only supported telemetry keys.
4. total allowed token caps cannot be lower than 32 tokens.

C. Glossary:
- Fidelity step: an ordered transformation with lower compute or stricter output budget.
- Light eval proxy: asynchronous scoring service for quality delta estimation.

Document change log:
- 2026-09-07: Draft created (Maya Thompson).
- 2026-09-14: Added policy schema and rollout timeline (Ethan Park).
- 2026-09-21: Updated console UX notes and canary criteria (Rhea Kapoor).
text:
Title: Graceful fidelity degradation PRD

Overview:
This PRD describes the user-facing and technical requirements for a Graceful Fidelity Degradation feature: a platform-level capability that reduces model output fidelity in controlled ways when customer-configured SLOs (latency, error rate, cost budget) are at risk. The objective is to preserve availability and acceptable latency by switching to lower-fidelity model variants, structured output with summarization, or token-budgeted responses while guaranteeing predictable cost and automated rollback when pressure abates.

Background and problem statement:
- Problem: Production applications using Redwood Inference occasionally face capacity or latency pressure (regional outages, batch-surge traffic, cold-starts for private deployments). Current fallbacks are coarse (full model variant swap or throttling) and can cause large quality regressions or customer surprise.
- Opportunity: Provide a predictable, observable, and recoverable fidelity-degradation surface that application teams can opt into per-route to maintain availability and aligned unit economics.

Goals (success criteria):
1. Preserve availability for 99.9% of routed requests under transient capacity pressure by reducing per-request compute in controlled steps.
2. Provide measurable fidelity/quality delta metrics (ROUGE/BLEU/embedding-similarity or custom eval sets) exposed in Console for every degradation step.
3. Ensure automated rollback to full-fidelity within 30s once SLO metrics return to normal.
4. Surface cost delta and estimated token savings in the Console and usage APIs.
5. Allow per-tenant and per-route policy configuration with sensible defaults.

Non-Goals:
- This feature is not a substitute for long-term capacity provisioning. It will not change billing semantics for token accounting; it surfaces estimated savings but does not change metering.
- It does not implement model training or quality-improvement techniques beyond variant selection and structured output templates.

Target personas and use-cases:
- Product engineers building chat assistants who require high availability across regions.
- Platform/infra teams wanting graceful SLO-preserving behavior without manual playbooks.
- Customer success teams enabling this for enterprise customers with strict uptime SLAs.

User stories:
- As a product engineer, I want to configure a per-route fidelity policy so my chat route degrades to a summarization-only response when latency SLO is threatened.
- As an SRE, I want automated, observable rollback so I do not need to manually re-enable full-fidelity.
- As a product manager, I want Console charts that show quality delta for each degradation step compared to golden prompts.

High-level approach and design summary:
- Policy model: per-tenant, per-route JSON policy with prioritized ordered steps (e.g., model-variant -> quantized-variant -> structured-output -> token-cap). Each step includes trigger conditions, max duration, and permissible quality-budgets.
- Runtime enforcement: Serving runtime will evaluate policies in a sidecar decision path before scheduling a request. Decisions are tagged in traces and logs for observability.
- Observability: New Console widgets, structured event logs, and trace spans for decisions. Quality metrics are computed asynchronously via lightweight eval proxies and batched against stored prompt-sets.

Functional requirements (must-have):
1. Policy schema (example) - stored in Config Service and exposed in API:

Example policy JSON (user-visible):

{
  "max_latency_ms": 250,
  "steps": [
    {"id": "variant-a-quant", "type": "model_variant", "variant": "llama-70b-q4a", "triggers": {"p95_latency_ms": 300}},
    {"id": "summarize-only", "type": "structured_output", "template": "summarize_first_paragraph", "triggers": {"p95_latency_ms": 400}},
    {"id": "token-cap-200", "type": "token_cap", "max_tokens": 200, "triggers": {"region_capacity_utilization": 0.85}}
  ]
}

2. API: PATCH /v1/routes/{route_id}/fidelity-policy to create/update policies.
3. Console: a policy editor UI with preview of expected behavior for each step and simulated quality deltas using tenant prompt-sets.
4. Runtime tags: Every degraded request must emit a decision tag with step id and rationale.
5. Rollback: automatic step reversal when trigger conditions clear for 3 consecutive evaluation windows (configurable, default 3 x 10s).
6. Opt-in: Default tenant state = disabled. Admins can enable per-tenant compliance mode (required by some enterprise customers).
7. Observability: Time-series metrics: fidelity.degradation.count, fidelity.degradation.duration, fidelity.quality.delta.mean, fidelity.rollback.count.
8. Alerts: SLO burn alert when automated degradation triggers > 10% of requests for > 5 minutes for a tenant.

Non-functional requirements (NFRs):
- Latency overhead: decision layer must add <= 3ms p95 to overall request path when policy checks are cached.
- Storage: Config entries per route should be < 5KB.
- Security: Policies are tenant-scoped; only route owners or org admins can modify. Policy changes produce audit log entries.
- Scalability: Decision service must support 10k policy evaluations/sec in hosted control-plane.

Quality and evaluation metrics:
- Primary: % availability preserved during simulated pressure (target 99.9%).
- Secondary: mean quality delta measured by tenant eval-set (target median delta <= 12% for first fidelity step).
- Cost: estimated per-request compute savings aggregated over a week when enabled.

Policy schema and validation rules (table):
| Field | Type | Description | Constraints | Default |
|-------|------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| max_latency_ms | int | Target latency SLO for route | >50 and <5000 | 250 |
| steps | list | Ordered degradation steps | 1..5 steps | n/a |
| step.id | string | Unique step id | Alphanumeric + dashes | required |
| step.type | enum | model_variant, structured_output, token_cap | one of allowed types | required |
| step.triggers | object | Trigger predicates | See triggers table | required |

Triggers supported (examples):
- p95_latency_ms > X
- region_capacity_utilization > 0.Y
- error_rate_5m > Z

Rollout plan and milestones:
1. Discovery & API design (4w): finalize schema, UX mockups, security review. Owner: Product.
2. Control-plane implementation (6w, parallel): policy store, validation, audit logs. Owner: Eng - Platform.
3. Runtime integration (8w, parallel): sidecar decision path and tagging. Owner: Eng - Serving Runtime.
4. Telemetry & Console (6w): ingest metrics, add Console charts, preview. Owner: Eng - Platform + DX.
5. Canary (4w): enable for 5 pilot tenants (internal and 2 customers) with manual ops rollout.
6. GA and docs (2w): runbook, migration guide, public docs.

Total expected lead time to GA: ~6 months when run in parallel across teams.

Canary strategy and criteria:
- Canary targets: internal app (redwood-console), two low-risk customers (consent obtained).
- Success criteria: degrade invocation latency saved >=20% on average when triggered, quality delta within targets on golden prompts, rollback operates within 30s.

Observability and traces:
- Trace annotation: fidelity.step_id, fidelity.trigger_reason, fidelity.pre_estimated_cost_savings.
- New dashboards: Per-tenant fidelity events, per-route decision timelines, quality-delta over time.
- Light eval proxy: asynchronously scores a sample of degraded responses vs golden outputs and writes fidelity.quality.delta metrics.

Console UX and admin surfaces:
- Policy editor (advanced mode) + simple toggle view for quick enable/disable.
- Preview pane that runs 10 selected tenant prompts through simulated steps and highlights the expected change and calculated similarity score.
- Billing view: projected weekly savings and number of degraded requests.

Pricing & packaging considerations:
- Feature will be available to Dedicated and Hosted customers; Private customers require on-prem runtime compatibility (see Dependencies).
- Default offering: included for paid hosted accounts.
- Enterprise add-on: compliance-mode support (audit retention > 1 year) and SRE-assisted rollout for a fee.
- Pricing note: this feature only provides estimated savings; it does not change per-token metering. For enterprise contracts that purchase Dedicated reserved capacity, assist with a consultancy bundle to define safe policies and guardrails.

Migration guidance for existing customers:
1. Audit existing fallback policies and route-level fallbacks.
2. Create tenant fidelity policy in a staging environment and run Console preview.
3. Schedule controlled canary; monitor fidelity.quality.delta metrics and SLO burn.
4. If unacceptable quality delta, tighten triggers or reduce allowed steps.

Operational runbook (short):
1. Detect: Alert fires when fidelity.degradation.count ratio > 0.1 for 5m.
2. Investigate: Check Console traces for step IDs and triggers.
3. Mitigate: If quality regressions exceed thresholds, temporarily set route.policy = disabled for tenant via PATCH API.
4. Remediate: If regional capacity is the root cause, trigger cross-region failover per existing runbook.
5. Postmortem: Capture decision timeline and attach to incident.

Security, privacy, and compliance considerations:
- Policies are tenant-scoped with RBAC. Audit logs record author, timestamp, and diff.
- For regulated customers, disabling automated degradation is permitted via compliance-mode.
- The light eval proxy only uses anonymized prompt-sets unless tenant opts in to provide labeled prompts.

Dependencies and risks:
- Control-plane policy store and Config Service availability.
- Serving runtime integration complexity (must maintain <=3ms added latency).
- Risk of customer surprise: need strong Console previews and defaults.

Open questions (to resolve in discovery):
- Should token-cap steps be metered differently in billing reports? (Finance)
- What are acceptable default quality deltas per model family? (Applied ML)
- How to limit cross-tenant test data exposure for light eval proxy? (Security)

Ownership and contacts:
- Product Owner: Maya Thompson (product)
- Eng - Platform: Owen Martinez
- Eng - Serving Runtime: Ethan Park
- Applied ML / Quality: Rhea Kapoor
- Customer Success contact for pilot: Priya Desai

Appendices:
A. Example CLI to preview a route policy:

$ curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/routes/rt-123/preview-fidelity \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' \
  -d '{"policy": {"max_latency_ms":250, "steps": [{"id":"summ","type":"structured_output","template":"summarize_first_paragraph","triggers": {"p95_latency_ms":350}}]}}'

Returns: JSON with simulated outputs, similarity scores, and estimated cost delta.

B. Minimal policy validation pseudo-rules:
1. steps.length between 1 and 5.
2. each step.id unique.
3. triggers reference only supported telemetry keys.
4. total allowed token caps cannot be lower than 32 tokens.

C. Glossary:
- Fidelity step: an ordered transformation with lower compute or stricter output budget.
- Light eval proxy: asynchronous scoring service for quality delta estimation.

Document change log:
- 2026-09-07: Draft created (Maya Thompson).
- 2026-09-14: Added policy schema and rollout timeline (Ethan Park).
- 2026-09-21: Updated console UX notes and canary criteria (Rhea Kapoor).

title: variant-marketplace-prd-catalog-and-addon-pricing-2026-09-10
preview:
Overview:
This PRD describes the Variant Marketplace: a first-class product surface that lets customers discover, select, and meter curated model variants and optional add-ons (latency tiers, quantization profiles, distillation presets, and managed warm pools). The Marketplace enables price differentiation by variant and supports metered add-ons to improve unit economics without sacrificing discoverability or operational controls.

Context and motivation:
- Customers increasingly want to choose model tradeoffs (cost vs quality vs latency) without building custom model ops.
- Current catalog is binary (model version). We need a marketplace UX + billing contract that encodes variant attributes and add-on metering.
- This unlocks: explicit pricing for lower-cost quantized variants, premium pricing for ultra-low-latency variants on dedicated capacity, and per-customer curated variant bundles.

Goals (what success looks like):
1) Catalog: Surface at least 6 curated variant types (base fp16, int8-quant, int4-quant, distilled-small, latency-optimized, safety-filtered) with clear attributes and expected quality deltas.
2) Pricing: Implement per-variant and per-add-on metering that feeds billing and usage APIs. Clear price delta visibility in Console.
3) UX: Allow customers to pin a default variant and buy managed add-ons (warm-pool, premium routing) via Console and API.
4) Telemetry: Emit variant_id, add_on_ids, price_modifier, and per-request unit_cost for every billing event.
5) Rollout: Pilot with 3 enterprise customers and GA within 12 weeks after pilot completion.

Non-goals:
- This PRD does not include marketplace for third-party models (future phase).
- It does not replace Dedicated capacity reservations; it augments them with variant-level pricing.

Success metrics (KPIs):
- Percent of new hosted requests selecting a non-default variant: target 12% in Q1 post-GA.
- Incremental ARPU from add-ons: +8% for pilot customers.
- Incorrect billing events rate: <0.01% during GA.
- Time-to-serve for premium routing add-on: p95 <= 120ms.

High-level product requirements (epics):
A. Catalog model metadata and discovery
  - Requirement A1: Extend model catalog schema with fields: variant_id, variant_type, expected_quality_delta_pct, cost_index, recommended_workloads, compatibility_matrix.
  - Acceptance criteria: Console list shows variant badges, filter by cost/latency/size, and API returns variant metadata in catalog endpoints.

B. Pricing & billing contract changes
  - Requirement B1: Billing schema supports price_modifier per variant (positive or negative basis points) and per-add-on flat or per-token meter.
  - Requirement B2: Add-ons must be representable as catalog SKUs: warm_pool_hour, premium_routing_hour, reduced_token_cost_percent.
  - Acceptance criteria: Billing pipeline emits line items with sku, unit_price, quantity and matched variant_id. Test billing reconciliation with sandbox invoice.

C. Console & API UX
  - Requirement C1: Console variant selector in Deployment and Route pages; API param variant_selector: {variant_id, add_ons: []}.
  - Requirement C2: Allow tenant admin to set account-wide default variant with override per-route.
  - Acceptance criteria: Console flow completed in 3 clicks; SDK accepts variant_selector and runtime uses variant routing.

D. Runtime routing and SLOs
  - Requirement D1: Runtime must support preference-aware routing: try preferred variant, fallback to compatible variant when capacity constrained.
  - Requirement D2: Enforce SLOs for premium add-ons: routing must meet latency budget or mark request downgraded and bill accordingly.
  - Acceptance criteria: Canary tests show correct fallback behavior and billing label 'downgraded' in telemetry.

E. Observability and telemetry
  - Requirement E1: New telemetry fields: variant_id, add_on_ids, price_modifier_basispts, billed_unit_cost, routing_decision.
  - Acceptance criteria: Dashboards show per-variant token consumption and add-on revenue.

Implementation details and examples:
- Catalog schema (prose):
  - variant_id: string (eg vm-int8-202609)
  - variant_type: one of [base, quantized, distilled, latency-optimized, safety-filtered]
  - cost_index: numeric relative cost factor compared to base (1.0 = base)
  - expected_quality_delta_pct: -2.5 (meaning expected 2.5% quality delta vs base)
  - compatibility_matrix: list of model families and max_seq_length

- Example API usage (Console and SDK):
  - REST body example (represented as text):
    POST /v1/requests
    {
      "model": "redwood-alpha-13b",
      "variant_selector": {"variant_id":"vm-int8-202609","add_ons":["warm_pool_hour","premium_routing"]},
      "input": "summarize: ..."
    }
  - Expected response includes: request_id, chosen_variant_id, billed_unit_cost (per-token)

Pricing matrix (example):
- Variant-level modifiers (relative to base rate):
  | Variant type           | Cost modifier | Typical use case                     |
  |------------------------|---------------|---------------------------------------|
  | base (fp16)            | 0 bps         | Standard production                    |
  | int8-quant             | -30%          | Cost-sensitive batching workloads     |
  | int4-quant             | -45%          | High volume, tolerant quality loss    |
  | distilled-small        | -55%          | UI companion responses, cheap reps    |
  | latency-optimized      | +40%          | Real-time assistants, sub-150ms SLO   |
  | safety-filtered        | +10%          | Regulated outputs with extra checks   |

- Add-on SKUs (examples):
  | SKU                  | Meter type    | Unit price example | Billing rule             |
  |----------------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
  | warm_pool_hour       | per-hour      | $0.30 / vCPU-hour  | prorated hourly per-tenant|
  | premium_routing_hour | per-hour      | $1.50 / tenant-hr  | only billed if routed to premium pool|
  | reduced_token_cost_pct | percentage  | policy-based       | reduces base token price by X% for route|

Billing behavior rules (examples):
1) If a request is chosen for variant vm-int8-202609 and uses premium_routing, line items: tokens_used @ base_rate * (1-0.30) and add-on: premium_routing_hour prorated.
2) If premium routing fails and fallback occurs, mark request as billed_with_fallback and use fallback variant modifier. Audit trail must show original requested_variant and resolved_variant.

Console mock flow (text):
1) Deployment -> Edit -> Variant selector: choose tab "Marketplace".
2) Filter by Cost or Latency -> pick variant -> optional checkboxes for add-ons.
3) Confirm changes; preview expected per-request unit cost.

Rollout plan and timeline (12 weeks post-approval):
- Week 0-2: Schema, billing pipeline prototyping, and Console mocks. Owner: Product + Billing infra.
- Week 3-5: Backend implementation (catalog, API changes) + runtime routing hooks. Owner: Eng-serving-runtime.
- Week 6-8: End-to-end integration, billing sandbox, and telemetry dashboards. Owner: Eng-platform + Finance.
- Week 9-10: Pilot with 3 customers (account managers + customer success). Collect feedback and reconcile billing.
- Week 11-12: GA rollout, documentation, self-serve enablement, and SDK updates.

Operational playbook (during pilot):
1) Enable feature flag per-tenant. 2) Monitor telemetry dashboard for billed_unit_cost anomalies. 3) Finance runs daily batch invoice reconciliation for pilot tenants. 4) Roll back feature flag if billing mismatch > 0.1% for 48h.

Open questions and trade-offs:
- Price modeling: use percentage modifiers vs absolute per-token delta. Percentages are easier for multi-model families but can be less precise for extremely large models.
- Warm pool allocation: dedicated warm pools per-tenant vs shared premium pools with tagging. Dedicated pools give predictable perf but higher fixed cost.
- Variant discovery: include quality evaluations and client-facing examples in Console or link to separate eval pages?

Risk matrix and mitigations:
- Risk: Incorrect billing mapping between variant and line item -> Mitigation: End-to-end sandbox reconciliation and daily alert on mismatches.
- Risk: Customers accidentally select a high-cost variant -> Mitigation: Console shows warning and needs 2 clicks to confirm for variants that increase cost by >25%.
- Risk: Performance regressions on quantized variants -> Mitigation: model-onboarding must include perf/quality gates in quantization-profiles pipeline.

Ownership and stakeholders:
- Product owner: Ava Chen (product)
- Engineering owners: Runtime (Kiran Patel), Billing infra (Diego Morales), Console (Hannah Park)
- Finance reviewer: Priya Iyer
- Customer success: Maya Singh (pilot coordination)

Related artifacts and references:
- Catalog model-onboarding checklist: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding
- Billing schema spec: /confluence/finance-and-legal/contracts-and-legal-process/billing-schema-spec
- Quantization profiles and test harness: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles

Appendix: Example reconciliation checklist for pilot customers:
- Nightly job compares billed line items to telemetry token counts grouped by variant_id.
- Tolerance threshold: 0.1% token mismatch; if exceeded notify product + finance and pause feature for tenant.

Notes:
- This PRD intentionally separates catalog discovery (product-facing) from long-term marketplace commerce (third-party models). Phase 2 will evaluate an open marketplace after core billing and telemetry are stable.

- This PRD intentionally separates catalog discovery (product-facing) from long-term marketplace commerce (third-party models). Phase 2 will evaluate an open marketplace after core billing and telemetry are stable.

title: PRD: Shadow Evaluator — Prompt-set Driven Regression Detector
preview:
Overview

Summary:
The Shadow Evaluator is a product initiative to provide an automated, prompt-set driven regression detection service that runs shadow (non-production-impacting) evaluations against candidate model variants prior to rollouts. The goal is to catch regressions in real-world prompt patterns and alert product and model owners with actionable diagnostics before customer-facing traffic is exposed.

This PRD defines user journeys, functional and non-functional requirements, high-level design, rollout plan, success metrics, and operational run/playbook for the feature.

Motivation and context:
- Customers and internal teams increasingly rely on model variant rollouts and automated canaries. Existing unit-test and lab evals miss distributional edge-cases embedded in customer prompt-sets.
- We need an evaluation layer that: 1) reuses customer prompt-sets (curated + captured), 2) runs candidate model variants in shadow mode, 3) computes fidelity and quality deltas against baseline, and 4) returns prioritized regressions with reproducible evidence.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Detect regressions that would have impacted customer-facing traffic with at least 80% precision and 60% recall in pilot customers for top-5 critical prompt-sets.
- Provide per-prompt diagnostic payloads (inputs, baseline output, candidate output, diff highlights, metric breakdown).
- Integrate with Console to surface alerts in the rollout workflow and with Optimize to suggest mitigations (roll back, lower-fidelity fallback, quantized variant).
- Low operational cost: shadow eval should not increase serving cost for hosted traffic more than 2% at typical canary scales.

Non-goals:
- This is not a full replacement for offline, human-in-the-loop evaluation for safety-critical models.
- We will not initially support multimodal prompt-sets (images, audio) — text-only for v1.

User stories:
- As a platform engineer, I want to register a production prompt-set and schedule nightly shadow runs so regressions are flagged before deploys.
- As a product manager, I want to see a ranked list of prompt regressions (with confidence scores, token-level diffs, and sample traces) after a candidate model build completes.
- As an SRE, I want the evaluators to run in isolated capacity pools (dedicated or preemptible) and to automatically fall back to a lower-cost simulation mode if capacity is constrained.

Functional requirements:
1) Prompt-set ingestion:
   - Support three sources: curated prompt libraries (internal), tenant-provided prompt-sets (customer uploads or pinned route sets), and captured production samples (anonymized and consented).
   - Each prompt-set must have metadata: owner_team, sensitivity (low/medium/high), intended route(s), and acceptance test markers.

2) Evaluation runs:
   - Support two run modes: fast-preview (sampled subset, <1000 prompts) and full-run (entire prompt-set).
   - Shadow runs execute candidate model + baseline model in parallel, capturing synchronous responses, latency, and token-level logs.
   - Default sampling policy: stratified sampling across route and prompt-length buckets; configurable per prompt-set.

3) Metrics and detectors:
   - Compute per-prompt and aggregated metrics: ROUGE-L, BLEU, embedding similarity (cosine), perplexity ratio, hallucination score (heuristic), latency delta, and token-count delta.
   - Regression rules: alert when top-level metric delta crosses configured thresholds (example table below).

    | Metric | Baseline threshold | Alert delta (default) | Rationale |
    |--------|--------------------|------------------------|-----------|
    | Embedding cosine | >= 0.85 | drop > 0.05 | semantic shift |
    | ROUGE-L | >= 0.3 | drop > 0.07 | content loss |
    | Hallucination score | <= 0.12 | increase > 0.05 | safety risk |
    | Latency (p95) | <= 300ms | increase > 150ms | perf regression |
4) Ranking and prioritization:
   - Rank regressions by severity score = weighted sum(metric deltas * traffic weight). Provide top-20 actionable items.

5) Evidence and reproducibility:
   - For any flagged prompt, provide a reproducible artifact bundle (input, seed/context where applicable, baseline output, candidate output, evaluation metrics, and deterministic invocation parameters).
   - Bundles must be downloadable via Console and accessible via an internal S3 path.

6) Integration points:
   - Console: Alert tile in rollout page with quick actions (pause rollout, rollback, annotate, open issue).
   - CI/CD: API to run shadow-evaluator as a post-build check with wait/timeout options.
   - Alerts: send to Slack on high-severity regressions and create JIRA ticket when the regression persists for >24h.

Non-functional requirements:
- Data residency: prompt-set copies used by the evaluator must respect tenant residency and retention policies.
- Cost cap: default daily spend per prompt-set in hosted is $X (configurable per-customer) — initial pilot use will default to $50/day limit.
- Isolation: evaluation compute must not share GPU instances with customer production serving nodes.
- Determinism: where models are deterministic (seeded), runs should be reproducible; otherwise, log RNG seeds and sampling params.

Success metrics and KPIs:
- Time to detect (median): <6 hours from candidate model availability for fast-preview runs.
- True positive precision on pilot tenants: >=80% for top-100 regressions.
- Operator action rate: >=60% of high-severity alerts lead to a human decision (rollback, hold, or accept) within 48h.
- Cost impact: additional serving cost <=2% for pilot traffic.

High-level design:
- Components:
  - Prompt-store: metadata catalog and secure storage for prompt-sets.
  - Runner fleet: dedicated evaluation GPU pool (or CPU fallback) that executes paired inference jobs (baseline + candidate).
  - Metrics engine: computes deltas and applies regression rules (configurable via YAML rules).
  - Evidence builder: compiles reproducible bundles and stores artifacts in object storage.
  - Alerting & UX: Console integration, Slack/JIRA hooks, and CI/CD API gateway.

Data flow (simplified):
1. Prompt-set registered -> prompt-store validates metadata and triggers ingestion pipeline (anonymization, bucketing).
2. Candidate model build completes -> CI calls shadow-evaluator API to schedule fast-preview run.
3. Runner fleet executes paired invocations; metrics recorded to metrics engine.
4. Metrics engine applies rules, ranks regressions, and generates evidence bundles.
5. Alerts posted and Console renders diagnostics.

Operational details and runbook excerpt:
- Running ad-hoc evaluation (CLI):
  redwood eval run --prompt-set id:ps-1234 --model candidate:2026-09-01 --mode fast-preview --max-prompts 500 --timeout 3600

- Sample evaluation rules YAML (stored in config service):
  name: "default-shadow-rules"
  rules:
    - metric: "embedding_cosine"
      comparator: "decrease_gt"
    - metric: "rouge_l"
      comparator: "decrease_gt"
    - metric: "hallucination_score"
      comparator: "increase_gt"

- Evidence bundle layout (S3 path example):
  s3://redwood-evidence/ps-1234/run-2026-09-01/case-42/
    - input.txt
    - baseline.json (text + token-level log)
    - candidate.json (text + token-level log)
    - metrics.yaml
    - diff.txt

Rollout plan and milestones:
- Phase 0 (Q3 2026): internal pilot with 3 product teams, limited prompt-sets, manual review flows.
- Phase 1 (Q4 2026): beta for 10 external customers (opt-in), Console integration, Slack alerts.
- Phase 2 (Q1 2027): GA with tenant-configurable rules, billing controls, and CI/CD plugin.

Acceptance criteria for GA:
- End-to-end tests demonstrating detection of injected regressions in curated prompt-sets.
- Console UI surfaces alerts and allows at least two remediation actions.
- API SLA: evaluation job accepted within 5 minutes, fast-preview median completion <6 hours.
- Security review passed (data residency and PII handling).

Monitoring, SLOs and alerts:
- Evaluation job queue: p95 queuing time <10m (SLO for GA).
- Runner failure rate: <1% per-run. Alert if failure rate >2% for 30m.
- False positive rate (pilot): tracked but no strict SLA — aim <20% for top-100.

Security and privacy:
- Only prompt-sets marked as "capturable" will auto-include production samples. Customer opt-out respected.
- PII scrubbing pipeline with configurable redaction rules applied during ingestion.
- Evidence bundles for tenant-owned prompt-sets are access-restricted and only available to tenant and authorized Redwood teams.

Backward compatibility and migrations:
- Existing eval and lab pipelines remain unchanged. Shadow Evaluator exposes opt-in APIs that do not alter the baseline rollout behavior unless an operator triggers an automated rollback action.

UI/Console considerations (brief):
- Rollout page: new "Shadow Evaluator" card showing recent runs, top regressions, sample diffs, and quick actions.
- Prompt-set catalog: allow tagging and ownership, show last-run status, and cost spend.

Developer API examples (HTTP):
POST /api/v1/evals/run
Headers: Authorization: Bearer <token>
Body (JSON):
{
  "prompt_set_id": "ps-1234",
  "candidate_model": "candidate:2026-09-01",
  "baseline_model": "production:2026-08-15",
  "mode": "fast-preview",
  "max_prompts": 1000
}

(Responses will include run_id and pre-signed links to evidence bundles when complete.)

Open questions (to resolve in design review):
- Default weighting strategy for severity score and how tenant traffic weights are computed.
- Exact list and definitions for hallucination heuristics that will be used across model families.
- Cost metering model for large prompt-sets (per-prompt vs per-run).

Ownership and contacts:
- Product lead: Aisha Kapoor
- Engineering owner: Marcus Lee (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE contact: Leah Thompson (eng-sre)
- Security reviewer: Daniel Chen (security-and-compliance)

Related pages:
- /confluence/product-docs/requirements/lightweight-eval-capture-and-lossy-metrics-prd-2025-11-20
- /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/shadow-run-benchmarking-notes

Appendix: sample alert workflow
1. Shadow run completes -> high severity regression detected for prompt-set ps-1234.
2. Console alert posted and Slack message to #rollouts with run_id and top-3 cases.
3. Automated ticket created in JIRA (P0 if severity > 0.8) and assigned to model owner.
4. Model owner inspects evidence bundle, decides to rollback — selects quick-action in Console.

Notes:
- This PRD intentionally scopes v1 to text-only prompt-sets and to a hosted-first experience; private/dedicated customers can opt into variant execution in Private via a separate packaging agreement and client connector.

End of document."

title: PRD: Latency Budgeting, Customer Contracts, and First‑Party SLOs
preview:
Summary
-------
This PRD defines the product scope, customer contracts, and operational controls required to introduce first‑party Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for latency across Redwood’s Hosted and Dedicated surfaces. The feature — Latency Budgeting & Contracting — lets customers: (a) declare per‑route latency targets, (b) bind those targets to billing/credit commitments, and (c) opt into automated route shaping and fallback policies that protect higher‑value traffic.

Goals and non‑goals
------------------
Primary goals:
- Provide a clear, enforceable lifecycle for per‑route latency SLOs (declare → observe → enforce → invoice).
- Enable commercial contracts that map latency tiers to pricing credits and capacity reservations.
- Offer runtime controls (shaping, prefetched capacity, graceful degradation) so customers achieve SLOs without manual operations.

Non‑goals for this PRD:
- Full multi‑region, cross‑tenant capacity arbitration (handled in a follow‑on).
- Provider‑level network SLA enforcement (we will expose metrics and guidance; third‑party network SLAs remain external).

Background and user problems
----------------------------
Customers building latency‑sensitive features (chat, real‑time assistants, search‑like reranking) need predictable p95/p99 latency for specific API routes. Currently Redwood provides aggregate latency dashboards and per‑model profiles, but lacks:
1) a contractable SLO primitive customers can sign up to,
2) the connection between latency SLOs and billing/credit instruments, and
3) runtime routing guarantees (e.g., preferential scheduling, dedicated pools, or throttled fallbacks) that respect commercial tiers.

User personas impacted
- Platform Engineer (Enterprise customer): needs SLO guarantees to satisfy internal SLAs.
- Product Manager (startup): needs cost/latency predictability to price SLAs to their users.
- Redwood Sales/CS: needs packaging and negotiation levers tied to system features.

Success metrics (measure over first 6 months)
- 20 paying customers adopt latency SLOs within 6 months of GA.
- Average p95 violation rate <= 1.5% for routes subscribed to an SLO.
- Revenue uplift: SLO‑linked credits and reserved capacity contribute +8% ARR for Dedicated bookings tied to SLOs.
- Reduction in manual support escalations for latency issues of 40% for customers with SLOs.

Product requirements (must/have/should)
-------------------------------------
MUST:
1. SLO declaration API: customers can create/update/delete per‑route SLOs (targets: p50/p95/p99, SLA window: 1h/24h/30d).
2. Contract binding: each active SLO must be bindable to a billing object (credit bucket, reserved slot, or enterprise contract id).
3. Observability: time‑series metrics for SLO windows (latency distribution, request counts, violations) surfaced in Console and via API.
4. Runtime enforcement knobs: preferential queueing, dedicated pool assignment, and automatic fallbacks to compatible model variants.
5. Audit trail: all SLO changes and enforcement events must be logged (who, what, when).

SHOULD:
1. Provide a developer preview CLI for self‑serve SLOs on Hosted plans.
2. Suggest automated remediation actions (recommended capacity or batch size) from Redwood Optimize.
3. Map violations to billing adjustments (credit refunds or SLAs) as a configurable policy per contract.

COULD (nice‑to‑have):
1. Per‑tenant weight‑based traffic shaping across shared pools to prioritize SLOed tenants dynamically.
2. Time‑of‑day scheduled SLOs for seasonal workloads.

API surface (examples)
---------------------
Example request to declare an SLO (POST /v1/slo):

```json
{
  "route": "/v1/chat/completions",
  "target_ms": 150,
  "percentile": 95,
  "evaluation_period": "24h",
  "billing_binding": "contract_12345",
  "policy": { "fallback_model": "redwood-medium-quant-v2", "prefer_dedicated_pool": true }
}
```

Example response (201):
```json
{ "slo_id": "slo_67890", "status": "active" }
```

Observability and dashboards
---------------------------
Console pages and API endpoints must expose:
- SLO summary card: active SLOs, current p95/p99, current violation rate.
- Historical windowed chart: latency percentile over evaluation period with annotated violations.
- Per‑route heatmap: requests by model variant and node pool (hosted/dedicated).
- Alerting: default alert on >3 violations in 24h (integrations: PagerDuty, Slack, email).

Operational design and runtime behaviors
--------------------------------------
1) Enforcement order: routing decisions apply in this order — dedicated pool assignment → preferential queueing → KV/prefix cache warming → fallback variant.
2) Preferential queueing: SLOed traffic assigned to dedicated front‑line tokens with a separate elastic window governed by a token bucket (parameters defined per SLO).
3) Fallbacks: when latency or queue depth threatens to violate the SLO, traffic is routed to preapproved fallback model variants with documented quality downgrade.
4) Reservation: customers can optionally reserve a fixed number of concurrent slots or throughput on Dedicated to guarantee headroom.

Operational runbook (high level)
1. Incident detection: alerts trigger if violation rate rises > threshold.
2. Automated mitigation: route shaping and fallback are applied automatically if enabled.
3. Owner escalation: if automated mitigation fails, page oncall SRE and assigned account success engineer.
4. Post‑incident: generate incident summary and credit calculations if contractual thresholds were missed.

Table: Configuration fields and constraints
-----------------------------------------
| Field | Type | Allowed values / notes | Required |
| Route | string | API route or tag (e.g., "/v1/chat/*") | yes |
| Percentile | int | 50, 95, 99 | yes |
| Target_ms | int | 20–5000 (server enforced max) | yes |
| Evaluation_period | string | 1h, 24h, 30d | yes |
| Billing_binding | string | contract id or credit bucket | yes |
| Prefer_dedicated_pool | bool | prefers dedicated; subject to capacity | no |
| Fallback_model | string | model variant id | no |
| Auto_mitigation | bool | enables runtime shaping & fallback | no |

Pricing and commercial mapping
-----------------------------
We will expose three latency tiers for commercial packaging:
- Bronze (best effort): no credit binding, no guaranteed capacity.
- Silver (SLO credits): per‑route credit commitment with best‑effort preferential routing.
- Gold (reserved capacity): monthly reserved throughput/concurrency + guaranteed routing and limited SLA credits on violation.

Pricing implications (example mapping):
| Tier | Commitment | Typical uplift vs base | Violation credit |
| Bronze | none | 0% | none |
| Silver | monthly credit purchase (tokens) | +12–25% | credits refunded pro‑rata on violation |
| Gold | reserved capacity (monthly/annual) | +35–60% | up to 5% service credit on sustained breach |

Note: final commercial terms will be negotiated; the above are guidance for sales packaging and quoting tools.

Rollout plan and milestones
---------------------------
Phase 0 — Discovery & infra prototyping (Q2 2026)
- Validation with 5 pilot customers.
- Prototype preferential queueing in a canary pool.

Phase 1 — Developer preview (Q3 2026)
- Public API for SLO declaration on Hosted (self‑serve bronze/silver).
- Console UX for SLO summaries.
- Redwood Optimize suggestions integrated in console.

Phase 2 — Enterprise GA (Q4 2026)
- Gold tier (reserved capacity) with contract binding, billing integration, and formal SLA credits.
- Dedicated pool orchestration with tenancy and quota mapping.

Phase 3 — Cross‑region & hybrid capacity arbitration (2027)
- Dynamic weight‑based shaping across shared pools and region failover support.

Security, privacy and compliance notes
------------------------------------
- All SLO definitions and contract bindings are metadata only — no user PII is stored in SLO configs.
- Audit logs for SLO changes are retained per customer retention policy and accessible via the Console and API.
- For regulated customers (HIPAA, SOC2+), SLOs bound to Private deployments must be enforced only within VPC/on‑prem resources.

Open risks and mitigations
-------------------------
- Risk: Overpromising SLOs when shared capacity is saturated. Mitigation: conservative capacity admission controls and mandatory reservation for Gold.
- Risk: Complexity in credit calculations and billing disputes. Mitigation: publish deterministic calculation formulas and provide near‑real‑time violation reports.
- Risk: Quality regressions when falling back to quantized variants. Mitigation: include fallback quality estimate and require approval for fallback models in contract.

Ownership and org impacts
------------------------
- Product: Priya Natarajan (product owner) — requirements, roadmap, go‑to‑market packaging.
- Eng Infrastructure / Serving Runtime: Diego Alvarez — runtime enforcement, queueing, pool assignment.
- Observability: Maya Chen — metrics, dashboards, alerts.
- SRE / Oncall: Liam O'Connor — runbooks and incident handling.
- Sales / CS: Rebecca Lim — commercial packaging and pilot management.

Support playbook for Sales/CS (summary)
-------------------------------------
1. Intake: collect route list, expected QPS, current p95 baseline, and desired tier.
2. Pilot: enable Silver credits and run a 2‑week pilot with telemetry.
3. Quote: if pilot shows >80% success, propose Gold reserved capacity & contract.
4. Post‑sale: monthly review, violation reports, and optimization suggestions.

Appendix A — Violation calculation pseudo‑logic
---------------------------------------------
1. For each evaluation_period, compute percentile latency using server‑side histogram buckets.
2. If observed_percentile_ms > target_ms, mark window as violation.
3. Continuous violation windows beyond X (configurable, default X=3) trigger SLA credit calculation and CS notification.

Appendix B — Example CLI (preview)
---------------------------------
# create SLO
redwood slo create --route /v1/chat/completions --percentile 95 --target-ms 150 --evaluation-period 24h --contract contract_12345 --auto-mitigation

Appendix C — Related artifacts and next steps
---------------------------------------------
- Design spec: latency‑tiered routing (implementation details) — link in related_pages.
- Billing integration spec (finance): to finalize credit accounting models.
- Pilot checklist: include data retention, approval, and rollback criteria.

Questions for review
--------------------
- Should Silver tier include a soft reservation token to reduce noisy neighbor risk?
- What is the maximum number of active Gold reservations we can support per Dedicated pool without rearchitecting allocator?
- Which teams will own sales negotiations for bespoke SLO credit formulas?

Revision history
----------------
- 2026‑04‑22: initial draft (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026‑05‑18: added pricing guidance and pilot plan (reviewers: Diego, Maya).
- 2026‑06‑10: incorporated SRE runbook updates and CLI preview (final review).
text:
Summary
-------
This PRD defines the product scope, customer contracts, and operational controls required to introduce first‑party Service Level Objectives (SLOs) for latency across Redwood’s Hosted and Dedicated surfaces. The feature — Latency Budgeting & Contracting — lets customers: (a) declare per‑route latency targets, (b) bind those targets to billing/credit commitments, and (c) opt into automated route shaping and fallback policies that protect higher‑value traffic.

Goals and non‑goals
------------------
Primary goals:
- Provide a clear, enforceable lifecycle for per‑route latency SLOs (declare → observe → enforce → invoice).
- Enable commercial contracts that map latency tiers to pricing credits and capacity reservations.
- Offer runtime controls (shaping, prefetched capacity, graceful degradation) so customers achieve SLOs without manual operations.

Non‑goals for this PRD:
- Full multi‑region, cross‑tenant capacity arbitration (handled in a follow‑on).
- Provider‑level network SLA enforcement (we will expose metrics and guidance; third‑party network SLAs remain external).

Background and user problems
----------------------------
Customers building latency‑sensitive features (chat, real‑time assistants, search‑like reranking) need predictable p95/p99 latency for specific API routes. Currently Redwood provides aggregate latency dashboards and per‑model profiles, but lacks:
1) a contractable SLO primitive customers can sign up to,
2) the connection between latency SLOs and billing/credit instruments, and
3) runtime routing guarantees (e.g., preferential scheduling, dedicated pools, or throttled fallbacks) that respect commercial tiers.

User personas impacted
- Platform Engineer (Enterprise customer): needs SLO guarantees to satisfy internal SLAs.
- Product Manager (startup): needs cost/latency predictability to price SLAs to their users.
- Redwood Sales/CS: needs packaging and negotiation levers tied to system features.

Success metrics (measure over first 6 months)
- 20 paying customers adopt latency SLOs within 6 months of GA.
- Average p95 violation rate <= 1.5% for routes subscribed to an SLO.
- Revenue uplift: SLO‑linked credits and reserved capacity contribute +8% ARR for Dedicated bookings tied to SLOs.
- Reduction in manual support escalations for latency issues of 40% for customers with SLOs.

Product requirements (must/have/should)
-------------------------------------
MUST:
1. SLO declaration API: customers can create/update/delete per‑route SLOs (targets: p50/p95/p99, SLA window: 1h/24h/30d).
2. Contract binding: each active SLO must be bindable to a billing object (credit bucket, reserved slot, or enterprise contract id).
3. Observability: time‑series metrics for SLO windows (latency distribution, request counts, violations) surfaced in Console and via API.
4. Runtime enforcement knobs: preferential queueing, dedicated pool assignment, and automatic fallbacks to compatible model variants.
5. Audit trail: all SLO changes and enforcement events must be logged (who, what, when).

SHOULD:
1. Provide a developer preview CLI for self‑serve SLOs on Hosted plans.
2. Suggest automated remediation actions (recommended capacity or batch size) from Redwood Optimize.
3. Map violations to billing adjustments (credit refunds or SLAs) as a configurable policy per contract.

COULD (nice‑to‑have):
1. Per‑tenant weight‑based traffic shaping across shared pools to prioritize SLOed tenants dynamically.
2. Time‑of‑day scheduled SLOs for seasonal workloads.

API surface (examples)
---------------------
Example request to declare an SLO (POST /v1/slo):

```json
{
  "route": "/v1/chat/completions",
  "target_ms": 150,
  "percentile": 95,
  "evaluation_period": "24h",
  "billing_binding": "contract_12345",
  "policy": { "fallback_model": "redwood-medium-quant-v2", "prefer_dedicated_pool": true }
}
```

Example response (201):
```json
{ "slo_id": "slo_67890", "status": "active" }
```

Observability and dashboards
---------------------------
Console pages and API endpoints must expose:
- SLO summary card: active SLOs, current p95/p99, current violation rate.
- Historical windowed chart: latency percentile over evaluation period with annotated violations.
- Per‑route heatmap: requests by model variant and node pool (hosted/dedicated).
- Alerting: default alert on >3 violations in 24h (integrations: PagerDuty, Slack, email).

Operational design and runtime behaviors
--------------------------------------
1) Enforcement order: routing decisions apply in this order — dedicated pool assignment → preferential queueing → KV/prefix cache warming → fallback variant.
2) Preferential queueing: SLOed traffic assigned to dedicated front‑line tokens with a separate elastic window governed by a token bucket (parameters defined per SLO).
3) Fallbacks: when latency or queue depth threatens to violate the SLO, traffic is routed to preapproved fallback model variants with documented quality downgrade.
4) Reservation: customers can optionally reserve a fixed number of concurrent slots or throughput on Dedicated to guarantee headroom.

Operational runbook (high level)
1. Incident detection: alerts trigger if violation rate rises > threshold.
2. Automated mitigation: route shaping and fallback are applied automatically if enabled.
3. Owner escalation: if automated mitigation fails, page oncall SRE and assigned account success engineer.
4. Post‑incident: generate incident summary and credit calculations if contractual thresholds were missed.

Table: Configuration fields and constraints
-----------------------------------------
| Field | Type | Allowed values / notes | Required |
| Route | string | API route or tag (e.g., "/v1/chat/*") | yes |
| Percentile | int | 50, 95, 99 | yes |
| Target_ms | int | 20–5000 (server enforced max) | yes |
| Evaluation_period | string | 1h, 24h, 30d | yes |
| Billing_binding | string | contract id or credit bucket | yes |
| Prefer_dedicated_pool | bool | prefers dedicated; subject to capacity | no |
| Fallback_model | string | model variant id | no |
| Auto_mitigation | bool | enables runtime shaping & fallback | no |

Pricing and commercial mapping
-----------------------------
We will expose three latency tiers for commercial packaging:
- Bronze (best effort): no credit binding, no guaranteed capacity.
- Silver (SLO credits): per‑route credit commitment with best‑effort preferential routing.
- Gold (reserved capacity): monthly reserved throughput/concurrency + guaranteed routing and limited SLA credits on violation.

Pricing implications (example mapping):
| Tier | Commitment | Typical uplift vs base | Violation credit |
| Bronze | none | 0% | none |
| Silver | monthly credit purchase (tokens) | +12–25% | credits refunded pro‑rata on violation |
| Gold | reserved capacity (monthly/annual) | +35–60% | up to 5% service credit on sustained breach |

Note: final commercial terms will be negotiated; the above are guidance for sales packaging and quoting tools.

Rollout plan and milestones
---------------------------
Phase 0 — Discovery & infra prototyping (Q2 2026)
- Validation with 5 pilot customers.
- Prototype preferential queueing in a canary pool.

Phase 1 — Developer preview (Q3 2026)
- Public API for SLO declaration on Hosted (self‑serve bronze/silver).
- Console UX for SLO summaries.
- Redwood Optimize suggestions integrated in console.

Phase 2 — Enterprise GA (Q4 2026)
- Gold tier (reserved capacity) with contract binding, billing integration, and formal SLA credits.
- Dedicated pool orchestration with tenancy and quota mapping.

Phase 3 — Cross‑region & hybrid capacity arbitration (2027)
- Dynamic weight‑based shaping across shared pools and region failover support.

Security, privacy and compliance notes
------------------------------------
- All SLO definitions and contract bindings are metadata only — no user PII is stored in SLO configs.
- Audit logs for SLO changes are retained per customer retention policy and accessible via the Console and API.
- For regulated customers (HIPAA, SOC2+), SLOs bound to Private deployments must be enforced only within VPC/on‑prem resources.

Open risks and mitigations
-------------------------
- Risk: Overpromising SLOs when shared capacity is saturated. Mitigation: conservative capacity admission controls and mandatory reservation for Gold.
- Risk: Complexity in credit calculations and billing disputes. Mitigation: publish deterministic calculation formulas and provide near‑real‑time violation reports.
- Risk: Quality regressions when falling back to quantized variants. Mitigation: include fallback quality estimate and require approval for fallback models in contract.

Ownership and org impacts
------------------------
- Product: Priya Natarajan (product owner) — requirements, roadmap, go‑to‑market packaging.
- Eng Infrastructure / Serving Runtime: Diego Alvarez — runtime enforcement, queueing, pool assignment.
- Observability: Maya Chen — metrics, dashboards, alerts.
- SRE / Oncall: Liam O'Connor — runbooks and incident handling.
- Sales / CS: Rebecca Lim — commercial packaging and pilot management.

Support playbook for Sales/CS (summary)
-------------------------------------
1. Intake: collect route list, expected QPS, current p95 baseline, and desired tier.
2. Pilot: enable Silver credits and run a 2‑week pilot with telemetry.
3. Quote: if pilot shows >80% success, propose Gold reserved capacity & contract.
4. Post‑sale: monthly review, violation reports, and optimization suggestions.

Appendix A — Violation calculation pseudo‑logic
---------------------------------------------
1. For each evaluation_period, compute percentile latency using server‑side histogram buckets.
2. If observed_percentile_ms > target_ms, mark window as violation.
3. Continuous violation windows beyond X (configurable, default X=3) trigger SLA credit calculation and CS notification.

Appendix B — Example CLI (preview)
---------------------------------
# create SLO
redwood slo create --route /v1/chat/completions --percentile 95 --target-ms 150 --evaluation-period 24h --contract contract_12345 --auto-mitigation

Appendix C — Related artifacts and next steps
---------------------------------------------
- Design spec: latency‑tiered routing (implementation details) — link in related_pages.
- Billing integration spec (finance): to finalize credit accounting models.
- Pilot checklist: include data retention, approval, and rollback criteria.

Questions for review
--------------------
- Should Silver tier include a soft reservation token to reduce noisy neighbor risk?
- What is the maximum number of active Gold reservations we can support per Dedicated pool without rearchitecting allocator?
- Which teams will own sales negotiations for bespoke SLO credit formulas?

Revision history
----------------
- 2026‑04‑22: initial draft (Priya Natarajan).
- 2026‑05‑18: added pricing guidance and pilot plan (reviewers: Diego, Maya).
- 2026‑06‑10: incorporated SRE runbook updates and CLI preview (final review).

title: Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator — Product Brief and Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This document describes the Conversational SLA Composer and Contract Simulator (SLA Composer). The SLA Composer is a productized authoring UX and backend simulator that lets sales, solutions engineering, and customers design, preview, and validate SLA-driven consumption contracts for conversational workloads. It couples a declarative contract DSL, a simulation runner (SimRunner), and a telemetry-aware estimator to produce latency, cost, and quality projections for candidate contracts before signing.

Goals:
- Provide a single surface for creating vendor- and tenant-facing SLA contracts for conversational applications (chat, interactive assistants, streaming responses).
- Enable realistic pre-sale simulations matching customer's traffic shape to predict p50/p95 latency distribution, token costs, and fallback frequency.
- Reduce contract churn by surfacing trade-offs (batching, warm capacity, quantization, model variant fallthrough) early.
- Drive consistent commercial packaging by codifying guardrails for minimum economic and reliability thresholds.

Scope (what is in/out):
- In scope: Authoring UX in Console, contract DSL v1, SimRunner for synthetic traffic playback and scenario slices, estimator for cost & latency, recommended guardrail presets for Hosted / Dedicated / Private.
- Out of scope for v1: full legal contract generation, automated billing integration, marketplace SKU publication.

High-level components:
1) SLA Composer UX (console plugin): form-based + YAML editor for advanced users. Generates a Contract YAML using the DSL.
2) Contract DSL v1: fields for routes, SLOs (latency percentiles, availability), fidelity buckets, fallback policies, warm capacity commitments, regional affinity.
3) SimRunner: simulator service that replays customer traffic shapes, applies batching/quantization rules, and models multi-tier fallbacks.
4) Estimator: produces cost, projected token count, and economic confidence bands.
5) Report generator: human-readable SLA preview and machine-readable contract artifact.

Key concepts and terminology:
- Route: logical API path (e.g., /chat/complete, /embed/upsert).
- Fidelity bucket: discrete fidelity profile for responses (high/medium/low quantization + variant).
- Warm capacity: reserved pool expressed in vCPU/GPU-hours or credit-equivalent.
- Fallback frequency: expected % of requests that drop to a lower-cost variant or region under load.

User journeys and primary personas:
- Sales: create conservative contract for enterprise prospects, simulate peak-hour load, export contract for SRE review.
- Solutions Engineer: iterate fidelity vs cost trade-offs with customer, validate SLA feasibility on Dedicated.
- Customer Admin: import customer traffic samples and run private simulations in VPC (for Private deployments).

Requirements (table):
| id | requirement | acceptance criteria | priority | owner | notes |
| RQ-001 | Contract DSL supports per-route p50/p95 latency & availability | Composer UI shows projection plots and warnings; DSL produces contract YAML | P0 | product/strategy | Must support p95 latency target and p50 baseline |
| RQ-002 | SimRunner must replay traffic shapes and produce latency histogram (p50/p95/p99) | Results reproducible; run for 1M requests within 4h for typical scenario | P0 | eng-platform | Use existing synthetic-runner infra as base |
| RQ-003 | Estimator must output cost estimate with 90% confidence interval | CI calculated from historical variance + model variance | P1 | finance-infra | Integrate with runtime-cost-models service |
| RQ-004 | Support warm capacity commitment presets for Hosted/Dedicated/Private | Presets available in Console and DSL; show cost delta vs on-demand | P1 | product | Presets editable by solutions engineering |
| RQ-005 | Simulator supports modeling fallbacks to compatible variants and regions | Simulator reports fallback frequency and resulting latencies | P0 | eng-serving-runtime | Need variant compatibility matrix input |

User stories (selected):
- As a Sales rep, I want to draft a contract that guarantees p95 <= 350ms for 95% of requests in US-EAST so I can promise an SLA to the prospect.
- As a Solutions Engineer, I want to adjust fidelity buckets and see immediate cost/latency trade-off previews to pick an economically viable configuration.
- As an SRE reviewer, I want a simulation report attached to the contract that includes expected error rates and fallback counts during burst scenarios.

Acceptance criteria (v1):
1) Console UX allows creating and saving a contract YAML and triggering a simulation.
2) For provided traffic shapes (CSV or JSON trace sample), SimRunner returns latency histograms and cost estimate within 6 hours for 10M effective tokens.
3) Estimator integrates with runtime-cost-models and reports token-level breakdown and warm vs on-demand delta.
4) Generated contract artifact can be exported and consumed by Dedicated provisioning automation (staged API contract).

Design notes and technical approach:
- Contract DSL: YAML-first with strict schema validation via JSONSchema. Example snippet included in Appendix.
- SimRunner: event-driven replay using existing synthetic-runner with extensions for batch-aware scheduling and KV-prefetch modeling. It will call a local runtime emulator that implements batching/quant behavior to estimate latency rather than making live production calls.
- Estimator: bootstrap from historical telemetry per-model and per-instance family (p95 scaling curves), combine with simulation outputs to produce cost confidence intervals.
- Privacy: for Private deployments, traffic sampling runs in-customer VPC using an ephemeral SimRunner worker; no traces leave the VPC unless customer opts in.

Operational SLOs for SLA Composer itself:
- Composer UX availability: 99.9% during business hours.
- SimRunner job completion: 95% of jobs complete successfully within target window (6h for 10M tokens).
- Simulation reproducibility: same input + seed -> within 5% variation on p95 latency for stable configs.

Rollout plan and milestones:
1) Alpha (Q3 2026): internal-only Console plugin + SimRunner prototype, limited to Sales + SE.
2) Private beta (Q4 2026): enable Private deployment simulation in customer VPCs; collect feedback.
3) Public beta (Q1 2027): full Console flow live in Hosted with standard presets and exports to Dedicated provisioning.
4) GA (Q2 2027): billing hooks, legal artifact export, and partner-facing templates.

Ownership and stakeholders:
- Product lead: Priya Natarajan (product) — overall roadmap and business acceptance.
- Tech lead: Luca Martinez (eng-platform) — SimRunner and estimator integration.
- UX: Maya Chen (dev-ex) — Console workflows and reporting.
- Finance integration: Aaron Patel (finance-legal) — cost model calibration and commercial presets.

Telemetry, metrics, and dashboards:
- Per-contract simulation dashboard: latency distributions, fallback heatmaps, token cost breakdown.
- Adoption metrics: contracts created, simulations run, time-to-first-simulation, simulation-to-contract conversion rate.
- Accuracy metrics: historical predicted vs observed p95 error and cost delta; tracked quarterly to recalibrate models.

Security and compliance considerations:
- Private simulations run entirely inside customer network by default. When customer provides traces, we require PII-scrubbing by SE before ingestion unless customer explicitly accepts otherwise.
- Contract artifacts stored encrypted at rest with tenant scoping.

Example Contract DSL (short snippet):
---
routes:
  - name: chat.complete
    path: /v1/chat/complete
    traffic_shape: { peak_qps: 1200, mean_seq_len: 420, burst_minutes: 10 }
    slos:
      p95_latency_ms: 350
      availability_pct: 99.95
    fidelity_buckets:
      - name: gold
        quant: fp16
        model_variant: rdx-llama-38b-v2
        percent_traffic: 80
      - name: silver
        quant: int8
        model_variant: rdx-llama-7b-q8
        percent_traffic: 20
warm_capacity:
  reserved_gpu_hours_per_day: 12
fallback_policy:
  on_capacity_pressure: route_to: silver, max_fallback_pct: 30
---

Appendix: Simulation CLI example
$ rdx-simrunner run --contract contract.yaml --trace sample_trace.json --seed 42 --out report.json

Open questions / risks:
- Accuracy of estimator for novel models (new model families may lack enough telemetry to produce reliable CI). Mitigation: conservative default multipliers and explicit confidence bands.
- Legal acceptance: simulated guarantees are indicative; we must ensure contract wording delineates simulated projections vs guaranteed contractual obligations. Legal to review before GA.
- Cost of running large-scale simulations can be non-trivial; consider crediting model for customer-initiated sims.

Related pages and references:
- Consumption SLA playbook (internal)
- Dedicated provisioning API and contract schema
- Runtime-cost-models service (telemetry & cost calibration)

Next steps (30/60/90):
- 30 days: finalize Contract DSL schema and wire up JSONSchema validation; build minimal Console form.
- 60 days: prototype SimRunner job type and validate against three canonical customer traffic shapes.
- 90 days: integrate estimator and produce the first contract preview report for internal review.

Contact: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai) — for product questions; Luca Martinez (luca@redwood.ai) for engineering/ infrastructure questions.
