title: PRD: Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime — API, SLOs, Pricing, and Rollout
preview:
Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-tenant opt-in, and automatic rollback behavior.

Non-goals:
- Replacing the runtime entirely; initial release will be an optional add-on per route.
- Deep automatic re-writing of prompts to improve batching compatibility (future work).

Background and problem statement:
Redwood currently exposes streaming inference (low-latency, token-by-token) and batched endpoints (optimized throughput) as separate serving modes. Customers with mixed traffic patterns must select one mode per product, build separate retry/fanout logic, or accept suboptimal cost/latency tradeoffs. The hybrid runtime will let us route requests dynamically to streaming or batch execution paths or run a hybrid path that streams early tokens and finishes via a batch path for long responses, reducing cost while maintaining interactive feel.

User stories:
1) As a web chat developer, I want streaming responses for short customer interactions but fall back to batched completion for long-form generation to keep cost predictable.
2) As a platform engineer, I want per-route controls for latency and cost so my team can enforce SLAs.
3) As an SRE, I want telemetry to detect when hybrid routing increases error rates or tail latency so we can auto-roll back to streaming.

Functional requirements (high level):
- API: New request parameter \"execution_mode\" with enum values [\"streaming\", \"batch\", \"hybrid\", \"auto\"]. Default is \"auto\" which uses policy routing.
- Per-route config: Routes may specify target SLOs (p50/p90/p99), cost preference (min-latency | min-cost | balanced), and max-fidelity model set.
- Hybrid behavior: For \"hybrid\" mode, initial tokens must be streamed immediately; the runtime may switch to batch execution for subsequent tokens if batching is available. The switch must be transparent to clients: a single streaming connection/stream ID is preserved.
- Backward compatibility: Existing streaming and batched APIs continue to work with no change.

Non-functional requirements (SLOs & scale):
- Streaming routes: median token latency <= 150ms at p50 under base traffic (defined as <20% of instance concurrency).
- Batched routes: throughput per GPU should improve by at least 1.8x for batched workloads vs streaming-only baseline for 512-token avg sequences.
- Hybrid routes: under mixed traffic, overall end-to-end 95th percentile latency should not exceed streaming-only p95 by more than 30% for the same requests.
- Availability: 99.9% for hosted customers (per existing SLA tiers). Hybrid launch must not degrade global availability.

Telemetry and metrics (required):
For each request/route include the following telemetry fields in Console and exported traces:
- execution_mode_selected: {streaming|batch|hybrid|fallback_streaming|fallback_batch}
- batch_id (if batched) and batch_size
- token_latency_ms (per token sampled at p50/p95)
- end_to_end_latency_ms
- cost_estimate_microtokens (pre- and post-execution)
- fallback_reason (if a fallback occurred: capacity|error|timeout|policy)

API design (examples):
- Request JSON example (hybrid):
{
  \"model\": \"rw-gnl-7b\",
  \"prompt\": \"Summarize the following notes...\",
  \"execution_mode\": \"hybrid\",
  \"route_config\": {
    \"slo_ms\": {\"p50\": 120, \"p95\": 600},
    \"cost_preference\": \"balanced\"
  }
}

- SDK/CLI opt-in toggles: developers can override via headers or SDK flags: X-Redwood-Execution-Mode, redwood.execution.mode.

Price & packaging implications:
- Introduce a \"hybrid\" consumption metric tracked at token-level: tokens_streamed and tokens_batched. Billing will apply existing per-token rates but expose a hybrid surcharge (initial beta) to account for routing overhead: hybrid_surcharge = $0.00002 / request (configurable).
- Revenue transparency: Console must show a per-route breakdown: raw_tokens, batched_tokens, streamed_tokens, estimated_savings_vs_streaming.
- Packaged offering: Hybrid runtime will be included in an \"Optimize Hybrid\" add-on for enterprise + Dedicated customers initially; hosted customers can enable via feature flag and pay metered usage.

Design constraints & trade-offs:
- Preserving streaming semantics is paramount: clients expect partial tokens as they are generated. Any switching must maintain ordering and IDs.
- Batching improves GPU utilization but adds head-of-line wait; hybrid requires predictive routing heuristics to avoid increased tail latency.
- Cost attribution must be reliable enough for billing; conservative rounding required until confidence >95%.

Routing & policy engine (requirements):
- Policies: static (route-level), adaptive (runtime load), and tenant-preference.
- Heuristic examples: switch to batch if (queued_same_model_requests >= threshold && estimated_batch_fill_time < slo_window).
- Fallbacks: if batch capacity unavailable or batch fails, auto-fallback to streaming with a recorded fallback_reason.

Rollout plan (phases):
1) Internal Canary (month 0–1): enable hybrid in infra namespaces; run synthetic traffic patterns and measure regressions vs streaming baseline.
2) Beta customer program (month 2–4): invite 10 enterprise customers; per-tenant opt-in with telemetry dashboards and weekly QBR.
3) Public launch opt-in (month 5): available via Console toggle for all hosted customers; add hybrid_surcharge trial.
4) Default routing (month 8): after telemetry shows stable SLOs and savings, enable \"auto\" routing for select low-risk routes with opt-out.

Migration and compatibility guidance for customers:
- Minimal code change: set execution_mode in existing calls or toggle via Console route settings.
- Best practices: mark latency-critical routes explicitly as \"streaming\"; set balanced cost_preference for conversational UIs.
- Mitigations: provide a migration checklist and traffic simulation tooling in Console to preview cost/latency tradeoffs before enabling hybrid.

Testing & QA strategy:
- Synthetic workloads: short-chat, long-form, mixed sessions; measure p50/p95/p99 latency and cost per 10k requests.
- Chaos tests: simulate failed batch workers, delayed kernels, and verify auto-fallback and no data loss.
- A/B testing: use canary routing and evaluate metrics for 2 weeks with statistical significance thresholds (power >= 0.8).

Security & compliance considerations:
- Maintain existing request isolation guarantees for multi-tenant dedicated customers.
- Ensure hybrid routing does not create new data residency crossovers: batch candidates must be selected within the same region and tenant-scoped pools.
- Audit logging: every decision to switch execution paths must be logged for 30 days (configurable per enterprise).

Ownership & timeline:
- Product owner: Ava Chen (product)
- Engineering lead: Miguel Ortega (eng-serving-runtime)
- SRE lead: Samira Khan (eng-sre)
- Designer: Priya Nair (eng-devex)
- Target milestones:
  - Spec freeze: 2026-05-15
  - Canary build complete: 2026-06-15
  - Beta onboarding starts: 2026-07-01
  - Public opt-in launch: 2026-09-01

Open questions (to resolve in design phase):
- Exact hybrid_surcharge amount and whether it should be waived for early adopters.
- Data model for precise per-token crediting across streamed -> batched transitions.
- UI affordances for displaying intermediate token prices in Console.

Appendix A — example telemetry table (human-readable):
| metric_key | description | unit | ingestion frequency | owner |
|-----------|-------------|------|---------------------|-------|
| execution_mode_selected | mode chosen by policy | enum | per-request | serving-runtime |
| batch_size | number of requests aggregated | count | per-batch | serving-runtime |
| token_latency_ms | per-token latency sample | ms | sampled | observability |
| fallback_reason | why fallback occurred | string | per-fallback | sres |

Appendix B — CLI sample to enable hybrid on a route:
$ redwood routes update --route-id r-12345 --set execution_mode=auto --set policy.cost_preference=balanced

Appendix C — minimal YAML for Dedicated config (example):
route:
  id: conversational-primary
  execution_mode: auto
  policy:
    slo_ms:
      p50: 100
      p95: 500
    cost_preference: balanced

References and related docs:
- Observability & cost/latency tradeoffs guide (internal): ../product-overview/observability-cost-latency-tradeoffs-guide-2025-06-09.json
- Local developer runtime sandbox (design): ../design-specs/local-developer-runtime-sandbox-design-spec-2026-02-12.json

Please review the SLOs and pricing sections carefully. Next steps: engineering scoping session (2026-04-20), finalize telemetry contract (2026-04-30).

Summary:
This PRD defines the product requirements for a Streaming-to-Batch Hybrid Runtime (\"hybrid runtime\") that unifies low-latency streaming and high-throughput batched inference in a single serving surface. The hybrid runtime should allow per-route policies that select streaming, batched, or hybrid execution, provide transparent pricing breakdowns, and preserve existing SLOs for customers during rollout.

Goals (what success looks like):
- Enable a single API surface where developers can request streaming, batched, or hybrid inference with minimal code change.
- Achieve median 50–150ms token latency for streaming routes under light load, and 2–4x cost reduction on high-concurrency transactional workloads via intelligent batching.
- Provide per-request cost and latency attribution in the Console and Billing API to make tradeoffs visible.
- Ship a staged rollout with canary, per-tenant opt-in, and automatic rollback behavior.

Non-goals:
- Replacing the runtime entirely; initial release will be an optional add-on per route.
- Deep automatic re-writing of prompts to improve batching compatibility (future work).

Background and problem statement:
Redwood currently exposes streaming inference (low-latency, token-by-token) and batched endpoints (optimized throughput) as separate serving modes. Customers with mixed traffic patterns must select one mode per product, build separate retry/fanout logic, or accept suboptimal cost/latency tradeoffs. The hybrid runtime will let us route requests dynamically to streaming or batch execution paths or run a hybrid path that streams early tokens and finishes via a batch path for long responses, reducing cost while maintaining interactive feel.

User stories:
1) As a web chat developer, I want streaming responses for short customer interactions but fall back to batched completion for long-form generation to keep cost predictable.
2) As a platform engineer, I want per-route controls for latency and cost so my team can enforce SLAs.
3) As an SRE, I want telemetry to detect when hybrid routing increases error rates or tail latency so we can auto-roll back to streaming.

Functional requirements (high level):
- API: New request parameter \"execution_mode\" with enum values [\"streaming\", \"batch\", \"hybrid\", \"auto\"]. Default is \"auto\" which uses policy routing.
- Per-route config: Routes may specify target SLOs (p50/p90/p99), cost preference (min-latency | min-cost | balanced), and max-fidelity model set.
- Hybrid behavior: For \"hybrid\" mode, initial tokens must be streamed immediately; the runtime may switch to batch execution for subsequent tokens if batching is available. The switch must be transparent to clients: a single streaming connection/stream ID is preserved.
- Backward compatibility: Existing streaming and batched APIs continue to work with no change.

Non-functional requirements (SLOs & scale):

- Batched routes: throughput per GPU should improve by at least 1.8x for batched workloads vs streaming-only baseline for 512-token avg sequences.
- Hybrid routes: under mixed traffic, overall end-to-end 95th percentile latency should not exceed streaming-only p95 by more than 30% for the same requests.
- Availability: 99.9% for hosted customers (per existing SLA tiers). Hybrid launch must not degrade global availability.

Telemetry and metrics (required):
For each request/route include the following telemetry fields in Console and exported traces:
- execution_mode_selected: {streaming|batch|hybrid|fallback_streaming|fallback_batch}
- batch_id (if batched) and batch_size
- token_latency_ms (per token sampled at p50/p95)
- end_to_end_latency_ms
- cost_estimate_microtokens (pre- and post-execution)
- fallback_reason (if a fallback occurred: capacity|error|timeout|policy)

API design (examples):
- Request JSON example (hybrid):
{
  \"model\": \"rw-gnl-7b\",
  \"prompt\": \"Summarize the following notes...\",
  \"execution_mode\": \"hybrid\",
  \"route_config\": {
    \"slo_ms\": {\"p50\": 120, \"p95\": 600},
    \"cost_preference\": \"balanced\"
  }
}

- SDK/CLI opt-in toggles: developers can override via headers or SDK flags: X-Redwood-Execution-Mode, redwood.execution.mode.

- Introduce a \"hybrid\" consumption metric tracked at token-level: tokens_streamed and tokens_batched. Billing will apply existing per-token rates but expose a hybrid surcharge (initial beta) to account for routing overhead: hybrid_surcharge = $0.00002 / request (configurable).
- Revenue transparency: Console must show a per-route breakdown: raw_tokens, batched_tokens, streamed_tokens, estimated_savings_vs_streaming.
- Packaged offering: Hybrid runtime will be included in an \"Optimize Hybrid\" add-on for enterprise + Dedicated customers initially; hosted customers can enable via feature flag and pay metered usage.

Design constraints & trade-offs:

- Batching improves GPU utilization but adds head-of-line wait; hybrid requires predictive routing heuristics to avoid increased tail latency.
- Cost attribution must be reliable enough for billing; conservative rounding required until confidence >95%.

Routing & policy engine (requirements):
- Policies: static (route-level), adaptive (runtime load), and tenant-preference.
- Heuristic examples: switch to batch if (queued_same_model_requests >= threshold && estimated_batch_fill_time < slo_window).
- Fallbacks: if batch capacity unavailable or batch fails, auto-fallback to streaming with a recorded fallback_reason.

Rollout plan (phases):
1) Internal Canary (month 0–1): enable hybrid in infra namespaces; run synthetic traffic patterns and measure regressions vs streaming baseline.
2) Beta customer program (month 2–4): invite 10 enterprise customers; per-tenant opt-in with telemetry dashboards and weekly QBR.
3) Public launch opt-in (month 5): available via Console toggle for all hosted customers; add hybrid_surcharge trial.
4) Default routing (month 8): after telemetry shows stable SLOs and savings, enable \"auto\" routing for select low-risk routes with opt-out.

title: Console Release Notes (Beta) — In-console Usage Insights (Route + Model Cost Breakdown) — 2026-03
preview:
## Summary
We’re rolling out a **beta** of **In-console Usage Insights** in the Redwood Console. This release adds route- and model-level breakdowns to help engineering and finance stakeholders identify the biggest cost drivers (tokens, cache behavior, retries/fallbacks, and context length patterns) and reconcile usage with billing.

**Who this is for:** engineering leads, platform teams, and FinOps partners who need reliable cost attribution by API route and model.

**Where to find it:** Console → **Usage & Cost** → **Usage Insights (Beta)**

---

## What’s included in this beta
### 1) Route + model cost & usage breakdown
You can now view a breakdown such as:
- **Route**: `/v1/chat/completions` (and other Redwood + OpenAI-compat endpoints)
- **Model**: `llama-3.1-70b` (with stable family/version bucketing)

Breakdowns include:
- **Total cost** and **usage** by time bucket
- **Tokens in / tokens out** (and their contribution to spend)
- **Cache**: hit/miss rate and cost impact (where applicable)
- **Retries and fallbacks**: counts and cost impact
- **Context length distribution** (high-level buckets) to surface prompt inflation
- **Latency-correlated spend** indicators (e.g., “slower requests are more expensive” patterns where present)

### 2) “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” insights
The Insights page highlights:
- Top routes/models by cost (ranked)
- Explanations for major deltas (e.g., “token out up 35% WoW”, “cache hit rate down 18 pts”, “model mix shifted to larger model”)

### 3) Exportable reports (CSV)
From any filtered view you can export CSV for:
- Engineering (cost per route/model, token drivers)
- Finance/chargeback workflows (project/workspace level rollups)

Exports are generated server-side and delivered via a signed download URL (with audit logging).

### 4) Data freshness indicator
The page displays a **Last updated** timestamp and shows a banner if data is partial or lagging.

---

## Beta scope and limitations
This beta is intentionally scoped to ensure correctness and performance:
- **Fixed set of dimensions**: time range, workspace/project, environment, deployment mode (where available), region (where available), route, model/version bucket.
- **No scheduled email reports** (planned follow-up).
- **Recommendations only**: Insights link to docs and suggested levers (caching/batching/routing), but the console will not automatically change optimization settings.
- **Retention follows existing telemetry retention** for the workspace. Historical backfill is limited and may be incomplete depending on when route/model tagging became available.

---

## Enabling the beta (allowlist + feature flag)
Usage Insights (Beta) is gated behind a feature flag and account allowlist.

### Option A — For internal teams (Redwood employees)
1) Confirm the target workspace/org ID.
2) File an internal enablement request:
   - Jira: **INT-9132 — Enable Usage Insights beta for account**
   - Include: workspace ID, customer name, deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private), and whether exports are required.
3) Release coordination will update the allowlist and confirm within 1 business day.

### Option B — For Solutions Engineering / CSM-led enables
1) Ensure the customer understands beta constraints (see “Known issues”).
2) Obtain customer approval if exports + audit logging are required.
3) Submit INT-9132 with:
   - Approver (AE/CSM)
   - Security considerations (restricted workspace? private deployment?)
   - Success criteria (what they need to validate in beta)

### Verification
Once enabled, the workspace should see:
- Console → Usage & Cost → **Usage Insights (Beta)**
- A “Beta” badge and a “Last updated” freshness timestamp

---

## Permissions and governance
### Who can see cost
- Cost views are controlled by workspace role + a workspace setting (“Hide cost in console”).
- If a user can’t see cost, the page will show a permission-denied state and guidance to contact an admin.

### Export controls
- CSV exports require the **Cost:Export** permission.
- Export actions generate audit events (who exported, what filters, timestamp, download URL issued).
- Restricted workspaces may have additional export limitations per retention/compliance settings.

---

## Known issues (beta)
We’re tracking the items below as the most common beta blockers.

1) **Totals may not exactly match invoices in edge cases**
   - Symptoms: Console totals differ from invoice totals by a small amount.
   - Typical causes: rounding across time buckets, late-arriving events, or differences in cutoff time.
   - Mitigation: compare using the same time zone + time range; check freshness banner; if drift persists, open Support ticket and reference **SUP-24811**.

2) **Missing route breakdown for some OpenAI-compatible endpoints**
   - Symptoms: requests appear under “Unknown route” or grouped unexpectedly.
   - Root cause: route normalization mapping gaps.
   - Mitigation: upgrade to the latest gateway/SDK versions where applicable; if still missing, reference **SUP-24827**.

3) **Duplicate rows in exports due to model aliases/version bucketing**
   - Symptoms: same model appears multiple times in CSV for the same time bucket.
   - Root cause: alias mapping inconsistencies during the beta window.
   - Mitigation: use the “Model family” filter where available; if blocking, reference **SUP-24844**.

4) **Insights page may time out for long ranges (e.g., 30 days) on large workspaces**
   - Symptoms: query timeout, slow loads, or incomplete table render.
   - Mitigation: use 24h / 7d ranges for interactive analysis; use CSV export for larger ranges; reference **SUP-24863** for persistent performance issues.

5) **Streaming endpoints may have incomplete route tags in some regions (intermittent)**
   - Symptoms: streaming traffic undercounts or is grouped incorrectly.
   - Mitigation: check freshness banner; if the issue is region-specific, escalate via #eng-platform and reference the tracking thread on missing streaming route tags.

---

## Support, feedback, and escalation
- Customer-facing support should use the emerging playbook (in progress): **ENG-3826 — Support playbook and known issues**.
- For suspected correctness issues (billing mismatch, retry attribution ambiguity):
  - Collect workspace ID, time range, route, model, and screenshot of the “Last updated” timestamp.
  - Escalate to the #eng-platform channel and tag **Nadia Rahman** (cost attribution) and **Cole Summers** (billing correctness).

Feedback channel for beta users:
- Console page includes a “Send feedback” link.
- Internally, route feedback to **Marissa Cole** (product) and **Dylan Brooks** (eng lead).

---

## What’s next (toward GA)
We’ll move from beta to GA after we meet these criteria:
- Reconciliation: breakdown totals match billing within tolerance across representative workspaces and deployment modes.
- Performance: interactive query p95 meets targets for 24h/7d ranges.
- Quality: route normalization + model bucketing stable; duplicate alias rows resolved.
- Governance: export audit events and RBAC verified in restricted workspaces.

Planned follow-ups after beta:
- More “what changed?” explanations and guardrails to avoid misleading conclusions.
- Scheduled reporting (email / shareable reports) evaluation.
- Additional breakdown dimensions (within cardinality guardrails).

Feedback channel for beta users:
- Console page includes a “Send feedback” link.
- Internally, route feedback to **Marissa Cole** (product) and **Dylan Brooks** (eng lead).

---

## What’s next (toward GA)
We’ll move from beta to GA after we meet these criteria:
- Reconciliation: breakdown totals match billing within tolerance across representative workspaces and deployment modes.
- Performance: interactive query p95 meets targets for 24h/7d ranges.
- Quality: route normalization + model bucketing stable; duplicate alias rows resolved.
- Governance: export audit events and RBAC verified in restricted workspaces.

Planned follow-ups after beta:
- More “what changed?” explanations and guardrails to avoid misleading conclusions.
- Scheduled reporting (email / shareable reports) evaluation.
- Additional breakdown dimensions (within cardinality guardrails).

title: Hosted API Packages: Free Tier, Included Usage, and Overage Policy
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s **internal policy** for:
- Hosted API **packages** (Free and paid tiers)
- **Included usage** and what counts toward it
- **Overage behavior** (what happens at/above limits)
- How billing **meters map** to packages
- **Customer-facing wording** (approved language + prohibited language)

This policy exists to ensure consistent packaging, predictable customer experience, and audit-ready billing behavior.

**Effective date:** 2026-03-01 (for new signups). Existing customers transition at renewal or via signed order form amendment.

---

## Scope
Applies to Redwood **Hosted API** usage purchased via self-serve or order form:
- Text generation / chat
- Embeddings
- Reranking (where enabled)

Does **not** apply to:
- Redwood Dedicated (see Dedicated metering pages)
- Redwood Private licensing

---

## Definitions (use these exact terms)
### Metered quantities
**Billed tokens**
- Tokens used for billing and packaging limits.
- Computed using Redwood’s canonical tokenization and request accounting rules.
- Exposed via the Usage API as `billed_prompt_tokens`, `billed_completion_tokens`, and `billed_total_tokens`.

**Raw tokens (internal only)**
- Debug/telemetry quantity that may include internal retries, fallbacks, or non-billable work.
- Do not use in customer-facing explanations.

**Requests**
- Counted per successful API request (HTTP 2xx/3xx) and select billable error classes as defined in the meter spec.
- Streaming counts as **one request** per initiation (not per chunk).

**Embedding units**
- For embeddings, usage is metered in billed tokens for the embedding input. (We also store vector count/dimensions for ops, but packaging is based on billed tokens unless otherwise stated in an order form.)

### What “included usage” means
Included usage is the amount bundled with a package **during a billing period**. Charges apply only when:
1) a customer is on a plan that allows overage, and
2) usage exceeds included amounts.

### Billing period
- Self-serve: monthly, aligned to subscription start date (unless otherwise stated).
- Contracted enterprise: per order form.

---

## Canonical Hosted API package policy (standard self-serve)
> Note: List prices and exact rates are maintained in the pricing catalog. This page defines **policy and mechanics**; amounts below are the default 2026Q1 packages.

### Package table (default self-serve)
| Package | Target customer | Included billed tokens / mo | Included requests / mo | Overage allowed? | Support | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| Free | Evaluation / prototypes | 250,000 | 5,000 | No (hard stop) | Community | No SLA; stricter rate limits |
| Builder | Early production | 20,000,000 | 250,000 | Yes | Standard | Requires payment method |
| Growth | Scaling teams | 100,000,000 | 1,500,000 | Yes | Standard+ | Higher default concurrency |
| Scale | High volume | 500,000,000 | 8,000,000 | Yes | Priority | Contracted add-ons possible |
| Enterprise | Regulated / custom | Per order form | Per order form | Yes (as contracted) | Premium | Custom terms, SLAs, invoicing |

### Overage pricing principles
- Overage is charged using the same meters as included usage (billed tokens and/or requests).
- Overage is **disabled** on Free by default.
- Overage must be **enabled explicitly** for any account with a custom cap (Enterprise) and reflected in the order form.

---

## How meters map to packages (source of truth)
### Meter fields used for packaging enforcement
Packaging limits and overage calculations rely on these canonical fields:
- `billed_prompt_tokens`
- `billed_completion_tokens`
- `billed_total_tokens` (= prompt + completion)
- `request_count`
- `cache_hit_tokens` (display and attribution; does not change billed totals unless explicitly discounted by contract)
- Dimensions used for reporting (not limits unless contract says): `model`, `region`, `endpoint`, `customer_id`, `workspace_id`

### What counts toward billed tokens
Counts toward billed tokens:
- Prompt tokens that are processed for a request
- Completion tokens successfully generated and delivered (including streamed tokens that were sent before a disconnect)
- Tool/function call tokens and structured output tokens that are part of the model’s generated output (see meter spec)

Does NOT count toward billed tokens:
- Non-billable internal retries that are deduped by idempotency keys
- Requests rejected before model execution due to auth failure, invalid params, or quota pre-check (typically 4xx)

### Caching policy (how to talk about it)
- We track cache-hit attribution for observability and (in some contracts) discounting.
- **Default:** cache hits still count toward billed tokens unless the customer has a specific caching discount addendum.
- Customer-facing phrasing must avoid implying “free tokens” unless a signed term exists.

---

## Free tier policy
### Eligibility
- Available to any new workspace that completes email verification.
- One Free workspace per legal entity by default (fraud/abuse controls may restrict).

### Limits & behavior
- Free includes the package amounts listed above.
- When a Free workspace reaches a limit, the API returns:
  - HTTP `402 Payment Required` with an error code `quota_exceeded`
  - A message directing the customer to add a payment method or upgrade
- No overage charges are incurred on Free.

### Free tier operational guardrails
- Rate limits are stricter than paid tiers and may be adjusted to protect platform stability.
- Free tier can be suspended for abuse (e.g., automated signup, policy violations).

---

## Overage policy (paid tiers)
### Default overage behavior
For Builder/Growth/Scale:
- Overage is **enabled** once a valid payment method is on file.
- Overage charges are calculated at the end of the billing period.

### Soft limit and hard limit behavior
To prevent surprise bills while avoiding unnecessary downtime:
- **Soft limit (100% of included):**
  - Send automated email and in-console banner to workspace admins.
  - Continue serving traffic.
- **Hard limit (120% of included) – default:**
  - Continue serving traffic if the account has “Overage Allowed” enabled.
  - If overage is disabled (rare on paid tiers), return `402 quota_exceeded`.

Enterprise exceptions:
- Enterprise accounts may have a contractual hard cap (e.g., “do not exceed $X/month”). If so, RevOps must configure the cap in billing controls and attach the signed term.

### Customer notifications
Minimum notification set (must be enabled for all paid workspaces):
- 80% included usage (warning)
- 100% included usage (soft limit)
- 110% included usage (approaching hard limit)
- Invoice preview available (see month-end preview process)

---

## Credits, refunds, and disputes (packaging interaction)
### Credits application order
Unless an order form specifies otherwise:
1) Included usage
2) Promotional or sales-issued credits
3) Overage charges

### Refunds/credits policy (high level)
- Billing disputes are governed by the dispute window in the MSA/order form.
- Any retroactive adjustment impacting recognized revenue requires Finance approval and must be documented with an audit trail.
- Support may issue customer goodwill credits only per the approvals matrix in the Discounts/Credits policy.

---

## Customer-facing wording guidelines (approved language)
### Principles
- Use **billed tokens** as the primary unit.
- Emphasize predictability and transparency.
- Avoid internal implementation details (retries, fallbacks, raw token counts) unless required for a specific incident.

### Approved phrases (use these)
- “We bill based on **billed tokens**, which reflect the tokens processed to generate and deliver your responses.”
- “Streaming responses are billed as a single request, and billed tokens reflect the output delivered.”
- “Your plan includes X billed tokens per month. If you exceed that amount, you’ll either be charged overage (paid plans) or asked to upgrade (Free).”
- “You can view billed token usage by day/model/region in the Console and export via the Usage API.”

### Avoid / do not say
- “We bill on raw tokens.”
- “Retries are always free.” (Only true if deduped and classified non-billable.)
- “Cache hits don’t count.” (Not true by default.)
- “Token counts match OpenAI exactly.” (We align semantics, but do not promise identical counts across providers.)

### If asked: “Why do billed tokens differ from my local tokenizer?”
Approved response:
- “Tokenization can vary across libraries and model families. Redwood reports **billed tokens** using our canonical tokenization and accounting rules, and we expose that same number consistently across the Console, Usage API, and invoices.”

---

## Packaging enforcement rules (implementation policy)
Engineering and RevOps must enforce limits consistently across:
- Console usage dashboard
- Usage API exports
- Invoice line items

**Policy requirements:**
1) A package limit must be enforceable using canonical meter fields (`billed_total_tokens`, `request_count`).
2) Any meter logic change that affects packaged usage requires:
   - Shadow-billing compare period (minimum 7 days for Hosted API)
   - Finance + Legal sign-off if customer invoices or usage definitions are impacted
   - Clear “effective date” and a customer comms plan if externally visible
3) Any experiment that changes packaging must tag cohorts in analytics and be reported with pre/post segmentation if meter fixes overlap.

---

## Examples (for internal training)
### Example 1: Free tier hits limit
- Workspace on Free uses 260,000 billed tokens in month.
- Result: request at 250,000+ returns `402 quota_exceeded`.
- Resolution: user upgrades or adds payment method to move to Builder.

### Example 2: Builder tier goes into overage
- Included billed tokens: 20,000,000
- Actual billed tokens: 24,500,000
- Overage billed tokens: 4,500,000
- Invoice shows:
  - Subscription fee (Builder)
  - Included usage (0 charge)
  - Overage line item based on billed tokens (and requests if applicable)

### Example 3: Streaming disconnect
- Customer starts a stream and receives 1,200 completion tokens before disconnect.
- Billed completion tokens = 1,200 (delivered tokens), not the model’s theoretical maximum.

---

## Operational checklist (for RevOps / Support)
When a customer asks about Free tier, limits, or overage:
1) Confirm current package and whether overage is enabled.
2) Pull billed token totals from the Usage API export used for invoicing.
3) Verify whether there was a metering incident during the period (meter sanity dashboard).
4) Use only the approved customer-facing phrasing above.
5) If requesting credits/refund: route per Discounts/Credits policy; document approvals.

---

## Ownership and change control
**Owner:** Product (Hosted API Pricing) — Nikhil Sharma

**Change control:**
- Pricing catalog changes: Product + FP&A + RevOps
- Usage definition / meter changes: Engineering + Finance (RevRec) + Legal
- Customer-facing copy: Product Marketing + Legal

For any exceptions (custom caps, overage disabled on a paid plan, cache discount terms), link the signed approval and attach it to the account record in CRM/billing notes.

title: Optimize Quality Gates (Beta) — Release Notes (Internal + Limited Customers)
preview:
## Summary
Quality Gates (Beta) adds mandatory evaluation-based guardrails before Redwood Optimize recommendations can be applied to production traffic.

This beta is targeted at:
- Internal routes/workloads
- A limited set of hosted API customers (by allowlist)

The goal is to prevent silent quality regressions from cost optimizations (batching, caching/KV behavior, and quantization profile changes) while keeping the Optimize workflow fast and auditable.

---

## Availability
**Deployment scope (Beta):** Hosted API (selected routes/accounts). Dedicated is “manual + documented” during beta.

**Default behavior:**
- Optimize recommendations that are eligible for one-click apply will now require a passing gate run.
- Auto-apply policies (where enabled) will not execute unless gates pass.

**Console surface area:** Gate status and results are visible in the Optimize recommendation detail view and the Rollouts UI (where rollouts are used).

---

## What changed
### 1) Mandatory gates for supported Optimize config change types
Gates are required for these Optimize change categories:
- **Batching policy updates** (continuous batching thresholds, microbatch sizing policy, queue/latency trade-offs)
- **Prefix / KV caching settings** (cache enablement, cache eligibility rules, TTL / eviction tuning that may affect output)
- **Quantization profile changes** (profile switches per model family and GPU SKU)

If an Optimize recommendation includes multiple change categories, the strictest gate tier applies (see “Gate tiers”).

### 2) New gate run lifecycle + audit trail
Each proposed config diff generates (or references) a Gate Run record with:
- Baseline selection (what we compared against)
- Requested config diff
- Gate tier + prompt set(s)
- Metrics, deltas, confidence/variance summary
- Artifacts (reports, sampled outputs, diff views)
- Final decision: **passed / failed / overridden / canceled**
- Approver identity for overrides + expiration

Audit events are emitted for: gate started, gate completed, decision made, apply attempted, apply blocked, override granted/expired.

### 3) Rollout coupling (defense-in-depth)
When a recommendation is applied via a rollout:
- **Canary promotion is blocked** if gates have not passed.
- If a gate fails after a canary has started (e.g., rerun due to flake policy), promotion is halted and the canary is eligible for rollback based on rollout policy.

### 4) Fast feedback via gate tiers
To keep developer experience responsive, the beta introduces two gate tiers:
- **Smoke Gate (fast):** Small, high-signal prompt subset. Intended for quick validation before deeper runs.
- **Full Gate:** Broader prompt-set coverage + stricter variance handling.

Tier selection is policy-driven (by change type, model family, and route/account tier).

---

## Gate tiers and expected timing (Beta)
Actual runtimes vary by model, GPU availability, and queue depth.

- **Smoke Gate:** typically 5–15 minutes end-to-end
- **Full Gate:** typically 25–60 minutes end-to-end

Notes:
- Gate runs may take longer in constrained regions (EU capacity has been a known bottleneck during early beta).
- If a run times out, the system will retry per the eval-harness retry policy. If it continues to time out, the gate is marked failed with reason “timeout”.

---

## What you’ll see in Console
In the Optimize recommendation detail panel:
- Gate status badge: **Not started / Running / Passed / Failed / Overridden**
- A summary table of key metrics with deltas vs baseline
- A “Why failed” section with reason codes (e.g., regression threshold exceeded, insufficient sample size, timeout, flaky suite quarantined)
- Links to artifacts (report + sampled outputs)
- Apply controls:
  - **Apply** is enabled only when gate status is Passed (or Overridden with an active, unexpired override)
  - **Request exception** is shown when gate status is Failed (or when gating cannot complete)

In Rollouts:
- Promotion step displays the gate requirement and blocks promotion if unmet.
- If blocked, the UI provides a direct link to the gate run results.

---

## Behavior when a gate fails
When a gate fails, the Optimize system will:
1) **Block apply** (one-click apply and any auto-apply policy)
2) Surface a clear failure reason (human-readable + reason code)
3) Preserve all artifacts for debugging and audit
4) Recommend next actions based on the failure type

### Common failure types and next actions
- **Quality regression detected:**
  - Review the metric deltas and sampled outputs.
  - Consider narrowing the change (smaller batching increase, more conservative cache eligibility, different quant profile).
  - Escalate to Applied ML if the regression appears inconsistent with the prompt category.

- **Insufficient sample size / high variance:**
  - Rerun (if allowed) or wait for automatic rerun policy.
  - Consider switching from Smoke → Full Gate for a more stable decision.

- **Flaky eval suite detected:**
  - The system may quarantine the suite; results will show “flake score” and rerun history.
  - If quarantined, the gate may fail “by policy” until coverage is restored.

- **Timeout / infrastructure failure:**
  - Check region capacity and retry outcome.
  - If repeated in a region, contact SRE (see Support section).

---

## Overrides / exceptions (Beta)
Overrides exist to unblock urgent changes while keeping the system safe and auditable.

### When overrides are appropriate
- Customer-impacting incidents where the optimization is required to maintain availability or cost controls
- Cases where a gate failure is confidently attributed to infra issues (e.g., repeated timeouts) and risk is understood
- Temporary mitigations while prompt set coverage is being repaired (with explicit follow-up)

Overrides are **not** intended to bypass genuine quality regressions.

### How to request an exception
1) In Console, open the failed recommendation.
2) Click **Request exception**.
3) Provide:
   - Business justification
   - Risk assessment (expected quality impact)
   - Proposed duration (default is short; longer requires stronger justification)
   - Links to artifacts and any supporting analysis
4) Submit for approval.

### Who can approve (Beta)
Approvers are restricted and vary by route/account tier:
- Product (Optimize): **Aditya Rao** (or delegate)
- Applied ML/Evals: **Jonas Weber** (or Karthik Iyer)
- Runtime safety (for quantization/batching changes with higher risk): **Mateo Alvarez** or **Noah Patel**
- SRE (for infra-driven failures/timeouts): **Sean Gallagher**

All overrides:
- Are **time-bound** and recorded with an expiration timestamp
- Require an owner and rationale
- Generate audit log events

---

## Known limitations (Beta)
1) **Hosted-first:** Dedicated and Private do not yet have full automation. Dedicated is supported via a manual workflow with required documentation; Private is design-only in this phase.

2) **Coverage gaps for niche workloads:** Some customer-specific prompt distributions (e.g., long-context edge cases, specialized formatting constraints) may not be fully represented in the current gating prompt sets.

3) **Regional capacity sensitivity:** Gate runtimes may spike under GPU scarcity. EU-West has seen intermittent timeouts during early beta; we are monitoring and adjusting scheduling.

4) **Quantization complexity:** Quant profile changes are more sensitive to model family + GPU SKU. In beta, baseline selection is conservative; some combinations will be blocked until compatibility checks and coverage are expanded.

5) **Online signals are informational (Beta):** Online canary quality signals are visible in limited form but are not yet a standalone pass/fail gate. Offline eval gates remain the primary enforcement mechanism.

6) **Flake management in progress:** Flaky suite detection exists, but quarantine/repair workflows are still being refined to reduce false blocks without weakening safety.

---

## Support and escalation
### For internal users
- #eng-ml for metric questions and prompt set coverage
- #eng-sre for timeouts/capacity and paging concerns
- #eng-runtime for config safety questions (batching/caching/quant)

### For limited beta customers
- Primary: Customer Success / Support channel per account
- If the issue blocks a critical change, Support can open an internal escalation referencing:
  - Gate Run ID
  - Route / model / region
  - Config diff summary
  - Failure reason code

### What to include in a support request
- Gate Run ID and link to artifacts
- Recommendation ID (Optimize)
- Model + version, region, route/account
- Whether the change is batching, caching, quantization, or mixed
- Time window and any relevant rollout links

---

## FAQ
**Q: Can I still make manual config changes?**
A: Yes, but beta policy is to log and (where possible) gate any change that maps to supported Optimize config primitives. Manual changes without gates may be restricted for allowlisted routes.

**Q: What happens if a gate is still running when I want to deploy?**
A: Apply/promotion remains blocked until the gate passes or an approved override is active.

**Q: What if I think the gate failure is wrong?**
A: Use the artifact links to review sampled outputs and variance details. If it looks like flakiness or infra issues, request an exception with evidence and file a follow-up to repair the suite.

**Q: Will this slow down Optimize recommendations?**
A: Recommendations are still generated as before. The gating step runs before apply/promotion. Smoke gates are designed to keep the common path fast.

---

## Changelog (Beta)
- Added gate-run state machine and persistence (pending/running/passed/failed/overridden)
- Added typed config diff schema + static validation pre-checks
- Integrated Optimize → eval-harness trigger API with retries/timeouts
- Added Smoke Gate tier for faster feedback on batching/cache changes
- Enforced apply blocking on gate failure, with explicit reason codes
- Added override flow with expiration and audit events
- Integrated gates with rollout controller to block canary promotion
- Added dashboards and alerting for gate health (pass rate, flake rate, timeouts)

---

## Next steps (post-beta)
- Expand coverage to more routes/accounts and additional model families
- Improve long-context and customer-specific prompt coverage
- Tighten variance modeling and flake quarantine/repair workflows
- Extend automation and policy primitives to Dedicated defaults; document Private workflow

For roadmap questions, contact Aditya Rao (Optimize) or Paula Mendes (Rollouts/telemetry).
text:
## Summary
Quality Gates (Beta) adds mandatory evaluation-based guardrails before Redwood Optimize recommendations can be applied to production traffic.

This beta is targeted at:
- Internal routes/workloads
- A limited set of hosted API customers (by allowlist)

The goal is to prevent silent quality regressions from cost optimizations (batching, caching/KV behavior, and quantization profile changes) while keeping the Optimize workflow fast and auditable.

---

## Availability
**Deployment scope (Beta):** Hosted API (selected routes/accounts). Dedicated is “manual + documented” during beta.

**Default behavior:**
- Optimize recommendations that are eligible for one-click apply will now require a passing gate run.
- Auto-apply policies (where enabled) will not execute unless gates pass.

**Console surface area:** Gate status and results are visible in the Optimize recommendation detail view and the Rollouts UI (where rollouts are used).

---

## What changed
### 1) Mandatory gates for supported Optimize config change types
Gates are required for these Optimize change categories:
- **Batching policy updates** (continuous batching thresholds, microbatch sizing policy, queue/latency trade-offs)
- **Prefix / KV caching settings** (cache enablement, cache eligibility rules, TTL / eviction tuning that may affect output)
- **Quantization profile changes** (profile switches per model family and GPU SKU)

If an Optimize recommendation includes multiple change categories, the strictest gate tier applies (see “Gate tiers”).

### 2) New gate run lifecycle + audit trail
Each proposed config diff generates (or references) a Gate Run record with:
- Baseline selection (what we compared against)
- Requested config diff
- Gate tier + prompt set(s)
- Metrics, deltas, confidence/variance summary
- Artifacts (reports, sampled outputs, diff views)
- Final decision: **passed / failed / overridden / canceled**
- Approver identity for overrides + expiration

Audit events are emitted for: gate started, gate completed, decision made, apply attempted, apply blocked, override granted/expired.

### 3) Rollout coupling (defense-in-depth)
When a recommendation is applied via a rollout:
- **Canary promotion is blocked** if gates have not passed.
- If a gate fails after a canary has started (e.g., rerun due to flake policy), promotion is halted and the canary is eligible for rollback based on rollout policy.

### 4) Fast feedback via gate tiers
To keep developer experience responsive, the beta introduces two gate tiers:
- **Smoke Gate (fast):** Small, high-signal prompt subset. Intended for quick validation before deeper runs.
- **Full Gate:** Broader prompt-set coverage + stricter variance handling.

Tier selection is policy-driven (by change type, model family, and route/account tier).

---

## Gate tiers and expected timing (Beta)
Actual runtimes vary by model, GPU availability, and queue depth.

- **Smoke Gate:** typically 5–15 minutes end-to-end
- **Full Gate:** typically 25–60 minutes end-to-end

Notes:
- Gate runs may take longer in constrained regions (EU capacity has been a known bottleneck during early beta).
- If a run times out, the system will retry per the eval-harness retry policy. If it continues to time out, the gate is marked failed with reason “timeout”.

---

## What you’ll see in Console
In the Optimize recommendation detail panel:
- Gate status badge: **Not started / Running / Passed / Failed / Overridden**
- A summary table of key metrics with deltas vs baseline
- A “Why failed” section with reason codes (e.g., regression threshold exceeded, insufficient sample size, timeout, flaky suite quarantined)
- Links to artifacts (report + sampled outputs)
- Apply controls:
  - **Apply** is enabled only when gate status is Passed (or Overridden with an active, unexpired override)
  - **Request exception** is shown when gate status is Failed (or when gating cannot complete)

In Rollouts:
- Promotion step displays the gate requirement and blocks promotion if unmet.
- If blocked, the UI provides a direct link to the gate run results.

---

## Behavior when a gate fails
When a gate fails, the Optimize system will:
1) **Block apply** (one-click apply and any auto-apply policy)
2) Surface a clear failure reason (human-readable + reason code)
3) Preserve all artifacts for debugging and audit
4) Recommend next actions based on the failure type

### Common failure types and next actions
- **Quality regression detected:**
  - Review the metric deltas and sampled outputs.
  - Consider narrowing the change (smaller batching increase, more conservative cache eligibility, different quant profile).
  - Escalate to Applied ML if the regression appears inconsistent with the prompt category.

- **Insufficient sample size / high variance:**
  - Rerun (if allowed) or wait for automatic rerun policy.
  - Consider switching from Smoke → Full Gate for a more stable decision.

- **Flaky eval suite detected:**
  - The system may quarantine the suite; results will show “flake score” and rerun history.
  - If quarantined, the gate may fail “by policy” until coverage is restored.

- **Timeout / infrastructure failure:**
  - Check region capacity and retry outcome.
  - If repeated in a region, contact SRE (see Support section).

---

## Overrides / exceptions (Beta)
Overrides exist to unblock urgent changes while keeping the system safe and auditable.

### When overrides are appropriate
- Customer-impacting incidents where the optimization is required to maintain availability or cost controls
- Cases where a gate failure is confidently attributed to infra issues (e.g., repeated timeouts) and risk is understood
- Temporary mitigations while prompt set coverage is being repaired (with explicit follow-up)

Overrides are **not** intended to bypass genuine quality regressions.

### How to request an exception
1) In Console, open the failed recommendation.
2) Click **Request exception**.
3) Provide:
   - Business justification
   - Risk assessment (expected quality impact)
   - Proposed duration (default is short; longer requires stronger justification)
   - Links to artifacts and any supporting analysis
4) Submit for approval.

### Who can approve (Beta)
Approvers are restricted and vary by route/account tier:
- Product (Optimize): **Aditya Rao** (or delegate)
- Applied ML/Evals: **Jonas Weber** (or Karthik Iyer)
- Runtime safety (for quantization/batching changes with higher risk): **Mateo Alvarez** or **Noah Patel**
- SRE (for infra-driven failures/timeouts): **Sean Gallagher**

All overrides:
- Are **time-bound** and recorded with an expiration timestamp
- Require an owner and rationale
- Generate audit log events

---

## Known limitations (Beta)
1) **Hosted-first:** Dedicated and Private do not yet have full automation. Dedicated is supported via a manual workflow with required documentation; Private is design-only in this phase.

2) **Coverage gaps for niche workloads:** Some customer-specific prompt distributions (e.g., long-context edge cases, specialized formatting constraints) may not be fully represented in the current gating prompt sets.

3) **Regional capacity sensitivity:** Gate runtimes may spike under GPU scarcity. EU-West has seen intermittent timeouts during early beta; we are monitoring and adjusting scheduling.

4) **Quantization complexity:** Quant profile changes are more sensitive to model family + GPU SKU. In beta, baseline selection is conservative; some combinations will be blocked until compatibility checks and coverage are expanded.

5) **Online signals are informational (Beta):** Online canary quality signals are visible in limited form but are not yet a standalone pass/fail gate. Offline eval gates remain the primary enforcement mechanism.

6) **Flake management in progress:** Flaky suite detection exists, but quarantine/repair workflows are still being refined to reduce false blocks without weakening safety.

---

## Support and escalation
### For internal users
- #eng-ml for metric questions and prompt set coverage
- #eng-sre for timeouts/capacity and paging concerns
- #eng-runtime for config safety questions (batching/caching/quant)

### For limited beta customers
- Primary: Customer Success / Support channel per account
- If the issue blocks a critical change, Support can open an internal escalation referencing:
  - Gate Run ID
  - Route / model / region
  - Config diff summary
  - Failure reason code

### What to include in a support request
- Gate Run ID and link to artifacts
- Recommendation ID (Optimize)
- Model + version, region, route/account
- Whether the change is batching, caching, quantization, or mixed
- Time window and any relevant rollout links

---

## FAQ
**Q: Can I still make manual config changes?**
A: Yes, but beta policy is to log and (where possible) gate any change that maps to supported Optimize config primitives. Manual changes without gates may be restricted for allowlisted routes.

**Q: What happens if a gate is still running when I want to deploy?**
A: Apply/promotion remains blocked until the gate passes or an approved override is active.

**Q: What if I think the gate failure is wrong?**
A: Use the artifact links to review sampled outputs and variance details. If it looks like flakiness or infra issues, request an exception with evidence and file a follow-up to repair the suite.

**Q: Will this slow down Optimize recommendations?**
A: Recommendations are still generated as before. The gating step runs before apply/promotion. Smoke gates are designed to keep the common path fast.

---

## Changelog (Beta)
- Added gate-run state machine and persistence (pending/running/passed/failed/overridden)
- Added typed config diff schema + static validation pre-checks
- Integrated Optimize → eval-harness trigger API with retries/timeouts
- Added Smoke Gate tier for faster feedback on batching/cache changes
- Enforced apply blocking on gate failure, with explicit reason codes
- Added override flow with expiration and audit events
- Integrated gates with rollout controller to block canary promotion
- Added dashboards and alerting for gate health (pass rate, flake rate, timeouts)

---

## Next steps (post-beta)
- Expand coverage to more routes/accounts and additional model families
- Improve long-context and customer-specific prompt coverage
- Tighten variance modeling and flake quarantine/repair workflows
- Extend automation and policy primitives to Dedicated defaults; document Private workflow

For roadmap questions, contact Aditya Rao (Optimize) or Paula Mendes (Rollouts/telemetry).

title: PRD: Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration (Rollouts + SLO Monitors)
preview:
## Overview
Redwood’s rollout controller supports canary, A/B testing, and staged model deployments. Today, mitigation for rollout-induced latency and error regressions is largely manual, relying on on-call detection and intervention. This PRD defines an integrated capability that automatically pauses or rolls back a rollout when SLO monitors detect sustained breaches, scoped to the exact rollout dimensions (route/service, model version, region, customer tier).

This feature is a core reliability differentiator for Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments: it reduces time-to-mitigation and helps prevent SLA violations during aggressive iteration.

## Goals
1) Automatically mitigate rollout regressions by pausing/rolling back when SLOs breach with high confidence.
2) Provide clear auditability: “what happened, why, and what did the controller do?”
3) Provide safe, configurable policy controls with guardrails to prevent oscillation and false positives.
4) Make behavior predictable for SRE and service owners via standard metrics, dashboards, alerts, and runbooks.

## Non-goals (MVP)
- Multi-region failover orchestration beyond rollback/pause (explicitly out of scope).
- Quality/eval-based rollback signals (handled under eval guardrails; to be added later as an additional signal class).
- Replacing existing incident response processes; this is an auto-mitigation rail that integrates with them.

## Primary users and user goals
### SRE / Observability
- Reduce MTTR for rollout-induced latency/error incidents.
- Avoid paging storms and flappy behavior; ensure minimum-sample gating and debounce.
- Ensure monitors are present, fresh, and correctly scoped; detect and alert on missing/stale monitors.
- Have runbook-ready events that show scope, breach evidence, and action taken.

### Platform / Service Owners (Hosted API, Dedicated control plane services, Console services)
- Roll out model versions safely with confidence that regressions will be contained.
- Configure policies per route/model/tier with templates and safe defaults.
- Understand “why did we rollback/pause?” directly in Console with links to relevant dashboards.
- Override/suppress automation when needed (e.g., known telemetry outage), with justification recorded.

### Product / Support (secondary)
- Provide clear customer-facing explanations and evidence during escalations (audit trail + screenshots/links).

## Problem statement
Rollouts can change latency, error rates, or saturation dynamics (e.g., batching/quantization changes, kernel regressions, routing changes). When issues occur, manual detection and mitigation can take minutes to tens of minutes. For higher tiers and latency-sensitive customers, this can burn error budgets and risk SLA violations.

Common failure modes we must handle:
- True regression during canary step (p95/p99 latency increase, 5xx spike).
- Monitor gaps (missing, stale, wrong labels) causing false negatives.
- Telemetry lag/partial outage causing false positives (e.g., missing data interpreted as breach).
- Oscillation: repeated pause/rollback/retry cycles.

## Definitions / terminology
- **Rollout**: a controlled shift of traffic from baseline model version to candidate version via steps (canary, staged %, or A/B).
- **Scope**: dimensions that identify the rollout target, at minimum: {service/route, model, model_version, region, customer_tier}. Some services also include {workspace/customer_id} for dedicated/private.
- **SLO monitors**: pre-defined latency/error/saturation monitors that can be queried for burn-rate/breach state over fast and slow windows.
- **Auto action**: a controller-initiated action: Pause rollout / Rollback to last known-good / Shift traffic back to baseline (A/B) / No-op (evaluate only).

## Requirements
### R1. Monitor subscription & scoping
The rollout controller must be able to evaluate SLO breach signals for the precise rollout scope.

Functional requirements:
- Identify the set of eligible monitors for the rollout scope:
  - Latency: p95 and/or p99 by route
  - Error rate: 5xx (and optionally 429/timeout where designated)
  - Saturation: GPU queue depth / request backlog / throttling (where available)
- Support scoping at minimum by: route/service, model_version, region, customer_tier.
- If a rollout step changes traffic split, evaluation must continue to use the candidate’s scoped metrics (not global service aggregates).

Acceptance criteria:
- For a staged rollout on one route in us-east-1 (self-serve tier), the evaluator queries monitors that match that exact scope.
- Scope is visible in the audit event and Console UI.

### R2. Breach evaluation semantics
We will use burn-rate based evaluation with dual windows and debounce/holdoff (see ADR-0127).

MVP semantics:
- Dual-window confirmation (fast + slow):
  - Fast window: 5m (detect sharp regressions quickly)
  - Slow window: 30m (confirm sustained impact)
- Minimum sample sizes to avoid low-traffic false positives.
- Debounce/holdoff to avoid flapping during transient spikes.
- Explicit handling for monitor staleness/missing data.

Breach outcomes:
- **BREACH_CONFIRMED**: conditions met (dual-window + min sample) -> eligible for auto action.
- **BREACH_SUSPECTED**: partial conditions met (e.g., fast window only) -> may pause depending on policy.
- **NO_BREACH**: normal.
- **INSUFFICIENT_DATA**: sample size below threshold.
- **MONITOR_STALE / MONITOR_MISSING**: cannot safely evaluate.

### R3. Actions and rollout state transitions
The system must support distinct actions depending on rollout type and policy.

Supported actions (MVP):
1) **Pause rollout**
- Freeze traffic split at current step.
- Mark rollout as PAUSED_AUTO with reason.

2) **Rollback rollout (revert to last known-good)**
- For canary/staged: shift traffic back to baseline model version.
- Must be safe and rate-limited (step-down strategy) and idempotent.
- Mark rollout as ROLLED_BACK_AUTO with reason.

3) **Shift traffic back to baseline (A/B)**
- For A/B tests: set candidate traffic to 0% (or safe minimum) and return to baseline.
- Mark as AB_BASELINE_RESTORED_AUTO.

4) **Suppress action (evaluate only)**
- Record that a breach was observed but action was suppressed due to configured suppression windows or manual overrides.

Rollout states (user-visible):
- RUNNING
- PAUSED_MANUAL
- PAUSED_AUTO (new)
- ROLLED_BACK_MANUAL
- ROLLED_BACK_AUTO (new)
- COMPLETED
- FAILED (system failure, e.g., rollback executor error)

State transition notes:
- Auto actions must be single-flight and idempotent.
- If rollback fails, mark rollout FAILED and page/notify per alert policy.

### R4. Auditability and Console visibility
Every evaluation that triggers an action must produce an audit trail record following ADR-0128.

MVP audit fields (user-facing summary in Console; full event in logs/event stream):
- rollout_id, rollout_type, current_step
- scope: route/service, model, candidate_version, baseline_version, region, customer_tier
- decision: action_taken (pause/rollback/restore/suppress), decision_time
- monitors evaluated: monitor_ids + type (latency/error/saturation)
- evidence summary:
  - windows (fast/slow)
  - burn-rate state / breach boolean
  - sample sizes
  - data freshness timestamps
- policy: policy_id + policy_version + configured thresholds
- suppression: any suppression reason (manual override, incident hold, telemetry outage flag)
- correlation_id(s) for dashboards/logs

Console UX requirements:
- Rollout timeline includes auto actions inline with clear labels.
- Event detail panel includes:
  - “Why did we act?” narrative (generated summary)
  - Link(s) to Grafana dashboard filtered by rollout_id and scope
  - “What changed?” baseline vs candidate versions
  - Admin-only controls to suppress/retry (if permissioned)

### R5. Policy configuration UX expectations
Policy must be configurable at:
- Per-rollout override (highest precedence)
- Per-service/route default
- Global safe defaults (fallback)

UX principles:
- Provide templates (e.g., “Conservative”, “Balanced”, “Aggressive”) with tier-specific defaults.
- Inline warnings when configs are unsafe (e.g., no min sample, too short windows, rollback on insufficient data).
- Preview of “effective policy” (computed final config after inheritance).
- Display what monitors must exist for the selected scope; show readiness status.

Configuration fields (MVP):
- Enabled: true/false
- Signals enabled: latency_p95, latency_p99, error_rate_5xx (saturation optional per service readiness)
- Thresholds:
  - latency regression (relative and/or absolute)
  - error rate threshold
- Evaluation:
  - fast_window, slow_window
  - min_requests_fast, min_requests_slow
  - debounce_seconds, holdoff_seconds
- Action policy:
  - on_confirmed_breach: rollback | pause
  - on_suspected_breach: pause | notify_only | none
- Suppression:
  - suppression_window_minutes after action
  - admin suppression toggle + required justification

### R6. Default thresholds (proposed)
Defaults are tiered to match SLA expectations and typical traffic patterns. These are starting points; SRE may tune based on incident history.

Notes:
- Latency thresholds are defined as relative regression vs baseline for the same scope.
- “Error rate” refers to 5xx / total requests for the scoped route.
- If service has very low traffic, min sample gating will prevent actions.

Proposed defaults table (MVP):

| Tier | Signals (default) | Confirmed breach action | Suspected breach action | Fast/Slow windows | Min req (fast/slow) | Latency regression threshold | Error rate threshold | Debounce/Holdoff | Suppression window |
|------|-------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Self-serve (Hosted) | p95 + 5xx | Pause | Notify-only | 5m / 30m | 300 / 2,000 | p95 +20% OR +60ms (whichever higher) | >1.0% for 5m AND >0.5% for 30m | 120s / 300s | 30m |
| Dedicated | p95 + p99 + 5xx | Rollback | Pause | 5m / 30m | 200 / 1,200 | p95 +15% OR +40ms; p99 +20% OR +80ms | >0.5% for 5m AND >0.25% for 30m | 120s / 300s | 45m |
| Private (VPC/on-prem) | p95 + p99 + 5xx | Rollback | Pause | 5m / 30m | 100 / 600 | p95 +10% OR +30ms; p99 +15% OR +60ms | >0.25% for 5m AND >0.10% for 30m | 180s / 600s | 60m |

Guardrails on defaults:
- Never rollback on INSUFFICIENT_DATA, MONITOR_MISSING, or MONITOR_STALE.
- If only fast window breaches (suspected), default is pause (or notify-only for self-serve).

Open question (PM/SRE): do we want p99 enabled by default for self-serve, or only for Dedicated/Private initially to reduce noise?

### R7. Safety guardrails
The system must reduce the chance of false positives and oscillation.

Guardrails (MVP):
1) **Minimum sample gating**: no action unless request counts exceed thresholds for both windows.
2) **Dual-window confirmation**: confirmed breach requires both windows to meet criteria (configurable, but bounded by safe limits).
3) **Debounce/holdoff**:
   - Debounce: require breach state to persist for N seconds before acting.
   - Holdoff: after an action, do not evaluate/act again for a cooldown period.
4) **Suppression during known incidents**:
   - Allow a global “known incident” suppress flag (managed by SRE) to stop auto actions during telemetry/monitoring outages.
5) **Rate-limited rollback execution**:
   - Traffic shifts must be incremental (step-down) with safeguards.
6) **Missing/stale monitors behavior**:
   - Default to fail-open for rollout progress (do not automatically rollback), but emit an alert/notification and block “enable auto rollback” readiness (release gate).

### R8. Observability requirements
We must ship standard metrics and dashboards.

MVP metrics (conceptual):
- rollout_slo_eval_total{outcome, signal_type, service, route, region, tier}
- rollout_auto_action_total{action, reason, service, route, region, tier}
- rollout_monitor_health{status=ok|missing|stale}

Dashboards and alerts:
- “Auto action triggered” alert (notify; page only if rollback fails or repeat failures occur).
- “Monitor missing/stale for enabled policy” alert (notify owner; gate enablement).
- Dashboard panels for:
  - auto actions over time
  - top routes by auto actions
  - suppression reasons
  - stale/missing monitor counts

### R9. Permissions and security
- Admin-only overrides (suppress/unsuppress/retry) must be permissioned via RBAC.
- Every override requires justification and is audited.
- Ensure audit events contain no customer prompt content. Only operational metadata and monitor references.

## User stories
1) As an SRE on-call, when a canary rollout causes p95 latency to increase sharply, I want the rollout to auto-pause or rollback within minutes so customer impact is minimized.
2) As a platform engineer, I want to configure a rollout policy template for my route and see clear warnings if my thresholds are unsafe.
3) As a service owner, I want to view a rollout timeline and click into an auto-rollback event to see monitor evidence and links to the dashboard.
4) As an SRE, I want alerts if monitors are missing/stale for routes that have auto-rollback enabled.
5) As an admin, I want to suppress automation during a telemetry incident for 2 hours, with justification captured, and later re-enable it.

## UX requirements (Console)
### Rollout Policy Editor (new section)
- Toggle: “Auto-mitigation on SLO breach” (enabled/disabled)
- Template dropdown: Conservative / Balanced / Aggressive (tier-aware)
- Editable fields: signals, thresholds, windows, min sample, action mapping
- Inline validation with safe bounds (e.g., minimum slow window, minimum min_requests)
- Effective policy preview (read-only summary)

### Rollout Detail: Audit timeline
- Timeline events show: “Auto-paused due to latency SLO burn (p95)” or “Auto-rolled back due to 5xx breach”
- Event detail panel:
  - Scope chips (route/model/version/region/tier)
  - Decision summary + timestamp
  - Evidence summary (fast + slow window status, sample sizes)
  - Links: “Open dashboard (filtered)” and “View monitor definitions”
  - Admin actions (if permissioned): suppress for X minutes; re-attempt rollout step (if supported)

## Dependencies
- Rollout controller hooks (state machine integration)
- SLO monitor provider / slo-toolkit burn-rate endpoint supporting rollout scoping
- Policy engine schema + validation + inheritance
- Observability pack: dashboards + alert rules
- Incident-bot / Slack notifications for auto actions
- RBAC updates for override endpoints

## Phased rollout plan
### Phase 0: Discovery + design (2–3 weeks)
Exit criteria:
- Confirm evaluation semantics (ADR-0127) and audit schema (ADR-0128)
- Finalize tier defaults and safe bounds with SRE
- Identify initial internal canary services/routes

### Phase 1: MVP in dev/staging (4–6 weeks)
Scope:
- Support pause on confirmed breach for canary/staged rollouts
- Audit trail events emitted; Console timeline view available behind flag
- Basic dashboards and notifications

Exit criteria:
- E2E test: inject synthetic latency and 5xx, verify auto pause, audit event, dashboard links
- No observed flapping in simulation suite

### Phase 2: Rollback execution + hardening (2–3 weeks)
Scope:
- Enable rollback actions for Dedicated/Private tiers (and selected internal routes)
- Add idempotency/locking and safe traffic shift strategy
- Add suppression + admin override

Exit criteria:
- Game day scenarios passed:
  - latency spike
  - 5xx burst
  - monitor staleness
  - telemetry lag
- Runbook published and on-call training completed

### Phase 3: Production enablement (1–2 weeks, staged)
- Enable behind feature flag by environment and region
- Start with internal production route(s), then expand to dedicated customers with high operational maturity
- Self-serve enablement later, defaulting to pause (not rollback) to reduce risk

Backout criteria:
- Auto actions causing >1 significant false positive per day for enabled routes
- Any safety issue in rollback executor (double execution, incorrect scope, unsafe shift)
- Monitor health instability (staleness across >10% of enabled scopes)

## Success metrics
Primary:
- Reduce MTTR for rollout-induced latency/error incidents by 50% (median) for enabled routes.
- Reduce number of SLA-impacting incidents attributable to rollouts by 25% over a quarter (for cohorts with feature enabled).

Secondary:
- Time-to-auto-mitigation (TTA) under 5 minutes for confirmed fast+slow breaches.
- < 1 false-positive auto rollback per 30 rollout-days (Dedicated/Private cohorts).
- Monitor readiness: >95% of enabled routes have fresh, correctly-scoped monitors.
- Operator satisfaction: SRE survey indicates audit trail is sufficient for triage without digging into raw logs (target: 4/5).

## Risks and mitigations
- Risk: False positives due to telemetry lag or low traffic.
  - Mitigation: min sample gating, dual-window confirmation, staleness detection, debounce.
- Risk: Oscillation during intermittent regressions.
  - Mitigation: holdoff + suppression window; require manual confirmation to retry after repeated actions (future enhancement).
- Risk: Missing monitors prevent automation.
  - Mitigation: readiness checks and release gates; alert owners; document monitor requirements.
- Risk: Rollback executor safety (partial failures, double-execute).
  - Mitigation: idempotency keys, locking, step-down traffic shifts, comprehensive E2E tests.

## Open questions
1) Should self-serve tier ever auto-rollback (vs pause-only) in MVP, or is pause-only safer until we have more operational data?
2) What is the default for “suspected breach” action in Dedicated/Private: pause vs immediate rollback for error spikes?
3) How do we represent saturation signals in policy templates given service-by-service variability?
4) What is the policy for suppressing automation during a declared incident: global toggle only, or per-service?
5) Should “monitor missing/stale” block rollouts (fail-closed) for certain tiers, or remain notify-only in MVP?

## Appendix: Example effective policy (for reference)
Example (Dedicated, Balanced template):
- enabled: true
- signals: latency_p95=true, latency_p99=true, error_5xx=true
- windows: fast=5m, slow=30m
- min_requests: fast=200, slow=1200
- thresholds:
  - latency_p95_regression: max(15%, 40ms)
  - latency_p99_regression: max(20%, 80ms)
  - error_5xx: >0.5% fast AND >0.25% slow
- actions:
  - confirmed_breach: rollback
  - suspected_breach: pause
- debounce_seconds: 120
- holdoff_seconds: 300
- suppression_window_minutes: 45

(Implementation details and bounds are defined in the engineering design spec and ADRs.)
text:
## Overview
Redwood’s rollout controller supports canary, A/B testing, and staged model deployments. Today, mitigation for rollout-induced latency and error regressions is largely manual, relying on on-call detection and intervention. This PRD defines an integrated capability that automatically pauses or rolls back a rollout when SLO monitors detect sustained breaches, scoped to the exact rollout dimensions (route/service, model version, region, customer tier).

This feature is a core reliability differentiator for Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments: it reduces time-to-mitigation and helps prevent SLA violations during aggressive iteration.

## Goals
1) Automatically mitigate rollout regressions by pausing/rolling back when SLOs breach with high confidence.
2) Provide clear auditability: “what happened, why, and what did the controller do?”
3) Provide safe, configurable policy controls with guardrails to prevent oscillation and false positives.
4) Make behavior predictable for SRE and service owners via standard metrics, dashboards, alerts, and runbooks.

## Non-goals (MVP)
- Multi-region failover orchestration beyond rollback/pause (explicitly out of scope).
- Quality/eval-based rollback signals (handled under eval guardrails; to be added later as an additional signal class).
- Replacing existing incident response processes; this is an auto-mitigation rail that integrates with them.

## Primary users and user goals
### SRE / Observability
- Reduce MTTR for rollout-induced latency/error incidents.
- Avoid paging storms and flappy behavior; ensure minimum-sample gating and debounce.
- Ensure monitors are present, fresh, and correctly scoped; detect and alert on missing/stale monitors.
- Have runbook-ready events that show scope, breach evidence, and action taken.

### Platform / Service Owners (Hosted API, Dedicated control plane services, Console services)
- Roll out model versions safely with confidence that regressions will be contained.
- Configure policies per route/model/tier with templates and safe defaults.
- Understand “why did we rollback/pause?” directly in Console with links to relevant dashboards.
- Override/suppress automation when needed (e.g., known telemetry outage), with justification recorded.

### Product / Support (secondary)
- Provide clear customer-facing explanations and evidence during escalations (audit trail + screenshots/links).

## Problem statement
Rollouts can change latency, error rates, or saturation dynamics (e.g., batching/quantization changes, kernel regressions, routing changes). When issues occur, manual detection and mitigation can take minutes to tens of minutes. For higher tiers and latency-sensitive customers, this can burn error budgets and risk SLA violations.

Common failure modes we must handle:
- True regression during canary step (p95/p99 latency increase, 5xx spike).
- Monitor gaps (missing, stale, wrong labels) causing false negatives.
- Telemetry lag/partial outage causing false positives (e.g., missing data interpreted as breach).
- Oscillation: repeated pause/rollback/retry cycles.

## Definitions / terminology
- **Rollout**: a controlled shift of traffic from baseline model version to candidate version via steps (canary, staged %, or A/B).
- **Scope**: dimensions that identify the rollout target, at minimum: {service/route, model, model_version, region, customer_tier}. Some services also include {workspace/customer_id} for dedicated/private.
- **SLO monitors**: pre-defined latency/error/saturation monitors that can be queried for burn-rate/breach state over fast and slow windows.
- **Auto action**: a controller-initiated action: Pause rollout / Rollback to last known-good / Shift traffic back to baseline (A/B) / No-op (evaluate only).

## Requirements
### R1. Monitor subscription & scoping
The rollout controller must be able to evaluate SLO breach signals for the precise rollout scope.

Functional requirements:
- Identify the set of eligible monitors for the rollout scope:
  - Latency: p95 and/or p99 by route
  - Error rate: 5xx (and optionally 429/timeout where designated)
  - Saturation: GPU queue depth / request backlog / throttling (where available)
- Support scoping at minimum by: route/service, model_version, region, customer_tier.
- If a rollout step changes traffic split, evaluation must continue to use the candidate’s scoped metrics (not global service aggregates).

Acceptance criteria:
- For a staged rollout on one route in us-east-1 (self-serve tier), the evaluator queries monitors that match that exact scope.
- Scope is visible in the audit event and Console UI.

### R2. Breach evaluation semantics
