- Export must reflect current filters.
- Must include stable column definitions, rounding rules, and currency.
- Must include metadata header (time range, generated_at, workspace/project, filters applied).

**R4.2** Export job lifecycle
- For large ranges, use async export generation with status polling.
- Provide signed URL downloads with expiry.

**R4.3** Shareable report link
- Link must enforce RBAC at view time (no unauthenticated sharing).
- Link should be invalidated when permissions change or report is deleted.

### R5. RBAC & governance
**R5.1** Permissions
- Separate permissions for:
  - viewing cost insights
  - exporting/download cost reports
  - creating shareable links
- Workspace setting: “Hide cost data” (e.g., show usage metrics without currency), with clear UI messaging.

**R5.2** Audit logging
- Audit events required for:
  - export requested
  - export downloaded
  - share link created/accessed

### R6. Correctness vs billing
**R6.1** Totals reconciliation
- For any filter context that corresponds to billable usage, the sum of breakdown rows should match the billing total for the same window within tolerance.

**R6.2** Tolerance policy
- Define acceptable variance due to timing/rounding (e.g., ≤ 0.5% or ≤ $5, whichever is greater) and show a banner when exceeded.

**R6.3** Customer-facing messaging
- If data is late/partial, display:
  - last complete timestamp
  - estimated completeness
  - note that totals may not match invoice until backfilled

### R7. Performance & reliability
**R7.1** Query performance targets (console)
- p95 response time targets (interactive):
  - 7d range: ≤ 2.0s p95
  - 30d range: ≤ 4.0s p95 (may require guardrails)

**R7.2** Guardrails
- Protect against high-cardinality dimensions:
  - route normalization required
  - cap number of groups returned (e.g., top 500 rows)
  - require additional filters for large org-level queries

### R8. Data freshness
**R8.1** Freshness indicator
- Always display “Data last updated at …” and “Approx. lag …”.

**R8.2** Partial-data behavior
- If lag exceeds threshold, show warning and optionally disable “What changed?” comparisons if they would be misleading.

---

## Proposed data & UI outputs (MVP)
### Core table: “Cost by route and model” (columns)
Required columns (MVP):
- Route (canonical)
- Model (bucketed)
- Total cost (currency)
- Requests
- Input tokens
- Output tokens
- Cache hit rate (if available; otherwise “—”)
- Retry rate / retry count
- Fallback rate / fallback count

Optional columns (post-MVP, behind flags):
- Avg latency (p50/p95)
- Streaming overhead estimate
- Region
- Deployment mode

### Export format (MVP)
- One CSV type for rollups (route+model per time range), with a follow-up for time-series export.
- Must include unique identifiers for route/model buckets to reduce ambiguity.

---

## Success metrics
### Adoption
- % of active workspaces with at least one Usage Insights view per week.
- # of exports created per week (and completion rate).

### Customer outcomes
- Median time-to-answer for “top cost routes/models” (qualitative + usability testing).
- Reduction in support tickets related to “where did my costs come from?” after GA.

### Reliability/trust
- Reconciliation success rate: % of queries where totals match billing within tolerance.
- Data freshness SLO compliance (% time lag below threshold).

### Performance
- Query latency p95 by common presets (24h/7d/30d).
- Export generation p95 for 30d range.

---

## Rollout plan (phased)
### Phase 0: Discovery & data audit (1–2 weeks)
Owners: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman, Dylan Brooks
- Validate available telemetry fields for route and model attribution.
- Identify gaps (e.g., missing route tags for streaming endpoints, alias duplication risk).
- Draft ADR for route normalization + model bucketing + attribution for retries/fallbacks.

Exit criteria:
- Confirm route/model coverage on at least 2 high-volume regions and hosted mode.
- Agreement on reconciliation method and tolerance policy.

### Phase 1: Backend aggregation + API contract + initial console tables (2–4 weeks)
Owners: Nadia Rahman (pipeline), Chloe Martin (backend), Dylan Brooks (console)
- Implement aggregation dataset keyed by: time bucket + workspace/project + route + model bucket.
- Implement v1 query endpoints.
- Console: ship the breakdown table with filters and drilldown navigation.

Exit criteria:
- 7d interactive queries meet p95 target for beta accounts.
- Reconciliation tests passing on sampled accounts.

### Phase 2: Top drivers + export + permissions + perf hardening (2–3 weeks)
Owners: Chloe Martin (API/export), Kaitlyn Nguyen (RBAC), Thomas Baird (guardrails)
- Implement “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” endpoints.
- CSV export (async), signed URLs, audit events.
- RBAC gating, workspace setting, and permission denied UX.
- Performance optimization (indexes/materialized views/caching).

Exit criteria:
- Export completes successfully for 30d range under size limits.
- Audit log events verified by Security.
- Guardrails prevent query timeouts for common misuses.

### Phase 3: Beta rollout + correctness validation + docs + launch comms (1–2 weeks)
Owners: Connor O’Brien (release), Elliot Price (docs), Markus Klein (SE)
- Enable feature flag + allowlist.
- Validate correctness vs billing on beta accounts; resolve high-severity discrepancies.
- Publish help center article and in-console tooltips.

Exit criteria (Beta → GA readiness):
- Billing reconciliation within tolerance for ≥ 95% of beta queries.
- No P0/P1 security issues; RBAC validated.
- Support playbook ready; known issues documented.

---

## Beta plan
### Beta audience (target)
- 5–10 accounts that:
  - have meaningful usage volume
  - have requested route/model reporting
  - include at least one compliance-sensitive customer for RBAC/audit validation

### Beta limitations (expected)
- Some dimensions (region/deployment mode) may be incomplete initially.
- “What changed?” explanations may be heuristic and will show confidence notes.
- Long time-range queries may require additional filters or use export.

---

## Dependencies
- Telemetry instrumentation must include reliable route tags (including streaming endpoints) and model bucket identifiers.
- Route normalization library shared across gateway/telemetry.
- Billing reconciliation hooks and tolerance policy agreement.
- RBAC integration with console roles and workspace settings.
- Observability dashboards for freshness and reconciliation drift.

---

## Risks & mitigations
1) **Cardinality explosion (routes, model aliases)**
   - Mitigation: route canonicalization; model bucketing; caps on groups; sampling/guardrails.

2) **Billing mismatch erodes trust**
   - Mitigation: reconciliation tests in CI; explicit tolerance; freshness banners; clear definitions.

3) **Misleading “top drivers” claims**
   - Mitigation: require confidence notes; only show drivers when underlying metrics exist; conservative copy.

4) **Permission leakage via exports/share links**
   - Mitigation: strict RBAC checks; signed URLs; audit logs; no anonymous links.

5) **Performance regressions for 30d range**
   - Mitigation: pre-aggregation, caching, index tuning; enforce default 7d; export for long windows.

---

## Open questions
1) What is the canonical list of “routes” to expose (Redwood-native vs OpenAI-compat mapping)?
2) Do we allow org-level views across multiple workspaces/projects in v1, or restrict to project-scoped first?
3) How do we represent costs for fallbacks (requested model vs served model) in UI and exports?
4) What is the final tolerance policy for reconciliation, and how do we message unavoidable drift (timing, rounding)?
5) What are the maximum supported ranges for interactive queries vs exports?

---

## Appendix: Stakeholders
- Product: Marissa Cole (lead), Paula Mendes (PM partner)
- Engineering: Dylan Brooks (Console & Tooling), Chloe Martin (Console Backend), Logan Wright (Telemetry & Analytics), Nadia Rahman (cost attribution)
- Observability: Allison Grant
- Security/RBAC: Marcus Lin, Kaitlyn Nguyen
- Docs/DX: Alex Martinez, Elliot Price
- Solutions/Support: Markus Klein, Dev Patel
- Finance partner: Rishi Malhotra

title: PRD: Shadow Traffic for Quality Eval Metrics (Console & Rollouts)
preview:
## Summary
Teams trust Redwood for safe rollouts based on latency/cost/error SLOs, but still lack a production-safe way to detect *quality* regressions (correctness, safety, instruction following, format adherence) when changing models/versions/quantization/prompt templates. This PRD proposes **Shadow Traffic**: duplicate a sampled subset of live requests to one or more candidate variants, score outputs asynchronously, and surface **quality deltas** alongside existing performance and error metrics—without affecting the user-visible response.

Shadow Traffic is designed to work across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments using existing routing/rollout primitives (route configs, canaries, A/B) and Redwood’s eval-harness scoring templates.

---

## Problem statement
### What customers do today
- Run offline prompt sets that do not match production distribution.
- Manually sample production logs and run ad-hoc eval scripts (slow, non-deterministic, often violates retention policies).
- Deploy canaries and hope user feedback/support tickets surface regressions.

### Why this fails
- Quality regressions often show up only under real production context (long contexts, tool outputs, domain-specific formats).
- Customers cannot accept user-visible risk during testing.
- Platform teams need consistent metrics and governance across many routes/teams.

### Customer pain (verbatim themes)
- “We can’t justify switching models without a quality dashboard we trust.”
- “We need a pre-flight quality gate during rollouts—not a postmortem.”
- “We need strict retention/redaction; we cannot store prompts indefinitely.”

---

## Goals
1. **Production-safe quality measurement**: shadow requests must never change user-visible responses and must be fail-open.
2. **Statistically meaningful deltas**: confidence intervals and minimum sample guidance built into the experience.
3. **Rollouts integration**: attach shadow experiment results to canary/A-B rollouts and support regression alerts.
4. **Minimal overhead**: strict budgets for latency impact, telemetry cardinality, and scoring compute.
5. **Security + compliance by default**: data minimization, redaction, short retention defaults, audit logs.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Full offline replay for arbitrary historical traffic beyond short retention.
- Executing arbitrary customer-supplied eval code inside Redwood (v1 focuses on Redwood-provided metrics + bring-your-own prompt sets via eval-harness where supported).
- Automatic rollback based on quality signals at launch (advisory-first; revisit post-beta).

---

## Target users & personas
### Persona A: ML Engineer (Quality owner)
**Jobs to be done**
- Compare candidate vs control quality during model/version/prompt changes.
- Understand *why* quality changed (examples, segments, rubric breakdown).
- Confidently recommend ship/rollback.

**Pain points**
- Offline eval sets miss production edge cases.
- Hard to correlate quality with latency/cost changes.
- Requires building internal pipelines.

### Persona B: Platform Engineer (Inference platform owner)
**Jobs to be done**
- Roll out changes safely across routes and teams.
- Enforce governance (sampling caps, data handling policies, RBAC).
- Avoid performance regressions and metrics stack overload.

**Pain points**
- High-cardinality metrics melt dashboards.
- Shadowing can create unpredictable cost if unbounded.
- Needs auditability and kill switches.

### Persona C: SRE / Observability Engineer
**Jobs to be done**
- Ensure shadow path does not impact user path.
- Detect scoring pipeline degradation (lag, backlog) and respond.

---

## User stories
1. **Create shadow experiment on a route**
   - As an ML Eng, I choose a route, specify sampling %, select 1–2 candidate variants (model version or config), and start.
2. **View quality deltas with confidence intervals**
   - As an ML Eng, I see rubric scores and heuristic metrics comparing control vs candidates over time and by segment.
3. **Drill into examples**
   - As an ML Eng, I can view redacted prompts/outputs (where allowed) for a small sample of flagged cases.
4. **Attach to rollout**
   - As a Platform Eng, I run a canary rollout and view shadow quality signals in the rollout timeline.
5. **Governance + safety**
   - As a Platform Eng, I cap sampling and scoring budgets per org/tenant and can disable shadowing instantly.

---

## Product scope (v1)
### In-scope
- Shadow traffic routing policy attached to a **route** and/or **rollout**.
- Deterministic request fingerprinting + bucketing for consistent comparisons.
- Async scoring pipeline for a default metric bundle (see below).
- Console UI: create experiment, results dashboard, drilldown examples (permission-gated), export.
- Alerting for quality regressions and scoring pipeline health.
- Hosted, Dedicated, Private support with environment-appropriate retention/storage defaults.

### Out of scope
- Unlimited number of candidates per experiment (v1: max 2 shadow variants per control).
- Arbitrary custom metrics plugins executed inside Redwood.

---

## Definitions
- **Control**: the variant that serves the user response.
- **Shadow variant**: candidate variant that receives mirrored requests but does not serve responses.
- **Shadow experiment**: configuration + time window + scoring rubric version.
- **Quality delta**: difference in metric distributions between control and candidate with confidence bounds.

---

## Functional requirements
### 1) Experiment configuration
- Create shadow experiment from:
  - A route (standalone), or
  - A rollout object (canary/A-B), linked for traceability.
- Required fields:
  - Control target (route current target)
  - Shadow targets (1–2)
  - Sampling rate (default 1%, configurable with caps)
  - Fingerprinting key selection (default: stable hash of normalized prompt + request metadata; excludes raw PII fields)
  - Runtime guardrails (timeouts, circuit breaker behavior)
  - Scoring bundle + rubric version pin
- Optional fields:
  - Segment filters (e.g., request_type=chat vs completion; region; model family)
  - Request field allowlist for storage (Dedicated/Private may expand)
  - Customer-defined tags (restricted list; validated against cardinality policy)

### 2) Shadow routing behavior
- Fail-open: shadow failures must not affect the user-visible path.
- Strict timeouts on shadow requests.
- Backpressure + circuit breaker:
  - If shadow queue backlog or shadow target error rate exceeds thresholds, auto-disable shadow forwarding for that experiment (with audit event + banner in Console).
- Streaming support:
  - Control response streams as normal.
  - Shadow captures are bounded (e.g., up to N tokens or until timeout) with explicit “partial_capture” markers.

### 3) Data capture & privacy
- Default capture: minimal fields needed for scoring (see “Data handling” section).
- Redaction pipeline applied before persistence.
- Retention defaults:
  - Hosted: short retention (default 7 days for scored aggregates; 24–72 hours for sampled examples).
  - Dedicated: configurable retention windows with org admin approval.
  - Private: customer-controlled storage location and retention; support “no examples stored” mode.

### 4) Scoring pipeline
- Asynchronous scoring with retry/dedupe.
- Support:
  - Heuristic metrics (fast, deterministic)
  - Model-graded rubric metrics (slower, costed)
- Store:
  - Per-request scores (short retention)
  - Aggregates by time window + safe dimensions (longer retention)
- Rubric version pinning:
  - Experiment results always labeled with rubric version; comparisons across rubric versions are not shown by default.

### 5) Console UX (v1 must-haves)
- “Shadow Experiments” page: create, list, status, last data received, scoring lag indicator.
- Results view:
  - Metric deltas with confidence intervals
  - Trend over time (hour/day)
  - Segment filters (cardinality-safe)
  - Clear “not enough samples yet” states with guidance
- Examples drilldown:
  - Permission-gated (RBAC + audit)
  - Redacted views by default; download/export gated by role
- Rollout integration:
  - Rollout timeline shows linked shadow experiment status and top regressions.

---

## Default quality metric bundle (v1)
v1 ships a **default metric bundle** intended to cover common regression types while keeping costs and ambiguity manageable.

### A) Heuristic metrics (low cost, deterministic)
1. **Format adherence**
   - Checks: valid JSON, required keys present, schema match, no extraneous text.
   - Inputs: output text; optional schema.
   - Caveat: strict schemas can over-penalize benign variations.

2. **Refusal rate (proxy)**
   - Detect refusals using pattern + classifier heuristics.
   - Caveat: false positives for safe disclaimers.

3. **Tool/function calling structure validity** (if applicable)
   - Valid tool name + arguments parse + required args.

4. **Length/verbosity drift**
   - Mean/median output tokens; tail behavior.
   - Used as a diagnostic, not a “quality score.”

5. **Error-classification deltas**
   - Increase in empty outputs, truncation markers, partial streaming captures.

### B) Model-graded metrics (higher cost; async)
1. **Instruction following (rubric score 1–5)**
   - Model-graded rubric prompts tuned for general instruction compliance.
   - Requires stable rubric model/version.

2. **Safety / policy compliance (rubric score + flags)**
   - Detects unsafe content categories and refusal appropriateness.
   - Output: categorical flags + score.

3. **Grounding/faithfulness proxy (where applicable)**
   - For requests with provided context: checks whether answer cites/uses given context.
   - Note: v1 is a proxy, not a formal factuality guarantee.

### v1 metric exclusions (explicit)
- Domain-specific correctness (requires custom evals; roadmap).
- Full jailbreak robustness testing (separate security program).

---

## Dimensions & segmentation (cardinality-safe)
v1 supports a restricted set of dimensions for aggregation:
- route_id (bucketed where needed)
- deployment_mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- region
- request_type (chat/completions/embeddings where relevant)
- model_id (control + shadow)
- rubric_version
- outcome (success/error/timeout/partial_capture)

Explicitly not supported as aggregation dimensions in v1:
- customer_user_id
- prompt_id (unless bucketed/coarsened)
- raw customer tags beyond an allowlisted set

---

## Success criteria
### Product success (beta)
- At least **3 beta customers** actively running shadow experiments weekly.
- Customers can make a ship/rollback decision using the dashboard within **< 30 minutes** of review time.
- At least **2 documented saves** where shadow metrics caught a regression before user exposure.

### Technical success
- **User-path impact**:
  - p99 latency impact on control path: **<= 1%** at default sampling.
  - No increased error rates attributable to shadowing.
- **Shadow path reliability**:
  - Shadow forwarding success rate (when enabled): **>= 99%** excluding shadow target errors.
  - Scoring pipeline: **95% of scored results available within 30 minutes** (target; measure separately by metric type).
- **Observability safety**:
  - No sustained metrics cardinality alerts from enabling shadow traffic under recommended configs.

### Trust & interpretability
- Confidence intervals shown for all primary deltas.
- Clear UI indicators for: insufficient sample size, scoring lag, partial captures, rubric version.

---

## Rollout plan
### Phase 0 (Requirements + architecture) — 2 weeks
- Finalize ADRs (routing placement, telemetry model).
- Define metric bundle v1 and rubric versioning policy.
- Sign off retention/redaction defaults with Security.

### Phase 1 (MVP + dogfood) — 4–6 weeks
- Shadow routing MVP + deterministic bucketing.
- Telemetry events v1 + basic aggregates.
- Scoring pipeline for heuristics + 1 model-graded rubric (instruction following).
- Internal dogfood on Redwood-owned tenants; weekly review.

### Phase 2 (Console + alerts + rollout integration) — 4 weeks
- Console create/view flows + example drilldowns.
- Attach to rollouts (timeline + linkage).
- Regression alerts (advisory) + scoring lag alerts.

### Phase 3 (Hardening + GA readiness) — 4–6 weeks
- Overhead benchmarks + backpressure tuning.
- Expanded rubric set (safety + format adherence + faithfulness proxy).
- Docs + SDK support; enablement.

---

## Pricing & packaging assumptions (v1)
**Principles**
- Shadow testing creates real incremental cost (extra inference on shadow targets + scoring compute + storage).
- Packaging should encourage safe use (sampling caps) while aligning with value.

**Proposed packaging direction (subject to PM-1845 decision)**
- Hosted:
  - Included: heuristic metrics + limited sampling caps.
  - Add-on: model-graded scoring pack (billed per scored request or per 1K scored requests) with higher retention.
- Dedicated:
  - Included: shadow routing + heuristics.
  - Add-on: model-graded scoring (bundled commit) + higher retention and examples.
- Private:
  - Base: feature available; scoring options depend on deployment constraints.
  - Add-on: compliance pack for extended audit/retention controls where needed.

**Console cost transparency requirements**
- Experiment creation flow must estimate incremental costs:
  - Shadow inference cost (by sampling % and target type)
  - Scoring cost (heuristic vs model-graded)
  - Storage/retention cost impact (if applicable)

---

## Beta customer plan
### Beta selection criteria
- Clear upcoming model/version/quantization change (time-bound decision).
- Willing to run shadow experiments for at least 2–4 weeks.
- Has a defined “quality owner” and a platform admin to configure policies.
- Accepts default rubric metrics (or can map them to internal expectations).

### Proposed beta candidates
1. **Northstar Customer Support AI** (Dedicated prospect / early adopter)
   - Why: high sensitivity to instruction following + safety; needs quality gates for upgrades.
   - Ask: “shadow evals during rollout” aligns directly.

2. **Aurora DevTools** (high-volume, cost-sensitive)
   - Why: can validate sampling guidance and cardinality safety under load.
   - Focus: dashboards, segmentation, cost transparency.

3. **HelioFin RegTech** (regulated fintech)
   - Why: validates retention/redaction defaults, audit logs, and Private/Dedicated controls.
   - Focus: “no examples stored” mode, auditability, data residency constraints.

### Beta commitments (what we provide)
- Dedicated Slack/CS channel + weekly check-ins.
- Preconfigured dashboards + alert templates.
- Guidance on sampling rates and minimum sample sizes.

### Exit criteria for GA
- Beta customers can run experiments without Redwood engineer intervention.
- No Sev-1/Sev-2 incidents attributable to shadow traffic for 30 days.
- Documented playbook for enable/disable and troubleshooting.

---

## Security, compliance, and data handling
### Data classification
- Shadow artifacts may contain customer prompts/contexts and model outputs (potentially sensitive).

### Default controls
- Redaction: configurable allowlist + truncation + hashing of stable identifiers.
- Retention: short by default; longer retention requires explicit tenant setting and role.
- Access: RBAC-gated; example drilldowns require elevated permission; all access is audited.
- Export: disabled by default for Hosted; configurable for Dedicated/Private.

### Private deployment considerations
- Support local storage backends and customer-managed keys.
- Support SIEM export of audit events.
- Allow disabling model-graded scoring if compute or policy prohibits.

---

## Risks & mitigations
1. **Risk: Shadowing impacts tail latency or overloads data plane**
   - Mitigation: strict timeouts, async processing, circuit breaker, sampling caps, perf canary gates.

2. **Risk: Telemetry cardinality explosion**
   - Mitigation: dimension allowlist, bucketing, server-side enforcement, pre-launch review with Observability.

3. **Risk: Misleading metrics (false positives / poor calibration)**
   - Mitigation: ship limited, well-defined bundle; label metrics as “proxy”; require confidence intervals; include example-driven diagnostics.

4. **Risk: Privacy/regulatory violations**
   - Mitigation: default short retention, redaction-first storage, RBAC + audit, tenant-level opt-outs.

---

## Dependencies
- Control plane shadow policy schema + validation (Platform Control Plane).
- Smart routing mirroring + deterministic bucketing (Routing).
- Telemetry event schemas + pipelines (Telemetry/Analytics).
- Scoring workers + eval-harness integration (Applied ML/Evals).
- Console UI + APIs + RBAC (Console & Tooling).
- SRE guardrails, dashboards, and alerting policy (SRE/Observability).

---

## Open questions
1. What is the default sampling % for Hosted vs Dedicated (1% vs 0.5%) given expected scoring costs?
2. Should we allow >2 shadow variants in v1 for enterprise tenants, or hold for v2?
3. Which rubric model(s) are acceptable for model-graded scoring in Private deployments (customer-supplied vs Redwood-supplied) and how is that configured?
4. What is the GA stance on “automatic rollback” using quality signals: remain advisory, or allow opt-in thresholds?
5. What is the default behavior for storing examples (off by default vs on with strict retention) by deployment mode?

---

## Appendix: v1 requirements checklist (for launch readiness)
- [ ] Shadow policy create/update audited (who, when, what changed)
- [ ] Kill switch per-tenant and per-experiment
- [ ] p99 overhead validated under perf canary suite
- [ ] Scoring lag dashboards + alerts + runbook links
- [ ] UI shows confidence intervals and minimum sample guidance
- [ ] Cardinality enforcement validated with synthetic worst-case tags
- [ ] Retention/redaction defaults signed off by Security and documented
text:
## Summary
Teams trust Redwood for safe rollouts based on latency/cost/error SLOs, but still lack a production-safe way to detect *quality* regressions (correctness, safety, instruction following, format adherence) when changing models/versions/quantization/prompt templates. This PRD proposes **Shadow Traffic**: duplicate a sampled subset of live requests to one or more candidate variants, score outputs asynchronously, and surface **quality deltas** alongside existing performance and error metrics—without affecting the user-visible response.

Shadow Traffic is designed to work across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments using existing routing/rollout primitives (route configs, canaries, A/B) and Redwood’s eval-harness scoring templates.

---

## Problem statement
### What customers do today
- Run offline prompt sets that do not match production distribution.
- Manually sample production logs and run ad-hoc eval scripts (slow, non-deterministic, often violates retention policies).
- Deploy canaries and hope user feedback/support tickets surface regressions.

### Why this fails
- Quality regressions often show up only under real production context (long contexts, tool outputs, domain-specific formats).
- Customers cannot accept user-visible risk during testing.
- Platform teams need consistent metrics and governance across many routes/teams.

### Customer pain (verbatim themes)
- “We can’t justify switching models without a quality dashboard we trust.”
- “We need a pre-flight quality gate during rollouts—not a postmortem.”
- “We need strict retention/redaction; we cannot store prompts indefinitely.”

---

## Goals
1. **Production-safe quality measurement**: shadow requests must never change user-visible responses and must be fail-open.
2. **Statistically meaningful deltas**: confidence intervals and minimum sample guidance built into the experience.
3. **Rollouts integration**: attach shadow experiment results to canary/A-B rollouts and support regression alerts.
4. **Minimal overhead**: strict budgets for latency impact, telemetry cardinality, and scoring compute.
5. **Security + compliance by default**: data minimization, redaction, short retention defaults, audit logs.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Full offline replay for arbitrary historical traffic beyond short retention.
- Executing arbitrary customer-supplied eval code inside Redwood (v1 focuses on Redwood-provided metrics + bring-your-own prompt sets via eval-harness where supported).
- Automatic rollback based on quality signals at launch (advisory-first; revisit post-beta).

---

## Target users & personas
### Persona A: ML Engineer (Quality owner)
**Jobs to be done**
- Compare candidate vs control quality during model/version/prompt changes.
- Understand *why* quality changed (examples, segments, rubric breakdown).
- Confidently recommend ship/rollback.

**Pain points**
- Offline eval sets miss production edge cases.
- Hard to correlate quality with latency/cost changes.
- Requires building internal pipelines.

### Persona B: Platform Engineer (Inference platform owner)
**Jobs to be done**
- Roll out changes safely across routes and teams.
- Enforce governance (sampling caps, data handling policies, RBAC).
- Avoid performance regressions and metrics stack overload.

**Pain points**
- High-cardinality metrics melt dashboards.
- Shadowing can create unpredictable cost if unbounded.
- Needs auditability and kill switches.

### Persona C: SRE / Observability Engineer
**Jobs to be done**
- Ensure shadow path does not impact user path.
- Detect scoring pipeline degradation (lag, backlog) and respond.

---

## User stories
1. **Create shadow experiment on a route**
   - As an ML Eng, I choose a route, specify sampling %, select 1–2 candidate variants (model version or config), and start.
2. **View quality deltas with confidence intervals**
   - As an ML Eng, I see rubric scores and heuristic metrics comparing control vs candidates over time and by segment.
3. **Drill into examples**
   - As an ML Eng, I can view redacted prompts/outputs (where allowed) for a small sample of flagged cases.
4. **Attach to rollout**
   - As a Platform Eng, I run a canary rollout and view shadow quality signals in the rollout timeline.
5. **Governance + safety**
   - As a Platform Eng, I cap sampling and scoring budgets per org/tenant and can disable shadowing instantly.

---

## Product scope (v1)
### In-scope
- Shadow traffic routing policy attached to a **route** and/or **rollout**.
- Deterministic request fingerprinting + bucketing for consistent comparisons.
- Async scoring pipeline for a default metric bundle (see below).
- Console UI: create experiment, results dashboard, drilldown examples (permission-gated), export.
- Alerting for quality regressions and scoring pipeline health.
- Hosted, Dedicated, Private support with environment-appropriate retention/storage defaults.

### Out of scope
- Unlimited number of candidates per experiment (v1: max 2 shadow variants per control).
- Arbitrary custom metrics plugins executed inside Redwood.

---

## Definitions
- **Control**: the variant that serves the user response.
- **Shadow variant**: candidate variant that receives mirrored requests but does not serve responses.
- **Shadow experiment**: configuration + time window + scoring rubric version.
- **Quality delta**: difference in metric distributions between control and candidate with confidence bounds.

---

## Functional requirements
### 1) Experiment configuration
- Create shadow experiment from:
  - A route (standalone), or
  - A rollout object (canary/A-B), linked for traceability.
- Required fields:
  - Control target (route current target)
  - Shadow targets (1–2)
  - Sampling rate (default 1%, configurable with caps)
  - Fingerprinting key selection (default: stable hash of normalized prompt + request metadata; excludes raw PII fields)
  - Runtime guardrails (timeouts, circuit breaker behavior)
  - Scoring bundle + rubric version pin
- Optional fields:
  - Segment filters (e.g., request_type=chat vs completion; region; model family)
  - Request field allowlist for storage (Dedicated/Private may expand)
  - Customer-defined tags (restricted list; validated against cardinality policy)

### 2) Shadow routing behavior
- Fail-open: shadow failures must not affect the user-visible path.
- Strict timeouts on shadow requests.
- Backpressure + circuit breaker:
  - If shadow queue backlog or shadow target error rate exceeds thresholds, auto-disable shadow forwarding for that experiment (with audit event + banner in Console).
- Streaming support:
  - Control response streams as normal.
  - Shadow captures are bounded (e.g., up to N tokens or until timeout) with explicit “partial_capture” markers.

### 3) Data capture & privacy
- Default capture: minimal fields needed for scoring (see “Data handling” section).
- Redaction pipeline applied before persistence.
- Retention defaults:
  - Hosted: short retention (default 7 days for scored aggregates; 24–72 hours for sampled examples).
  - Dedicated: configurable retention windows with org admin approval.
  - Private: customer-controlled storage location and retention; support “no examples stored” mode.

### 4) Scoring pipeline
- Asynchronous scoring with retry/dedupe.
- Support:
  - Heuristic metrics (fast, deterministic)
  - Model-graded rubric metrics (slower, costed)
- Store:
  - Per-request scores (short retention)
  - Aggregates by time window + safe dimensions (longer retention)
- Rubric version pinning:
  - Experiment results always labeled with rubric version; comparisons across rubric versions are not shown by default.

### 5) Console UX (v1 must-haves)
- “Shadow Experiments” page: create, list, status, last data received, scoring lag indicator.
- Results view:
  - Metric deltas with confidence intervals
  - Trend over time (hour/day)
  - Segment filters (cardinality-safe)
  - Clear “not enough samples yet” states with guidance
- Examples drilldown:
  - Permission-gated (RBAC + audit)
  - Redacted views by default; download/export gated by role
- Rollout integration:
  - Rollout timeline shows linked shadow experiment status and top regressions.

---

## Default quality metric bundle (v1)
v1 ships a **default metric bundle** intended to cover common regression types while keeping costs and ambiguity manageable.

### A) Heuristic metrics (low cost, deterministic)
1. **Format adherence**
   - Checks: valid JSON, required keys present, schema match, no extraneous text.
   - Inputs: output text; optional schema.
   - Caveat: strict schemas can over-penalize benign variations.

2. **Refusal rate (proxy)**
   - Detect refusals using pattern + classifier heuristics.
   - Caveat: false positives for safe disclaimers.

3. **Tool/function calling structure validity** (if applicable)
   - Valid tool name + arguments parse + required args.

4. **Length/verbosity drift**
   - Mean/median output tokens; tail behavior.
   - Used as a diagnostic, not a “quality score.”

5. **Error-classification deltas**
   - Increase in empty outputs, truncation markers, partial streaming captures.

### B) Model-graded metrics (higher cost; async)
1. **Instruction following (rubric score 1–5)**
   - Model-graded rubric prompts tuned for general instruction compliance.
   - Requires stable rubric model/version.

2. **Safety / policy compliance (rubric score + flags)**
   - Detects unsafe content categories and refusal appropriateness.
   - Output: categorical flags + score.

3. **Grounding/faithfulness proxy (where applicable)**
   - For requests with provided context: checks whether answer cites/uses given context.
   - Note: v1 is a proxy, not a formal factuality guarantee.

### v1 metric exclusions (explicit)
- Domain-specific correctness (requires custom evals; roadmap).
- Full jailbreak robustness testing (separate security program).

---

## Dimensions & segmentation (cardinality-safe)
v1 supports a restricted set of dimensions for aggregation:
- route_id (bucketed where needed)
- deployment_mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- region
- request_type (chat/completions/embeddings where relevant)
- model_id (control + shadow)
- rubric_version
- outcome (success/error/timeout/partial_capture)

Explicitly not supported as aggregation dimensions in v1:
- customer_user_id
- prompt_id (unless bucketed/coarsened)
- raw customer tags beyond an allowlisted set

---

## Success criteria
### Product success (beta)
- At least **3 beta customers** actively running shadow experiments weekly.
- Customers can make a ship/rollback decision using the dashboard within **< 30 minutes** of review time.
- At least **2 documented saves** where shadow metrics caught a regression before user exposure.

### Technical success
- **User-path impact**:
  - p99 latency impact on control path: **<= 1%** at default sampling.
  - No increased error rates attributable to shadowing.
- **Shadow path reliability**:
  - Shadow forwarding success rate (when enabled): **>= 99%** excluding shadow target errors.
  - Scoring pipeline: **95% of scored results available within 30 minutes** (target; measure separately by metric type).
- **Observability safety**:
  - No sustained metrics cardinality alerts from enabling shadow traffic under recommended configs.

### Trust & interpretability
- Confidence intervals shown for all primary deltas.
- Clear UI indicators for: insufficient sample size, scoring lag, partial captures, rubric version.

---

## Rollout plan
### Phase 0 (Requirements + architecture) — 2 weeks
- Finalize ADRs (routing placement, telemetry model).
- Define metric bundle v1 and rubric versioning policy.
- Sign off retention/redaction defaults with Security.

### Phase 1 (MVP + dogfood) — 4–6 weeks
- Shadow routing MVP + deterministic bucketing.
- Telemetry events v1 + basic aggregates.
- Scoring pipeline for heuristics + 1 model-graded rubric (instruction following).
- Internal dogfood on Redwood-owned tenants; weekly review.

### Phase 2 (Console + alerts + rollout integration) — 4 weeks
- Console create/view flows + example drilldowns.
- Attach to rollouts (timeline + linkage).
- Regression alerts (advisory) + scoring lag alerts.

### Phase 3 (Hardening + GA readiness) — 4–6 weeks
- Overhead benchmarks + backpressure tuning.
- Expanded rubric set (safety + format adherence + faithfulness proxy).
- Docs + SDK support; enablement.

---

## Pricing & packaging assumptions (v1)
**Principles**
- Shadow testing creates real incremental cost (extra inference on shadow targets + scoring compute + storage).
- Packaging should encourage safe use (sampling caps) while aligning with value.

**Proposed packaging direction (subject to PM-1845 decision)**
- Hosted:
  - Included: heuristic metrics + limited sampling caps.
  - Add-on: model-graded scoring pack (billed per scored request or per 1K scored requests) with higher retention.
- Dedicated:
  - Included: shadow routing + heuristics.
  - Add-on: model-graded scoring (bundled commit) + higher retention and examples.
- Private:
  - Base: feature available; scoring options depend on deployment constraints.
  - Add-on: compliance pack for extended audit/retention controls where needed.

**Console cost transparency requirements**
- Experiment creation flow must estimate incremental costs:
  - Shadow inference cost (by sampling % and target type)
  - Scoring cost (heuristic vs model-graded)
  - Storage/retention cost impact (if applicable)

---

## Beta customer plan
### Beta selection criteria
- Clear upcoming model/version/quantization change (time-bound decision).
- Willing to run shadow experiments for at least 2–4 weeks.
- Has a defined “quality owner” and a platform admin to configure policies.

1. **Northstar Customer Support AI** (Dedicated prospect / early adopter)
   - Why: high sensitivity to instruction following + safety; needs quality gates for upgrades.
   - Ask: “shadow evals during rollout” aligns directly.

2. **Aurora DevTools** (high-volume, cost-sensitive)
   - Why: can validate sampling guidance and cardinality safety under load.
   - Focus: dashboards, segmentation, cost transparency.

### Beta commitments (what we provide)

---

title: Console UX Spec — Shadow Experiments (Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics)
preview:
## 1. Overview
Shadow Experiments let customers mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy), score the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surface *quality deltas* alongside cost/latency/error metrics—without affecting the response returned to the end user.

This document specifies the **Console UX** for:
- Creating and managing Shadow Experiments
- Viewing results (quality deltas, confidence intervals, segments)
- Drilling into examples with safe data handling
- Attaching quality signals to Rollouts/Canaries

### 1.1 Goals
- Make it safe and obvious that shadowing does **not** change user responses and is **fail-open**.
- Help engineers answer: “Is the candidate variant materially worse/better on quality, and are we statistically confident?”
- Provide clear integration with Rollouts so quality signals can be reviewed during canaries and (later) used for automated rollback.

### 1.2 Non-goals (v1)
- Full offline replay across arbitrary historical windows.
- Customer-provided custom code execution inside Redwood.
- Deep prompt editing / prompt version management (shadow experiments can reference prompt template IDs/versions, but prompt authoring lives elsewhere).

### 1.3 Assumptions / dependencies
- Shadow routing policy exists and is enforceable at the route/rollout level (sampling %, shadow targets, timeouts, fail-open).
- Telemetry produces control+shadow paired events with bounded cardinality.
- Scoring pipeline produces a metric bundle keyed by (experiment_id, variant_id, time_bucket, segment).
- The “default metric bundle v1” is available (format adherence, refusal/safety, instruction-following rubric, basic heuristics). Additional bundles can be introduced via rubric versions.

---

## 2. Key concepts & terminology
- **Control**: The variant serving the user response.
- **Shadow target / Variant**: Candidate configuration receiving mirrored requests (does not serve user response).
- **Shadow Experiment**: A named configuration that defines sampling, targets, metrics bundle, and retention settings.
- **Run**: A time-bounded execution of an experiment (start/stop, config snapshot). In v1, we model “run” implicitly via an experiment’s active window + config revisions; UI will show “Runs” as ranges.
- **Metric bundle**: Versioned set of quality metrics and scoring rubrics.
- **Delta**: Shadow minus control for a metric (or ratio where relevant), with confidence interval.
- **Segment**: Filterable breakdown dimension (e.g., region, request_type, route, model family bucket). Segment options are limited by cardinality rules.

---

## 3. Information architecture (IA)
### 3.1 Navigation entry points
1) **Console → Observability → Quality → Shadow Experiments**
- Landing page lists experiments and recent regressions.

2) **Console → Routes → [Route] → Quality (tab)**
- “Start Shadow Experiment” CTA pre-fills the route and control variant.

3) **Console → Rollouts → [Rollout] → Quality signals (panel)**
- Inline panel showing attached experiment status and headline deltas.

### 3.2 Pages (v1)
- Shadow Experiments (list)
- Create Shadow Experiment (wizard)
- Shadow Experiment details
  - Overview (status, config snapshot)
  - Results (delta charts, segments)
  - Examples (sampled, redacted)
  - Activity/Audit (who changed what)

---

## 4. Permissions & access model
Shadow Experiments touch production request/response artifacts and therefore require explicit RBAC handling.

### 4.1 Roles (Console RBAC primitives)
We map to existing org/project permissions; naming below reflects UI labels (final mapping owned by Console backend/RBAC).

| Permission | Who typically has it | What it enables |
|---|---|---|
| View Experiments (metadata) | Project Viewer+ | List experiments, see metrics aggregates (no raw examples). |
| Create/Modify Experiments | Project Admin, Route Editor | Create, start/stop, edit sampling/targets within guardrails. |
| View Examples (redacted) | Project Admin by default; optionally enabled for specific groups | See redacted prompts/outputs and rubric breakdowns. |
| Export Examples | Restricted; disabled by default in Dedicated/Private unless explicitly allowed | CSV/JSON export of sampled examples. |
| Attach to Rollouts | Rollout Admin | Link an experiment to a rollout/canary step. |

### 4.2 Data sensitivity guardrails (UX)
- The UI must clearly label any table that includes request text as **“Sensitive (Redacted)”**.
- For tenants with stricter settings (Dedicated/Private):
  - Examples tab may be hidden entirely.
  - Export controls may be disabled.
  - Retention defaults are displayed and cannot be extended without explicit admin approval.

### 4.3 Audit visibility
All create/edit/start/stop actions show an “Audit” drawer with:
- Actor
- Timestamp
- Fields changed
- Config revision hash
- Link to the global Audit Log entry

---

## 5. User flows

### 5.1 Flow A — Create a shadow experiment (wizard)
Entry points:
- “Shadow Experiments” list → **Create experiment**
- Route page → Quality tab → **Start shadow experiment**
- Rollout page → Quality signals → **Attach new experiment**

Wizard steps:
1) **Basics**
   - Name (required)
   - Description (optional)
   - Scope: Route (required) + Environment (prod/staging) (if applicable)
   - Control variant (pre-filled from route; editable only if user has route edit permission)

2) **Shadow targets**
   - Add 1..N shadow variants
   - Variant types (UI selection):
     - Model version (e.g., “llama-3.1-70b@2026-01-10”)
     - Quantization profile (e.g., “int8-kv-cache”) 
     - Prompt template version (reference ID)
     - Routing policy (pre-defined policy ID)
   - Show estimated incremental cost per request (rough order) when available

3) **Sampling & bucketing**
   - Sampling rate (%), default 1%
   - Bucketing mode (read-only in v1; shown for transparency): “Deterministic request fingerprinting (consistent across time)”
   - Optional: “Exclude requests with these headers/attributes” (advanced; limited to safe low-cardinality fields)

4) **Metrics & scoring**
   - Metric bundle (default “Quality v1”) + version
   - Scoring latency expectation (e.g., “Typically 2–10 minutes; may be longer under load”)
   - Warning callout: “Quality metrics are asynchronous; use confidence intervals before making decisions.”

5) **Data handling**
   - Retention window (default: 24h for examples; aggregates 30d) — exact defaults are tenant-mode dependent
   - Redaction mode (read-only default in v1; “Redact known sensitive patterns + truncate”) with link to policy
   - Examples availability toggle (if tenant policy allows)

6) **Review & start**
   - Summary of config + guardrails
   - “Start experiment” button
   - “Start in paused state” (optional for admins; to validate config without producing data)

Success state:
- Redirect to experiment details (Overview → Results)
- Show “Data will appear shortly” empty state with ETAs

Failure states:
- Validation error (e.g., sampling too high; unsupported variant type for route)
- Policy denies (e.g., retention extension not allowed)
- Partial success: experiment created but unable to start (data-plane rollout pending) → show remediation steps


### 5.2 Flow B — View experiment results (decision-oriented)
Primary questions:
- Is the candidate variant’s quality statistically worse/better than control?
- Are we segmenting by a factor that indicates localized regression?
- Is scoring healthy (lag/backlog), or are results incomplete?

Steps:
1) Open experiment → Results tab
2) Select a metric (left panel)
3) Observe headline delta + CI and sample size
4) Apply segment filter(s) to locate regressions
5) Drill into Examples for representative failures


### 5.3 Flow C — Attach quality signals to a rollout
From Rollout detail page:
- Quality signals panel shows:
  - Attached experiment(s)
  - Current status (Active/Paused/Stopped)
  - Headline metrics (top 3) with deltas
  - Scoring health (lag, completeness)

Actions:
- Attach existing experiment (must match route + environment)
- Create new attached experiment (opens wizard pre-filled)
- Define “Quality gate thresholds” (v1: advisory only)

v1 behavior:
- Quality gates generate **warnings** in the rollout timeline.
- Rollout automation (auto-pause/rollback) remains manual in v1 (future flag).

---

## 6. UI specification by page

### 6.1 Shadow Experiments — list page
**Table columns**
- Name
- Route
- Control variant
- Shadow variants (count + chips)
- Status (Active/Paused/Stopped)
- Sampling rate
- Last 24h headline: “Regressions detected” badge (if any)
- Updated

**Default sorting**: Updated desc

**Filters**
- Route
- Status
- Owner/creator
- Time range (for headline signals)

**Empty states**
- No experiments: explanation + “Create experiment”
- Permission-limited: show metadata only; hide CTAs requiring elevated roles


### 6.2 Create Shadow Experiment — wizard
**Design requirements**
- Every step includes a “Why this matters” inline helper for first-time users.
- Guardrails are shown as *constraints*, not as hidden errors:
  - Sampling max (tenant/plan dependent)
  - Shadow target concurrency max
  - Max retained examples

**Warning callouts (must-have)**
- “Shadow traffic is mirrored; it does not change user responses.”
- “Shadowing may increase compute and telemetry costs.”
- “Shadow path is fail-open; if shadow targets fail, production responses continue normally.”


### 6.3 Shadow Experiment — Overview tab
**Header**
- Experiment name
- Status pill: Active / Paused / Stopped
- Primary actions:
  - Pause / Resume
  - Stop
  - Edit (creates a new config revision)

**Overview cards**
1) Config snapshot
   - Route
   - Control variant
   - Shadow variants
   - Sampling rate
   - Metrics bundle + version
   - Retention settings

2) Health
   - Shadow dispatch success rate (last 15m)
   - Scoring lag (p50/p95)
   - Data completeness: “% of mirrored pairs scored”

3) Activity
   - Recent config changes
   - Linked rollout (if attached)


### 6.4 Shadow Experiment — Results tab (core)
The Results tab is split into three zones:
- Left: Metric selector
- Center: Chart + headline delta
- Right: Filters and segments

#### 6.4.1 Metric selector (left)
Grouped sections:
- Safety & refusal
- Format adherence
- Instruction following (rubric)
- Heuristics

Each metric row shows:
- Metric name
- Directionality indicator (higher is better / lower is better)
- Latest delta mini-sparkline


#### 6.4.2 Headline delta (top of center)
Shows selected metric’s current evaluation window:
- **Delta value** (Shadow – Control) with sign coloring
- **95% confidence interval** (always shown; never optional)
- Sample size: number of paired requests scored
- “Data freshness” indicator: last scored timestamp

Decision labels (display-only):
- “Likely regression” if CI excludes 0 in the bad direction
- “Likely improvement” if CI excludes 0 in good direction
- “Inconclusive” otherwise

Note: Labels are informational and do not imply causal proof.


#### 6.4.3 Delta chart (center)
Chart types:
- **Time series delta** (default)
  - X: time
  - Y: delta
  - Band: confidence interval
- **Distribution comparison** (secondary)
  - Control vs Shadow score distributions for the chosen window

Chart requirements:
- Confidence bands must be visually distinct and colorblind-safe.
- Hover tooltip shows:
  - Control metric value
  - Shadow metric value
  - Delta
  - CI
  - Sample size
  - Scoring completion %

Time range selector:
- 1h, 6h, 24h, 7d (availability depends on retention)

Annotations:
- Config revision changes appear as vertical markers (“Sampling changed 1% → 2%”).
- Rollout events (if attached) appear as markers (“Canary step 2 started”).


#### 6.4.4 Segments & filters (right)
Filters are limited to cardinality-safe dimensions.

Default available segments (subject to telemetry policy):
- Region
- Request type (chat/completions/embeddings where applicable; shadow experiments focus on generation endpoints)
- Model family bucket (high-level)
- Token length bucket (short/medium/long)
- HTTP status bucket (2xx/4xx/5xx)

UI behavior:
- Multi-select, with “Top N segments by volume” option.
- When segmenting, show:
  - Per-segment delta table with CI
  - Small “volume share” bar
  - Sort by worst regression first

Guardrails:
- Do not allow free-form labels.
- If a user tries to segment by a disallowed dimension, show an explanation and link to the observability standard.


### 6.5 Shadow Experiment — Examples tab
Purpose: help debug *why* a metric changed, not serve as a data export feature.

**Access**: gated by “View Examples (redacted)” permission.

**Layout**
- Top controls: time range, metric filter (optional), segment filter (optional)
- Table/list of examples with:
  - Timestamp
  - Request fingerprint (hashed)
  - Segment chips (safe)
  - Control summary (short)
  - Shadow summary (short)
  - Metric outcomes (e.g., rubric score, refusal flag)

**Example detail drawer**
- Request (redacted + truncated)
- Control output (redacted)
- Shadow output (redacted)
- Rubric breakdown (human-readable)
- Links:
  - “Open in trace” (if available)
  - “Copy redacted payload” (copy only what’s displayed)

**Safety requirements**
- Never display raw identifiers (customer_id, user_id, email) even if present in prompt.
- Prompts/outputs are truncated with a visible “truncated” indicator.
- Provide “Report redaction miss” action (creates internal ticket; does not reveal raw text).

**Export (v1)**
- If enabled by tenant policy: export current filtered view up to N rows (default N=500).
- Export includes only redacted fields and excludes any internal IDs except fingerprint.


### 6.6 Shadow Experiment — Activity/Audit tab
- Config revision history (diff view)
- Start/stop/pause events
- Attachment events (linked to rollout)
- Links to Audit Log entries

---

## 7. Rollout integration UX
### 7.1 Rollout page — Quality signals panel
Panel placement: right rail (desktop), collapsible section (mobile).

Panel shows:
- Attached experiment (name + status)
- Last 24h headline:
  - 3 selected metrics (configurable; default: refusal rate, format adherence, instruction-following score)
  - Each with delta + CI
- Scoring health:
  - Lag p95
  - Completeness %

Actions:
- “View details” (opens experiment Results tab)
- “Edit attachment” (change which experiment is attached)
- “Configure quality gate” (v1: advisory)


### 7.2 Rollout timeline annotations
When attached, the rollout timeline includes:
- Markers when the experiment starts/stops
- Markers for detected likely regressions (advisory), e.g., “Refusal rate regression (CI excludes 0)”


### 7.3 Quality gate configuration (v1)
UI is present but clearly labeled “Advisory (no automated rollback in v1)”.

Gate configuration fields:
- Metric
- Threshold type: absolute delta / relative % change
- Direction: regression only / improvement only / both
- Minimum sample size requirement
- Required confidence (fixed at 95% in v1; displayed as non-editable)

When gate triggers:
- Add warning banner on rollout page
- Add timeline event
- Optional: send notification (Slack/email integration outside scope; UI shows “Notifications configured by org admins”)

---

## 8. System states & messaging

### 8.1 Scoring lag / incomplete data
If scoring is behind, Results tab shows:
- Banner: “Scoring delayed—results may be incomplete.”
- The headline area must include completeness % and last-scored timestamp.
- Charts should visually gray out the most recent interval if completeness < X% (threshold defined by observability standard; propose X=80%).

### 8.2 Shadow dispatch degradation (fail-open)
If shadow target errors increase:
- Overview Health card shows elevated error rate.
- Show banner: “Shadow targets failing; production responses unaffected (fail-open).”
- Provide link: “How to disable shadowing” (runbook).

### 8.3 No data / low sample size
- If sample size below minimum (propose <200 pairs): show “Inconclusive—insufficient samples” and nudge to:
  - increase sampling (if allowed)
  - extend time range
  - reduce segments

---

## 9. Copy requirements (must-have strings)
These strings are specifically called out to avoid ambiguity:
- “Shadow traffic mirrors requests to candidate variants but always returns the control response to users.”
- “Fail-open: if shadowing fails, production traffic continues normally.”
- “Quality metrics are asynchronous and may lag by minutes.”
- “Confidence intervals indicate uncertainty; avoid decisions on small samples.”
- “Examples are redacted and truncated; do not use this view for data export unless explicitly enabled.”

---

## 10. Analytics & instrumentation (Console)
Console events (for product understanding; not customer-facing):
- shadow_experiments.create_started / create_completed
- shadow_experiments.pause / resume / stop
- shadow_experiments.metric_selected
- shadow_experiments.segment_applied
- shadow_experiments.example_opened
- rollouts.quality_panel_opened

Guardrails:
- Do not include prompt text in Console analytics.

---

## 11. Open questions
1) **Run modeling:** Do we explicitly create immutable “run” objects in the backend for every start/stop cycle, or infer runs from state transitions? UX can support either; preferred is explicit runs for clearer comparison.
2) **Metric directionality:** For each v1 metric, confirm “higher is better” vs “lower is better” and standardize visual semantics.
3) **Threshold defaults:** Should the UI suggest default gate thresholds per metric (e.g., +0.5% refusal) or keep entirely user-defined?
4) **Segments availability by plan:** Confirm which segments are always allowed in Dedicated/Private and which are hosted-only.
5) **Export policy:** Confirm defaults (off by default) and the approval mechanism to enable.

---

## 12. Acceptance criteria (UX)
- A Project Admin can create an experiment from a Route page in <5 minutes without docs.
- Results show delta + CI everywhere a delta is shown (no “naked deltas”).
- Rollout page displays attached experiment headline signals and health.
- Examples tab is permission-gated and clearly labeled redacted/truncated.
- All error/empty states explain what is happening and how to recover (increase time range, sampling, remove segments, check scoring health).

---

## 13. Appendix — Proposed default metric tiles (v1)
These appear in the “headline metrics” set on overview/rollout panels:
- Refusal rate (lower is better)
- Format adherence (higher is better)
- Instruction-following rubric score (higher is better)

Secondary (available in metric list):
- Safety policy violation proxy (lower is better)
- JSON/Schema validity (higher is better)
- Verbosity drift (token length delta; interpret with caution)

(Exact definitions live in Applied ML / Evals docs; UI displays names and short descriptions only.)
text:
## 1. Overview
Shadow Experiments let customers mirror a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants (candidate model/version/quantization/prompt/routing policy), score the shadow outputs asynchronously, and surface *quality deltas* alongside cost/latency/error metrics—without affecting the response returned to the end user.

This document specifies the **Console UX** for:
- Creating and managing Shadow Experiments
- Viewing results (quality deltas, confidence intervals, segments)
- Drilling into examples with safe data handling
- Attaching quality signals to Rollouts/Canaries

### 1.1 Goals
- Make it safe and obvious that shadowing does **not** change user responses and is **fail-open**.
- Help engineers answer: “Is the candidate variant materially worse/better on quality, and are we statistically confident?”
- Provide clear integration with Rollouts so quality signals can be reviewed during canaries and (later) used for automated rollback.

### 1.2 Non-goals (v1)
- Full offline replay across arbitrary historical windows.
- Customer-provided custom code execution inside Redwood.
- Deep prompt editing / prompt version management (shadow experiments can reference prompt template IDs/versions, but prompt authoring lives elsewhere).

### 1.3 Assumptions / dependencies
- Shadow routing policy exists and is enforceable at the route/rollout level (sampling %, shadow targets, timeouts, fail-open).
- Telemetry produces control+shadow paired events with bounded cardinality.
- Scoring pipeline produces a metric bundle keyed by (experiment_id, variant_id, time_bucket, segment).
- The “default metric bundle v1” is available (format adherence, refusal/safety, instruction-following rubric, basic heuristics). Additional bundles can be introduced via rubric versions.

---

## 2. Key concepts & terminology
- **Control**: The variant serving the user response.
- **Shadow target / Variant**: Candidate configuration receiving mirrored requests (does not serve user response).
- **Shadow Experiment**: A named configuration that defines sampling, targets, metrics bundle, and retention settings.
- **Run**: A time-bounded execution of an experiment (start/stop, config snapshot). In v1, we model “run” implicitly via an experiment’s active window + config revisions; UI will show “Runs” as ranges.
- **Metric bundle**: Versioned set of quality metrics and scoring rubrics.
- **Delta**: Shadow minus control for a metric (or ratio where relevant), with confidence interval.
- **Segment**: Filterable breakdown dimension (e.g., region, request_type, route, model family bucket). Segment options are limited by cardinality rules.

---

## 3. Information architecture (IA)
### 3.1 Navigation entry points
1) **Console → Observability → Quality → Shadow Experiments**
- Landing page lists experiments and recent regressions.

2) **Console → Routes → [Route] → Quality (tab)**
- “Start Shadow Experiment” CTA pre-fills the route and control variant.

3) **Console → Rollouts → [Rollout] → Quality signals (panel)**
- Inline panel showing attached experiment status and headline deltas.

### 3.2 Pages (v1)
- Shadow Experiments (list)
- Create Shadow Experiment (wizard)
- Shadow Experiment details
  - Overview (status, config snapshot)
  - Results (delta charts, segments)
  - Examples (sampled, redacted)
  - Activity/Audit (who changed what)

---

## 4. Permissions & access model
Shadow Experiments touch production request/response artifacts and therefore require explicit RBAC handling.

### 4.1 Roles (Console RBAC primitives)
We map to existing org/project permissions; naming below reflects UI labels (final mapping owned by Console backend/RBAC).

| Permission | Who typically has it | What it enables |
|---|---|---|
| View Experiments (metadata) | Project Viewer+ | List experiments, see metrics aggregates (no raw examples). |
| Create/Modify Experiments | Project Admin, Route Editor | Create, start/stop, edit sampling/targets within guardrails. |
| View Examples (redacted) | Project Admin by default; optionally enabled for specific groups | See redacted prompts/outputs and rubric breakdowns. |
| Export Examples | Restricted; disabled by default in Dedicated/Private unless explicitly allowed | CSV/JSON export of sampled examples. |
| Attach to Rollouts | Rollout Admin | Link an experiment to a rollout/canary step. |

### 4.2 Data sensitivity guardrails (UX)
- The UI must clearly label any table that includes request text as **“Sensitive (Redacted)”**.
- For tenants with stricter settings (Dedicated/Private):
  - Examples tab may be hidden entirely.
  - Export controls may be disabled.
  - Retention defaults are displayed and cannot be extended without explicit admin approval.

### 4.3 Audit visibility
All create/edit/start/stop actions show an “Audit” drawer with:
- Actor
- Timestamp
- Fields changed
- Config revision hash
- Link to the global Audit Log entry

---

## 5. User flows

### 5.1 Flow A — Create a shadow experiment (wizard)
Entry points:
- “Shadow Experiments” list → **Create experiment**
- Route page → Quality tab → **Start shadow experiment**
- Rollout page → Quality signals → **Attach new experiment**

Wizard steps:
1) **Basics**
   - Name (required)
   - Description (optional)
   - Scope: Route (required) + Environment (prod/staging) (if applicable)
   - Control variant (pre-filled from route; editable only if user has route edit permission)

2) **Shadow targets**
   - Add 1..N shadow variants
   - Variant types (UI selection):
     - Model version (e.g., “llama-3.1-70b@2026-01-10”)
     - Quantization profile (e.g., “int8-kv-cache”) 
     - Prompt template version (reference ID)
     - Routing policy (pre-defined policy ID)
   - Show estimated incremental cost per request (rough order) when available

3) **Sampling & bucketing**
   - Sampling rate (%), default 1%
   - Bucketing mode (read-only in v1; shown for transparency): “Deterministic request fingerprinting (consistent across time)”
   - Optional: “Exclude requests with these headers/attributes” (advanced; limited to safe low-cardinality fields)

4) **Metrics & scoring**
   - Metric bundle (default “Quality v1”) + version
   - Scoring latency expectation (e.g., “Typically 2–10 minutes; may be longer under load”)
   - Warning callout: “Quality metrics are asynchronous; use confidence intervals before making decisions.”

5) **Data handling**
   - Retention window (default: 24h for examples; aggregates 30d) — exact defaults are tenant-mode dependent
   - Redaction mode (read-only default in v1; “Redact known sensitive patterns + truncate”) with link to policy
   - Examples availability toggle (if tenant policy allows)

6) **Review & start**
   - Summary of config + guardrails
   - “Start experiment” button
   - “Start in paused state” (optional for admins; to validate config without producing data)

Success state:
- Redirect to experiment details (Overview → Results)
- Show “Data will appear shortly” empty state with ETAs

Failure states:
- Validation error (e.g., sampling too high; unsupported variant type for route)
- Policy denies (e.g., retention extension not allowed)
- Partial success: experiment created but unable to start (data-plane rollout pending) → show remediation steps


### 5.2 Flow B — View experiment results (decision-oriented)
Primary questions:
- Is the candidate variant’s quality statistically worse/better than control?
- Are we segmenting by a factor that indicates localized regression?
- Is scoring healthy (lag/backlog), or are results incomplete?

Steps:
1) Open experiment → Results tab
2) Select a metric (left panel)
3) Observe headline delta + CI and sample size
4) Apply segment filter(s) to locate regressions
5) Drill into Examples for representative failures


### 5.3 Flow C — Attach quality signals to a rollout
From Rollout detail page:
- Quality signals panel shows:
  - Attached experiment(s)
  - Current status (Active/Paused/Stopped)
  - Headline metrics (top 3) with deltas
  - Scoring health (lag, completeness)

Actions:
- Attach existing experiment (must match route + environment)
- Create new attached experiment (opens wizard pre-filled)
- Define “Quality gate thresholds” (v1: advisory only)

v1 behavior:
- Quality gates generate **warnings** in the rollout timeline.
- Rollout automation (auto-pause/rollback) remains manual in v1 (future flag).

---

## 6. UI specification by page

### 6.1 Shadow Experiments — list page
**Table columns**
- Name
- Route
- Control variant
- Shadow variants (count + chips)
- Status (Active/Paused/Stopped)

- Updated

**Default sorting**: Updated desc

**Filters**
- Route
- Status
- Owner/creator
- Time range (for headline signals)

**Empty states**
- No experiments: explanation + “Create experiment”
- Permission-limited: show metadata only; hide CTAs requiring elevated roles


### 6.2 Create Shadow Experiment — wizard
**Design requirements**

- Guardrails are shown as *constraints*, not as hidden errors:
  - Sampling max (tenant/plan dependent)
  - Shadow target concurrency max
  - Max retained examples

**Warning callouts (must-have)**

- “Shadowing may increase compute and telemetry costs.”

### 6.3 Shadow Experiment — Overview tab
**Header**

- Status pill: Active / Paused / Stopped
- Primary actions:
  - Pause / Resume
  - Stop
  - Edit (creates a new config revision)

**Overview cards**
1) Config snapshot

2) Health

### 6.4 Shadow Experiment — Results tab (core)
The Results tab is split into three zones:
- Left: Metric selector
- Center: Chart + headline delta
- Right: Filters and segments

#### 6.4.1 Metric selector (left)
Grouped sections:
- Safety & refusal
- Format adherence

- Heuristics

Each metric row shows:

#### 6.4.2 Headline delta (top of center)

Note: Labels are informational and do not imply causal proof.


#### 6.4.3 Delta chart (center)

  - X: time
  - Y: delta
  - Band: confidence interval
- **Distribution comparison** (secondary)
  - Control vs Shadow score distributions for the chosen window

Chart requirements:

- Hover tooltip shows:
  - Control metric value
  - Shadow metric value
  - Delta
  - CI
  - Sample size
  - Scoring completion %

Time range selector:
- 1h, 6h, 24h, 7d (availability depends on retention)

Annotations:
- Config revision changes appear as vertical markers (“Sampling changed 1% → 2%”).
- Rollout events (if attached) appear as markers (“Canary step 2 started”).


#### 6.4.4 Segments & filters (right)
Filters are limited to cardinality-safe dimensions.

Default available segments (subject to telemetry policy):

UI behavior:

Guardrails:

### 6.5 Shadow Experiment — Examples tab
Purpose: help debug *why* a metric changed, not serve as a data export feature.

**Access**: gated by “View Examples (redacted)” permission.

**Layout**
- Top controls: time range, metric filter (optional), segment filter (optional)
- Table/list of examples with:
  - Timestamp
  - Request fingerprint (hashed)
  - Segment chips (safe)
  - Control summary (short)
  - Shadow summary (short)
  - Metric outcomes (e.g., rubric score, refusal flag)

**Example detail drawer**
- Request (redacted + truncated)
- Control output (redacted)
- Shadow output (redacted)
- Rubric breakdown (human-readable)
- Links:
  - “Open in trace” (if available)
  - “Copy redacted payload” (copy only what’s displayed)

**Safety requirements**
- Never display raw identifiers (customer_id, user_id, email) even if present in prompt.
- Prompts/outputs are truncated with a visible “truncated” indicator.
- Provide “Report redaction miss” action (creates internal ticket; does not reveal raw text).

**Export (v1)**
- If enabled by tenant policy: export current filtered view up to N rows (default N=500).
- Export includes only redacted fields and excludes any internal IDs except fingerprint.


### 6.6 Shadow Experiment — Activity/Audit tab
- Config revision history (diff view)
- Start/stop/pause events
- Attachment events (linked to rollout)
- Links to Audit Log entries

---

## 7. Rollout integration UX
### 7.1 Rollout page — Quality signals panel
Panel placement: right rail (desktop), collapsible section (mobile).

Panel shows:
- Attached experiment (name + status)
- Last 24h headline:
  - 3 selected metrics (configurable; default: refusal rate, format adherence, instruction-following score)
  - Each with delta + CI
- Scoring health:
  - Lag p95
  - Completeness %

Actions:
- “View details” (opens experiment Results tab)

- “Configure quality gate” (v1: advisory)


### 7.2 Rollout timeline annotations
When attached, the rollout timeline includes:
- Markers when the experiment starts/stops
- Markers for detected likely regressions (advisory), e.g., “Refusal rate regression (CI excludes 0)”


### 7.3 Quality gate configuration (v1)
UI is present but clearly labeled “Advisory (no automated rollback in v1)”.

Gate configuration fields:
- Metric
- Threshold type: absolute delta / relative % change
- Direction: regression only / improvement only / both

When gate triggers:
- Add warning banner on rollout page
